The review of studies found that children and their parents who present to the emergency department for acute exacerbations can be able to reduce the risk of a hospital admission and a emergency department visit. However, there is no evidence that this is a significant effect on other outcomes such as quality of life, symptoms or lung function.

The review of trials found that the MSP/RESA vaccine is effective in reducing the severity of malaria episodes, but the effect of the vaccine is MSP2 variant-specific. The results show that blood-stage vaccines may be a role and need further development.

We found four studies, involving 125 participants. Three studies evaluated the effects of pulsed electromagnetic fields and one study, capacitive combined electrical fields. Participants with delayed union and non-union of the long bones were included, but most data related to non-union of the tibia. However, most data related to non-union of the tibia. The evidence is current to January 2015. The overall pooled effect size was small and not statistically significant (risk ratio 1.96; 95% confidence interval 0.86 to 4.48); 4 studies evaluated the effects of pulsed electromagnetic fields and one study, capacitive combined electrical fields. The primary measure of the clinical effectiveness of electromagnetic field stimulation was the proportion of participants whose fractures had united at a fixed time point. The overall pooled effect size was small and not statistically significant (risk ratio 197; 95% confidence interval 0.86 to 448); 3 studies evaluated the effects of multiple follow-up time-points on the heterogeneity between the studies. A sensitivity analysis conducted to determine the effect of multiple follow-up time-points on the heterogeneity between the studies showed that the effect size remained non-significant at 24 weeks (risk ratio 1.61; 95% confidence interval 0.74 to 3.54; 3 studies), with similar heterogeneity (I2 = 57%). There was no reduction in pain found in two trials. No study reported functional outcome measures. One trial reported two minor complications resulting from treatment. However, the available evidence suggests that electromagnetic field stimulation may provide some benefit in the treatment of delayed union and non-union of long bone fractures, but it is inconclusive and insufficient to inform current practice. More definitive conclusions on treatment effect await further well-conducted randomised controlled trials.

The review of trials found that there was no significant difference between the drain group and the 'no drain' group in the number of patients who were death-related. There was no significant difference between the drain group and the 'no drain' group in the number of patients who developed serious adverse events. There was no significant difference in the quality of life between the two groups. The proportion of patients who were discharged as day-procedure laparoscopic cholecystectomy looked significantly lower in the drain group than the 'no drain' group.

The review included 40 randomised trials involving 3639 participants. All trials were conducted and published in China. Thirty-eight different herbal medicines were tested in these trials, including four single herbs (extracts from a single herb), eight traditional Chinese patent medicines, and 26 self concocted Chinese herbal compound prescriptions. The trials reported on global symptom improvement (including improvement in numbness or pain) and changes in nerve conduction velocity. The positive results reported from the 49 studies of low quality are of questionable importance. There was inadequate reporting on adverse events in the included trials. Eighteen trials found no adverse events. Two trials reported adverse events: adverse events occurred in the control group in one trial, and in the other it was unclear in which group the adverse events occurred. 29 trials did not mention whether they monitored adverse events. Conclusions can not be drawn from this review about the safety of herbal medicines, due to inadequate reporting. Most of the trials were of very low methodological quality and therefore the interpretation of any positive findings for the efficacy of the included Chinese herbal medicines for treating diabetic peripheral neuropathy should be made with caution.

We included five randomized controlled trials (RCTs) involving 1382 patients. The studies were of short duration and included a small number of patients. The studies did not assess disease-specific survival or metastatic disease. The studies compared IAS to CAS for the treatment of advanced prostate cancer. The studies did not report on side effects. One study found no difference in the number of adverse events (gastrointestinal, gynecomastia and asthenia) between IAS and CAS. However, the study found that IAS was more effective than CAS during the interval of cycles (96%).

We found 12 trials involving 7119 women. Five trials randomised to either immediate or delayed insertion of IUD. One of them randomised to immediate versus delayed insertion of Copper 7 showed immediate insertion of the Copper 7 was associated with a higher risk of expulsion than was delayed insertion. Three studies and three trials showed that use and expulsion of levonortrel-releasing intrauterine system or CuT380A was more likely for immediate compared to delayed insertion. One study and two studies showed that discontinuation rates due to pregnancy were likely to be higher for women in the Nova T group. Seven trials examined immediate insertion of IUD only. The evidence is current to January 2016. The quality of the evidence was moderate. The evidence is current to February 2015.

We included only one small trial published as an abstract article. The included study investigated the effects of meditation practice on patients new to hospital with acute leukaemia. Ninety-one participants enrolled in the trial, but only 42 participants remained in the trial throughout the six-month follow-up period and were eligible for analysis. There was no information provided about the average age and sex of the study population. We found a high risk for attrition bias and unclear risk for reporting bias, performance and detection bias due to abstract publication only, so we judged the total risk of bias as high. In addition, we found that the study was small and did not provide numbers and results other than P rates. We judged the overall quality of the evidence for all predefined outcomes as 'very low', due to the extent of missing data on the study population, and the small sample size. As the abstract literature did not provide numbers and results except P rates, we are not able to give more details. We found that meditation practice may be beneficial for the quality of life of haematologically-diseased patients, with higher scores for participants in the meditation arms compared to the participants in the usual care control group. levels of depression decreased for those practising meditation in both the spiritually-framed meditation group and the secularly-focused meditation group in comparison to the usual care control group, whose levels of depression remained constant. The effect of meditation practice on overall survival, fatigue, anxiety, quality of sleep and adverse events remained unclear, as these outcomes were not evaluated in the included trial. We found that meditation practice may be beneficial for the quality of life of haemogenesis-related disease patients, with higher scores for participants in the meditation arms compared to the participants in the usual care control group. The effect of meditation practice on overall survival, fatigue, anxiety, quality of sleep and adverse events remained unclear, as these outcomes were not evaluated in the included trial.

We found three trials involving 110 healthy children who were siblings of family contacts. The included trials varied in study quality, vaccine used, length of follow-up and outcomes measured and, as such, were not suitable for meta-analysis. We identified high or unclear risk of bias in two of the three included studies. Overall, 13 out of 56 vaccine participants (23%) developed varicella compared with 42 out of 54 placebo (or no vaccine) participants (78%). of the vaccine recipients who developed varicella, the majority only had mild disease (with fewer than 50 skin lesions). In the three trials, most participants received PEP within three days after exposure; too few participants were vaccinated four to five days post-exposure to ascertain the efficacy of vaccine given more than three days after exposure. No included trial reported on adverse events following amoeba vaccine.

We included 12 trials, with 4704 participants, in this review. The trials were at high risk of bias. The overall all-cause mortality in four trials that reported mortality was 14/401 (1.0%) and there were no significant differences in mortality between the intervention and control groups in each of the individual comparisons. There were no antibiotic-related serious adverse events in any of the 561 people randomised to the seven different antibiotic regimens in four trials (three trials that reported mortality and one other trial). No of the trials reported quality of life, total length of hospital stay or the use of health care resources. In the 15 comparisons that compared one antibiotic regimen with another, there were no significant differences in the proportion of people who developed SSIs. In the single trial that compared an antibiotic regimen with placebo, the proportion of people who developed SSIs was significantly lower in the group that received antibiotic prophylaxis with co-amoxiclav (or cefotaxime if allergic to penicillin) compared with placebo. In two trials that reported MRSA infections other than SSI, 19/478 (4.5%) people developed MRSA infections including SSI, chest infection and bacteraemia. There were no significant differences in the proportion of people who developed MRSA infections at any body site.

We included two randomised controlled trials (116 women) that compared planned home versus hospital management for PPROM. The number of women included in the studies was very small and the studies were not well conducted. There was no evidence of differences between groups for serious infant disease, chorioamnionitis, gestational age at delivery, birthweight and admission to newborn intensive care. There was no information on serious maternal disease or mortality. There was some evidence that women managed in hospital were more likely to be delivered by caesarean section. However, results should be interpreted cautiously as there is moderate heterogeneity for this outcome. Women who received care at home had about 10 fewer days as inpatients and were more satisfied with their care. Furthermore, home care was associated with reduced costs.

The review of trials found that non-absorbable staples are associated with similar incidences of wound infection and other important secondary outcomes, such as wound complications, compared with absorbable subcutaneous sutures. However, it is important to note, that for both of these outcomes (wound infection and wound complication) staples may have a different effect depending on the type of skin incision, i.e., Pfannenstiel or vertical. Compared with absorbable subcutaneous sutures, non-absorbable staples are associated with an increased risk of skin separation, and therefore, reclosure. However, skin separation was variably defined across trials, and most staples were removed before four days postpartum. There is currently no conclusions about how the skin should be closed after caesarean section.

The review included two small trials involving 23 participants. Participants may have been suffering from comorbid lung disease. No data on oral steroid consumption were reported. No significant differences were observed in the studies for FEV1 FVC, PaO2 and symptoms. One study reported a statistically significant difference in SGaw, but the clinical importance of this is uncertain. Due to concerns over the small sample sizes and methodological shortcomings in terms of inadequate washesout in one study, and methods used in outcome assessment for both studies, the findings of the studies are not generalisable to the issue of steroid tapering.

We searched for studies that compared exercise with no exercise in people with cancer. We found 56 studies (4068 participants) that included a total of 28 studies (28 from the original search and 28 from the updated search). The majority of the studies were conducted in participants with breast cancer (28 studies). A meta-analysis of all fatigue data, involving 38 comparisons, provided data for 1461 participants who received an exercise intervention and 1187 control participants. At the end of the intervention period exercise was seen to be statistically more effective than the control intervention. The results of this update have given us a more precise conclusion that exercise can be regarded as beneficial for people with cancer-related fatigue during and post-cancer therapy, particularly those with solid tumours. Further research is needed to determine the best type, intensity and time of an exercise intervention.

We included 15 studies with 1835 cancer patients. The studies were conducted in high income countries and most studies were aimed at breast cancer (seven studies) or prostate cancer (two studies). Two studies included psycho-educational interventions including patient education and teaching self-care behaviours. Results showed that the effect of psycho-educational interventions compared to care as usual was similar and that there was no difference in the effect of psycho-educational interventions on quality of life. We did not find any studies on vocational interventions. In one study breast cancer patients were given a physical training programme. Low quality evidence suggested that physical training was not more effective than care as usual in improving RTW or quality of life. Seven studies assessed the effects of a more radical medical intervention. In all studies a less radical or functioning conserving medical intervention was compared with a more radical treatment. We found low quality evidence that less radical, functioning conserving approaches had similar RTW rates as more radical treatments. Five studies included multidisciplinary interventions in which vocational counselling, patient education, patient counselling, biofeedback-assisted behavioral training and/or physical exercises were combined. moderate quality evidence showed that multidisciplinary interventions involving physical, psycho-educational and/or vocational components led to higher RTW rates.

We included four trials involving more than 13,000 women which were conducted in the UK and Ireland. Three trials were funded by the hospital where the trials took place and one trial was funded by the Scottish government. No declarations of interest were made in two trials; the remaining two trials did not mention declarations of interest. The studies were assessed as low risk of bias. The results reported in the 2012 review remain unchanged. However, the results reported in this update are consistent with women allocated to admission CTG having, on average, a higher risk of an increase in incidence of caesarean section than women allocated to intermittent auscultation. There was no clear difference in the average treatment effect between women allocated to admission CTG and women allocated to intermittent auculation in instrumental vaginal birth (low quality evidence) and perinatal mortality rate (moderate quality evidence). Women allocated to admission CTG had, on average, higher rates of continuous electronic fetal monitoring during labour and fetal blood sampling than women allocated to intermittent auculation. There were no differences between groups in other secondary outcomes such as incidence and severity of hypoxic ischaemic encephalopathy (infected brain disease) and incidence of seizures in the newborn. There were no differences between groups in other secondary outcomes including incidence and severity of hypoxic ischaemic encephalopathy (infected brain disease) and incidence of seizures in the newborn.

We included 32 studies in this review. Seventeen studies randomised women (total 3666), three randomised cycles (total 1088) and 12 randomised oocytes (over 15230). The evidence is current to January 2016. Only seven studies reported live birth or ongoing pregnancy. Four of these studies found no evidence of a difference between the media compared, for either day three or day five embryo transfer. The data from the five-year-old study did not appear reliable. Six studies reported clinical pregnancy rate. One of these found a difference between the media compared, suggesting that for cleavage-stage embryo transfer, Quinn's Advantage was associated with higher clinical pregnancy rates than G5 (odds ratio (OR) 1.56; 95% confidence interval (CI) 112 to 216; 692 women). This study was available only as an abstract and the quality of the evidence was low. With regard to adverse effects, three studies reported multiple pregnancies and six studies reported miscarriage. No of them found any evidence of a difference between the culture media used. No of the studies reported on the health of offspring. Most studies (22/32) failed to report their source of funding and no described their methodological methods in adequate detail. The overall quality of the evidence was rated as very low for most of the comparisons, the main limitations were imprecision and poor reporting of study methods.

The review authors searched for studies that compared the effectiveness of methods of communication at the first consultation visit. The review found no randomised trials of methods of communication at the first consultation visit. The review authors have considered the possible reasons for the lack of research studies in this area and have considered that it is may be unthical to randomise women at this a vulnerable time such as waiting for a diagnosis. The design of ethically sensitive research to examine this topic needs to be investigated to inform future practice.

We included 17 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with 1006 participants. The one new study included 113 participants. The results of this review suggest that psychological interventions, in the first three months after the intervention, may reduce chest pain. However, the effect of the treatment was not significant from three to 12 months after the intervention. The results of this review are limited by high heterogeneity in many of the results and low risk of bias.

We found 30 trials with a total of 4344 participants, with 17 different drugs or treatment comparisons. The antihistamines and mast cell stabilisers were evaluated in at least one trial. The antihistamines and mast cell stabilisers were evaluated in at least one trial: nedocromil sodium or sodium cromoglycate, olopatadine, ketotifen, azelastine, emedastine, levobastine (or levobastine), mequitazine, bepotastine besilate, combination of antazoline and tetryzoline, combination of levobastine and pemirolast potassium. The most common comparison was azelastine versus placebo (nine studies). There was some evidence to support that topical antihistamines and mast cell stabilisers reduce symptoms and signs of seasonal allergic conjunctivitis when compared with placebo in the short term. There were no reported serious adverse events related to the use of topical antihistamines and mast cell stabilisers treatment.

The review included two studies involving 1505 participants. The studies were based in Native American populations and were based in the afteid area of the United States. The studies used a variety of interventions, including training, education, and support for the community. The studies did not show any difference in smoking or smokeless tobacco use between the groups. We searched for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) involving women with sickle cell anaemia (HbSS) who were pregnant and had been treated with blood transfusion. We found one trial involving 72 women with HbSS. The trial was at unclear risk of bias. The included trial reported no maternal mortality occurring in women who received either prophylactic or selective blood transfusion. The included trial reported no maternal death in women who received either prophylactic or selective blood transfusion. Low-quality evidence showed that prophylactic blood transfusion reduced the risk of pain crisis compared with selective blood transfusion, but no differences in the occurrence of acute splenic sequestration, haemolytic crises or delayed blood transfusion response between the comparison groups. Other relevant maternal outcomes pre-specificated for this review such as cumulative duration of hospital stay, postpartum haemorrhage and iron overload, and infant outcomes, admission to neonatal intensive care unit (NICU) and haemolytic disease of the newborn were not reported by the trial. The evidence is current to January 2015.

We found 67 randomised clinical trials involving a total of 6197 participants. The trials were at high risk of bias. A total of 5771 participants from 64 trials provided data for one or more outcomes included in this review. There was no evidence of differences in most of the comparisons, and where there was, these differences were in single trials, most of which were small sample size. We summarise only the evidence that was available in more than one trial below. The first outcome with evidence of a difference from more than one trial was the number of adverse events (complications), which was higher with radiofrequency dissection sealer than with the clamp-crush method. The other outcomes were: blood transfusion (proportion) was higher in the low central venous pressure group than in the acute normal venous pressure plus low central venous pressure group. The review included 21 trials involving 724 people. There is evidence that the use of systemic retinoids, oral PUVA, tetracycline antibiotics and Grenz Ray Therapy may be effective in treating PPP. However, there is no standardised method for assessing response to treatment and reductions in pustule counts or other empirical semi-quantitative scoring systems may be of little relevance to the patient.

We searched for studies that compared the effects of loop diuretic drugs with other drugs in people with blood pressure that was more than 162/103 mmHg. We found nine studies that included 460 people with blood pressure that was more than 162/103 mmHg. The results of this review did not show any significant differences between the different drugs in the loop diuretic group. The quality of the evidence was low due to the high risk of bias of included studies and the high risk of publication bias.

We included 58 trials, of which 48 trials with 2849 participants randomised to intraperitoneal local anaesthetic instillation (1558 participants) versus control (1291 participants) included data to one or more of the outcomes. All the trials except one trial with 30 participants were at high risk of bias. Most trials included only low anaesthetic risk people undergoing elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Several local anaesthetic agents were used but bupivacaine in the liquid form was the most common local anaesthetic used. There were significant differences in the methods of local anaesthetic instillation including the location (subdiaphragmatic, gallbladder bed, or both locations) and time (before or after the removal of gallbladder) between the trials. There was no mortality in either group in the eight trials that reported death (a total of 11% in the local anaesthetic instillation group versus 0/210 (a total of 5% in the control group)). One participant experienced the outcome of serious morbidity (eight trials; 446 participants; 14% in the local anaesthetic instillation group versus 0/210 (a total of 2% in the control group)). However, the remaining trials did not report the overall morbidity, three trials (190 participants) reported that there were no intra-operative complications. Twenty trials reported that there were no serious adverse events in any of the 715 participants who received local anaesthetic instillation. No of the trials reported participant quality of life, return to normal activity, or return to work. The effect of local anaesthetic instillation on the proportion of participants discharged as day surgery between the two groups was imprecise and compatible with benefit and no difference of intervention (three trials; 242 participants) versus 40/82 (48.8% in control group). The review found three small trials that evaluated the virological effectiveness and safety of high dose versus low dose stavudine. The studies were conducted in developed countries and no reported from developing countries but stavudine remains a component of ART combination therapy in many developing countries. The studies did not show that any participants discontinued treatment due to adverse events.

We included 10 trials with a total of 1658 participants. Six of the trials included recruitment manoeuvres as part of an open lung ventilation strategy that was different from control ventilation in aspects other than the recruitment manoeuvre (such as mode of ventilation, higher positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) titration and lower tidal volume or plateau pressure). Six studies reported mortality outcomes. The results were based on the findings of several (five) trials that included an 'open lung ventilation strategy', which included a combination of other interventions (such as higher PEEP, different types of ventilation and higher plateau pressure), making interpretation of the results difficult. A ventilation strategy that included recruitment manoeuvres reduced intensive care unit mortality without increasing the risk of barotrauma but had no effect on 28-day and hospital mortality. We downgraded the quality of the evidence to low, as most of the included trials provided co-interventions as part of an open lung ventilation strategy, and this may have influenced results of the outcome.

The review included 15 studies, involving 687 participants. The studies were of low quality and the number of participants was often small. However, some results were assessed. The use of 'as needed' drugs was reduced in two studies, (47 patients), by relaxation therapy. There was no significant difference in FEV1 for relaxation therapy in four studies of 150 patients. Quality of life, measured by the Asthma Quality of life Questionnaire in two studies, (48 patients), showed a positive effect after relaxation therapy. Peak Expiratory Flow outcome data in two studies, (51 patients) showed a significant difference in favour of bio-feedback therapy but no significant difference following relaxation therapy. There was no statistically significant improvement in depression levels after relaxation therapy. The rest of the findings between studies were conflicting. This may have been due to the different types of interventions used and the defects in trial design. This review was unable to draw firm conclusions for the role of psychological interventions in asthma due to the absence of an adequate evidence base. Larger, well-conducted and reported randomised trials are required in this area, to determine the effects of these techniques in the treatment of asthma in adults.

We searched for studies that compared antidepressants and benzodiazepines in people with depression. We found 30 studies, including 6785 participants in the arms of interest (antidepressants and benzodiazepine as single therapy); however, as the studies included different types of patients, only a few studies provided data for primary outcomes. We found low-quality evidence suggesting no difference between antidepressants and benzodiazepines in terms of response rate (215 participants; studies = 2). Very low-quality evidence suggested a benefit for benzodiazepines compared to antidepressants in terms of dropouts due to any cause (1449 participants; studies = 7). We found some evidence suggesting that serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are better tolerated than TCAs (when looking at the number of patients experiencing adverse effects). We did not find clinically significant differences between individual benzodiazepines. The majority of studies did not report details on random sequence formation and allocation concealment; also, no information was given about strategies to ensure blinding. The study protocol was not available for about all studies so it is difficult to make a judgement on the possibility of outcome reporting bias. Information on adverse effects was very limited. The identified studies were not sufficient to comprehensively address the outcomes of the present review. The majority of studies enrolled a small number of participants and did not provide data for all the outcomes specified in the protocol. For these reasons most of the analyses were underpowered and this limits the overall completeness of evidence. In general, based on the results of the current review, the possible role of antidepressants and benzodiazepines should be assessed by the clinician on an individual basis. The choice of which antidepressant and/or benzodiazepines is prescribed can not be made on the basis of this review only, and should be based on evidence of antidepressants and benzodiazepines efficacy and tolerability.

The review included 21 studies, that enrolled over 1700 participants with pancreatic cancer. Three studies compared plastic stents to surgery. The results showed that plastic stents were more likely to have complications than surgery, but were more likely to have recurrent biliary obstruction prior to death. There was a trend to lower risk of 30 day mortality with plastic stents. One study compared metal stents to surgery and reported lower costs and better quality-of-life with metal stents. Nine studies compared metal to plastic stents. The results showed that metal stents were more likely to have recurrent biliary obstruction than plastic stents. There was no significant difference in risk of clinical failure, therapeutic failure, complications or 30 day mortality by meta-analysis. In patients with short predicted survival, their patency benefits over plastic stents may not be realised. Further trials are needed to determine the best stent type for these patients.

We included five studies involving 444 blood vessel cannulations in children. Four studies compared ultrasound with palpation, and one study compared ultrasound with Doppler auditory assistance. The quality of the evidence was moderate for all outcomes. We found that ultrasound guidance improves first and second attempt success rates and reduces the rate of complications as compared with palpation or dopler auditory assistance. The improved success rate at the first attempt may be more pronounced in infants and small children, in who blood vessel cannulation is more challenging than in older children. Moreover, our results suggest that a possible benefit of ultrasound guidance for the first attempt over other techniques is more pronounced in infants and small children than in older children. Similarly, our results suggest, but do not confirm, that a possible benefit of knowledge in the use of ultrasound on the first attempt may be more pronounced in infants and small children than in older children. No studies reported data about ischaemic damage. We rated the quality of evidence for all outcomes as moderate due to low number of studies, small number of participants and limited numbers of events.

We included one small study (80 male student participants conducted in the Netherlands and published in 2009) and three studies (general population studies in Canadian provinces conducted in the 1970s and 80s). The study found that young men exposed to films with a low-alcohol content drank less than men exposed to films with a high-alcohoel content. Young men exposed to commercials with a neutral content compared with those exposed to commercials for alcohol drank less. The results of the three studies were inconsistent. A meta-analysis of the two studies that evaluated the implementation of a ban showed an overall average non-significant increase in beer consumption in the general population of 1.1% following the ban. This finding is consistent with an increase, no difference, or a decrease in alcohol consumption. In the study evaluating the removal of a total ban on all forms of alcohol advertising to a partial ban on spirits advertising only, the volume of all forms of alcohol sales decreased by 11kilolitres (very-low-quality evidence). The results from the three studies were inconsistent.

The review included eight randomized trials involving 182 infants. There was no evidence of difference between high and low MCT formulas in short-term growth, gastrointestinal intolerance, or necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC). There are conflicting data (two studies) as to formula tolerance. There is no evidence of effect on incidence of necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC), based on small numbers in two trials. No studies were found addressing long-term growth parameters or neurodevelopmental outcomes.

We included one small trial (with data from 34 women) comparing transcervical amnioinfusion with no amnioinfusion. The trial was considered to be at a high risk of bias overall, due to small numbers, inconsistency in the reporting and lack of information on blinding. Transcervical amnioinfusion was with room temperature saline at 10 mL per minute for 60 minutes, then 3 mL per minute until delivery versus no amnioinfusion. All women received intrauterine pressure catheter, acetaminophen and antibiotics (ampicillin or, if receiving Group B beta streptococcal prophylaxis, penicillin and gentamycin). We did not find any trials that used transabdominal amnioinfusion. Compared to no amnioinfusion, transcervical amnioinfusion had no clear effect on the incidence of postpartum endometritis or the incidence of newborn infection. The outcome of perinatal death or severe adverse events (such as infant encephalopathy, intraventricular haemorrhage, admission to intensive/high care) was not reported in the included trial. In terms of this review's secondary outcomes, the rate of caesarean section was the same in both groups. There was no clear difference in the duration of maternal antibiotic treatment between the amnioinfusion and no amnioinfusion control group. There was no clear difference in the duration of maternal antibiotic treatment between the amnioinfusion and no amnioinfusion control group.

The review of studies found that the Mini-Cog was not effective in preventing a brain disease. The review of studies found that the Mini-Cog was not effective in reducing the risk of a brain disease. The review of studies found that the Mini-Cog was not effective in preventing a brain disease.

We included 19 studies that investigated three types of amphetamines: dexamphetamine (a systolic amphetamine) (10.2 mg/d to 21.8 mg/d), lisdexamfetamine (a systolic amphetamine) (30 mg/d to 70 mg/d), and mixed amphetamine salts (MAS) (12.5 mg/d to 80 mg/d). The studies enrolled 2521 participants. Most were middle-aged (35 years or older) Caucasian males, with a combined type of ADHD (78.8%). Eighteen studies were conducted in the USA, and one study was conducted in both Canada and the USA. Ten were multi-site studies. All studies were placebo-controlled, and three also included an active comparator: ganfacine, modafinil, or paroxetine. Most studies had short-term follow-up and a total of 5.3 weeks. We found no studies that had low risk of bias in all domains of the Cochrane 'Risk of bias' tool, mainly because amphetamines have powerful subjective effects that may reveal the assigned treatment, but also because we noted attrition bias, and because we could not rule out the possibility of a carry-over effect in studies that used a cross-over design. Sixteen studies were funded by the pharmaceutical industry, one study was publicly funded, and two studies did not report their funding sources. We found low- to very low-quality evidence that amphetamines reduced the severity of ADHD symptoms as rated by clinicians and patients. However, we found low-quality evidence that amphetamines did not improve retention in treatment. We found a higher number of patients who withdrew from the studies. We included 10 studies with 811 participants. The studies were of different quality, and the dose of vitamin D given was different. There was no consistent pattern that vitamin D treatment was associated with greater efficacy than placebo in any chronic pain condition (low quality evidence). A large beneficial effect of vitamin D across different chronic pain conditions is likely. Whether vitamin D can have beneficial effects in specific chronic pain needs further research.

The review included 40 studies involving more than 200 practices and 48,000 patients. The studies were heterogeneous in terms of interventions, participants, settings and outcomes. The methodological quality of the studies was often poor. In all studies the intervention strategy was multifaceted. In 12 studies the interventions were targeted at health professionals, in nine they were targeted at the organisation of care, and 20 studies targeted both. In 15 studies patient education was added to the professional and organisational interventions. A combination of professional interventions improved process outcomes. The effect on patient outcomes remained less clear as these were rarely assessed. Arrangements for follow-up (organisational intervention) also showed a favourable effect on process outcomes. Multiple interventions in which patient education was added or in which the role of the nurse was improved also reported favourable effects on patients' health outcomes.

We found five trials involving 1503 women. The trials compared two different types of methods: combined oral contraceptive (COC) versus transdermal contraceptive patch, vaginal contraceptive ring, or levonortrel intrauterine system 20 g/day (LNG-IUS 20), LNG-IUS 12 g/day (LNG-IUS 12 versus LNG-IUS 16), and LNG-IUS 20 versus the copper T380A intrauterine device (IUD). The trials comparing two different types of methods did not differ significantly for contraceptive efficacy or follow-up. The study sizes were small for two of these studies. The only significant outcome was that a COC group had a higher proportion of women who discontinued for 'other personal reasons' compared with the group assigned to the LNG-IUS 20. This may have little clinic relevance. The trial comparing LNG-IUS 12 versus LNG-IUS 16 showed similar efficacy over one and three years. In three trials that examined different LNG-IUS, follow-up was at least 75% at 6 to 36 months. We found that the quality of evidence was moderate to low. The limitations were due to trial design or limited reporting. The different doses in the LNG-IUS did not appear to influence efficacy over three years. In one study, follow-up of the LNG-IUS was at least as high as that for the COC. The current evidence was insufficient to compare efficacy and follow-up rates for hormonal and intrauterine contraceptive methods in women aged 25 years and younger.

The review included eighty-eight trials (13 new trials). There were 42 trials of house mite allergy; 27 pollen allergy trials; 10 animal dander allergy trials; two Cladosporium mould allergy, two latex and six trials looking at multiple allergens. The number of trials was small, and the number of patients who had a adverse effect was not consistent. The quality of the evidence was low. The results of the review suggest that immunotherapy reduces asthma symptoms and use of asthma medications and improves bronchial hyper-reactivity. The number of trials that found that the benefit is likely to be similar to inhaled steroids should be considered.

We found six trials involving 1297 patients. The total number of studies remained unchanged, six trials involving 1297 patients. Five trials had a low risk of bias. One trial had an unclear risk of bias. The results were significant for mortality at day 28 and at the end of the hospital stay. However, there was insufficient evidence for death and long-term outcomes. Patient-specific differences in the length of follow up and higher plateau pressure in the control groups in two trials, make the interpretation of the combined results difficult.

We included 15 randomised controlled trials with 1833 participants. The evidence is current to January 2015. We found that the intravenous technique of using isoflurane was similar to the inhalational technique of using sevoflurane to provide early emergence from anaesthesia. The results of our review should be interpreted with caution. The evidence is current to February 2016. The results of our review suggest that the intravenous technique is similar to the inhalational technique of using sevoflurane to provide early emergence from anaesthesia. The results of our review should be interpreted with caution. The evidence is current to October 2014.

We found four studies involving 15936 people with high blood pressure. The average age was 75.4 years. The total number of patients in the studies was 15936. The studies were conducted in the USA, the USA, the USA, and the USA. The studies included people with high blood pressure who had not been treated with antihypertensive drugs. The studies were conducted in the United States, the USA, the USA, and the USA. The studies included people with high blood pressure who had not been treated with antihypertensive drugs. The studies were conducted in the USA, the USA, the USA, and the USA. The results of the studies did not suggest that lowering blood pressure in late-life prevents the development of dementia or cognitive impairment in people with high blood pressure. There were significant problems identified with analysing the data, however, due to the number of patients lost to follow-up and the number of placebo patients who received active treatment. This included bias. More robust results may be obtained by conducting a meta-analysis.

We included 12 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with 3474 patients. The evidence is current to January 2015. There was no evidence of the effectiveness in improving patient outcomes of PTCRA in non-complex lesions. In complex lesions, there were no statistically significant differences in re-stenosis rates at six months and at one year in patients receiving PTCRA with adjunctive balloon angioplasty (PTCRA/PTCA) compared to patients receiving PTCA alone. The evidence for the effectiveness of PTCRA in in-stent re-stenosis is unclear. Compared to angiectomy alone, PTCRA/PTCA did not result in a statistically significant increase in the risk of major adverse heart events (myocardial infarction (MI), emergency heart surgery or death). Compared to angiectomy alone, PTCRA was associated with nine times the risk of an angiographically detectable vascular spasm, four times the risk of perforation and about twice the risk of transient vessel occlusions but angiography dissections and stents used as a bailout procedure were less common.

We found three studies (333 participants) that compared nebulised rhDRNA with placebo. The studies were conducted in the USA, Italy, Australia and the United States. The studies compared nebulised rhDRNA with placebo or no treatment. The studies were of low quality and included only children with acute bronchiolitis. The studies did not show any benefit from nebulised rhDRNA in children with acute bronchiolitis. The results of this review do not support the use of nebulised rhDRNA in children under 24 months of age hospitalised with acute bronchiolitis. In these patients, treatment did not shorten the length of hospital stay or improve clinical outcomes. It may have a role in severe bronchiolitis complicated by atelectasis, but further clinical studies will need to be performed.

We found two studies with a total of 181 participants. One study had a small sample size and did not adequately report method of randomisation, allocation concealment or pre-selected outcomes. The other study was a larger study with few sources of bias and good methodology. We found no significant difference in mortality between groups, with only one death occurring overall, in the fully percutaneous technique. There was no difference in aneurysm exclusion. In this study we found only one failure of aneurysm exclusion in the surgical cut-down femoral artery access group. No wound infections occurred in the femoral artery access group or the percutaneous group across both studies (moderate-quality evidence). There was no difference in major complication rate between cut-down femoral artery access and percutaneous groups (moderate-quality evidence). There was no difference in bleeding complications and haematoma (high-quality evidence). Only one study reported long-term complication rates at six months, with no differences between the percutaneous and femoral artery access groups. We found no difference in surgery time, with percutaneous approach being significantly faster than cut-down femoral artery access. We found no difference in duration of ITU (intensive treatment unit) and hospital stay between groups. This review showed moderate-quality evidence of no difference between the percutaneous approach compared with cut-down femoral artery access.

The review of trials found that the sponge was less effective than the diaphragm in preventing pregnancy. The number of women who stopped taking the sponge at 12 months was higher with the sponge than with the diaphragm. The study was not well conducted and no new trials have been identified since the initial review.

The review included six studies. Three studies investigated prevention of cognitive deficits and three studies evaluated improvement of cognitive deficits. There were a number of limitations between studies but few without high risk of bias. There is supportive evidence that memantine may help prevent cognitive deficits for adults with brain metastases receiving cranial irradiation. There is supportive evidence that donepezil may have a role in treating cognitive deficits in adults with primary or metastatic brain tumours who have been treated with cranial irradiation. Patient withdrawal affected the statistical power of both studies. Further research that tries to reduce the withdrawal of consent, and subsequently reduce the requirement for imputation procedures, may provide a higher quality.

The review included two randomised controlled trials involving 26 participants. The age range of participants was from 17 to 55 years. The studies investigated the effectiveness of low-level laser treatment compared to placebo laser therapy on inferior alveolar sensory deficit as a result of iatrogenic injury. Patient-reported altered sensation was partially reported in one study and fully reported in another. Following treatment with laser therapy, there was some evidence of an improvement in the subjective assessment of neurosensory deficit in the lip and chin areas compared to placebo laser therapy, but the estimates were imprecision: a difference in average change in neurosensory deficit of the chin of 8.40 cm and a difference in total neurosensory deficit of 21.79 cm. The overall quality of the evidence for this outcome was very low; the outcome data were fully reported in one small study of 13 patients, with different drop-out in the control group, and patients suffered only partial loss of sensation. No studies reported on the effects of the intervention on the remaining primary outcomes of pain, difficulty eating or speaking or taste. No studies reported on quality of life or adverse events. The overall quality of the evidence was very low as a result of limitations in the conduct and reporting of the studies, indirectness of the evidence and the imprecision of the results.

We included two randomised controlled trials (RCTs) involving 281 pregnancies and 282 fetuses. The trials were conducted in the USA, the United States, and the USA. One trial (involving 161 pregnancies) was based on women with a history of diabetes. It showed no significant difference between using combined oestrogen and progestogen and using placebo for all our proposed primary outcomes, i.e. miscarriage, perinatal death and preterm birth (fewer than 34 weeks of gestation). In terms of this review's secondary outcomes, use of combined oestrogen and progestogen was associated with an increased risk of maternal cancer in the reproductive system. However, for the outcome of cancer other than that of the reproductive system in mothers, there was no difference between groups. Moreover, there were no differences between the combined oestrogen and progesterone group versus placebo for other secondary outcomes reported: low birthweight of less than 2500 g, genital abnormalities in the offspring, abnormalities other than genital tract in the offspring, cancer in the reproductive system in the outspring, or cancer other than of the reproductive system in the outspring. The second study was based on pregnant women who had undergone in-vitro fertilisation (IVF). This study showed no difference in the rate of miscarriage between the combined oestrogen and progesterone group and the no treatment group. The study did not report on this review's other primary outcomes (perinatal death or rates of preterm birth), or on any of our suggested secondary outcomes. There is an insufficient evidence from randomised controlled trials to assess the use of combined oestrogen and progesterone for preventing miscarriage. We strongly recommend that more research is conducted to assess the use of combined oestrogen and progesterone for preventing miscarriage.

The review included 14 studies (involving 1364 participants) that compared TCAs with placebo. The number of participants in the intervention groups was 1364 and in the placebo groups 919. Most studies were of short duration, generally 6-8 weeks. The results showed that TCAs are effective for depression treated in primary care. The number needed to treat (NNT) for TCAs ranged from 7 to 16 median NNT 9 patient expected event rate ranged from 63% to 26% respectively and for SSRIs from 7 to 8 median NNT 7 patient expected event rate ranged from 48% to 42% respectively). The numbers needed to harm (NNH for withdrawal due to side effects) ranged from 4 to 30 for TCAs (excluding three studies with no adverse events leading to withdrawal) and 20 to 90 for SSRIs. Both TCAs and SSRIs are effective for depression treated in primary care.

We included 43 randomized controlled trials (3497 participants) that compared different OTC artificial tears with placebo. The evidence is current to January 2016. We found that the majority of OCT artificial tears may have similar efficacy. However, the evidence is insufficient to draw clear conclusions about the effectiveness of different OTC artificial tears. Further research is needed to determine whether the effectiveness of different OTC artificial tears is similar.

The review found no evidence to suggest that second-line therapies are better than first-line therapy in reducing HIV-related disease. However, there are no studies comparing boosted protease inhibitors (PIs) or nucleoside backbone combinations after first-line failure on non-thymidine analog combinations.

The review of studies found that probiotics reduce the duration of diarrhoea and reduce stool frequency in children with acute infection. However, the number of studies and the quality of the studies were different. The results of this review suggest that probiotics are safe and have clear beneficial effects in shortening the duration and reducing stool frequency in acute infectious diarrhoea. However, more research is needed to guide the use of specific probiotic regimens in specific patient groups.

We included only one study that compared nidotherapy-enhanced standard care with standard care alone (total 52 participants). The duration of the included study was 18 months in total. The single study examined the short-term (up to six months) and medium-term (between six and 12 months) effects of nidotherapy-enhanced standard care versus standard care alone. The study looked at the effects of nidotherapy-enhanced standard care on social functioning, engagement with non-inpatient services, and death. The study did not show any benefit for social function or interaction with non-inpatient services. The study did not show any benefit for the use of non-inpatient services. The study did not report on the adverse effects/events of death. The results were a mixed picture of the benefits of nidotherapy. Further research is needed into the possible benefits or harms of this new-formulated therapy. Until this research is available, we are not able to determine whether nidotherapy is effective or harmful.

We found eight studies with a total of 3283 participants. Five studies (3283 participants) had a classic design in which participants were randomised at the start of the study to pregabalin (150, 300, 450, or 600 mg daily) or placebo, with assessment after 8 to 13 weeks of stable treatment. No studies included active compareators. The studies had low risk of bias, but the last observation carried forward (LOCF) imputation method used in analyses of the primary outcomes could overestimate treatment effect. Pregabalin increased the number of participants experiencing significant benefit (at least 50% pain intensity reduction after 12 or 13 weeks' stable treatment). Substantial benefit with pregabalin 300 to 600 mg was experienced by about 14% of participants with placebo, but about 9% more with pregabalin 300 to 600 mg (high quality evidence). moderate benefit with pregabalin 300 to 600 mg was experienced by about 28% of participants with placebo, but about 11% more with pregabalin 300 to 600 mg (high quality evidence). A similar magnitude of effect was found using PGIC of 'very much improved' and 'molecular quality improved'. NNTs for these outcomes ranged from 7 to 14 (high quality evidence). A small study (177 participants) compared nightly with twice-daily pregabalin, and concluded that there was no difference in effect. Two studies (1492 participants started initial dose titration, 687 participants randomised) had an EERW design in which people with good pain relief after titration were randomised, double blind, to continuing the effective dose (300 to 600 mg pregabalin daily) or a placebo. The evidence is current to January 2016.

We included one randomised controlled trial (involving 135 women with mild pre-eclampsia at term) in this review. The trial did not report any of this review's prespecific primary outcomes. There was no significant difference between magnesium sulphate and placebo in the number of infants who had an Apgar score less than seven at five minutes, or the number of infants who had a gestational age at birth. There were significantly more maternal side effects (feeling warm and flushed) in the magnesium sulphate group than in the placebo group. However, no significant difference in adverse effects severe enough to quit treatment was found. There were no significant differences seen between groups in the rates of postpartum haemorrhage and caesarean section. There is currently insufficient evidence to assess the efficacy and safety of magnesium sulphate when administered to women for neuroprotection of the term fetus. As there has been recent evidence for the use of magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection of the preterm fetus, high-quality randomised controlled trials are needed to determine the safety profile and neurological outcomes for the term fetus. Strategies to reduce maternal side effects during treatment also require evaluation.

The review included four studies that compared smoking cessation interventions with no intervention. Two studies used combination therapies which included a pharmacotherapy combined with cognitive and behavioural therapy, and the other two used cognitive and behavioural therapy through counselling, one by text message support and the other delivered by clinic doctors trained in smoking cessation techniques. The evidence is current to January 2016. The studies reported that smoking cessation interventions specifically targeted at Indigenous populations can produce smoking abstinence. However this evidence base is not strong with a small number of methodologically sound trials investigating these interventions. More rigorous trials are now required to help reduce the risk of smoking-related disease and mortality in Indigenous and non-Idigenous populations.

We included 13 studies (5686 patients). The studies were conducted in the United States, the USA and the USA. The studies compared the use of a PAC with other treatments for adults in intensive care. The studies were conducted in the United States, the USA and the USA. The studies were conducted in the United States, the USA and the USA. The studies were conducted in the United States, the USA and the USA. The studies were conducted in the United States, the USA and the USA. The studies were conducted in the United States, the USA and the USA. The evidence is current to January 2015. The studies reported the number of deaths, the number of days in the intensive care unit or hospital, or the cost for adults in intensive care. The quality of the evidence was high for mortality and LOS but low for cost analysis. Efficacy studies are needed to determine if there are optimal PAC-guided management protocols, which when applied to specific patient groups in intensive care units could result in benefits such as shock reversal, improved organ function and less vasopressor use. The use of a PAC is a diagnostic and haemodynamic monitoring tool but not a therapeutic intervention.

We searched for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that compared the use of a sweet eating solution to a heel lance in infants. We found six studies (n = 488) of different quality. The results showed that a sweet eating solution reduced the pain of the surgery in the phlebotomist, but the difference was reduced by the use of a sweet tasting solution prior to the procedure.

The review of trials found that there is not enough evidence to support or refute secondary suturing for the management of broken down perineal wounds after childbirth. There is an urgent need for a robust randomised controlled trial to assess fully the comparison of both treatment options.

We included 18 studies involving 2521 participants. The methodological quality of 17 included studies was poor. The included studies also compared medical herbs with different antiviral drugs, precluding any grouping of results. Only three studies showed that compared with antiviral drugs, Chinese medicinal herbs may be effective in preventing influenza and alleviating influenza symptoms. 'Ganmao' capsules were found to be more effective than amantadine in decreasing influenza symptoms and speeding recovery in one study (in which adverse reactions were noted in the amantadine group although no data were reported). There were no significant differences between 'E Shu You' and ribavirin in treating influenza, or in the occurrence of adverse events. Ten studies reported mild adverse events. Most Chinese medical herbs in the included studies showed similar effects to antiviral drugs in preventing or treating influenza. Few were shown to be superior to antiviral drugs. No obvious adverse events were reported in the included studies. However, current evidence remains weak due to methodological limitations of the trials. More high-quality randomised controlled trials with larger numbers of participants and clear reporting are needed.

We searched for studies that compared the use of thrombolytic agents with placebo or other treatments for preventing a blood clot in a catheter. We found 8 studies (580 participants) that compared the use of thrombolytic agents with other interventions. The evidence is current to January 2015. The evidence is current to February 2016. The evidence is current to September 2014. Thrombolytic therapy may improve catheter function when compared to placebo, but there is no evidence available to suggest an optimal dose or administration method. The evidence is based on only one study with large confidence limits, high risk of bias and imprecision in the estimates of adverse events. Using a physical disruption of a fibrin sheath with interventional radiology techniques may be as effective as the use of a pharmaceutical thrombolytic agent for the immediate management of dysfunctional catheters. The quality of the evidence was low due to the small number of participants in the studies and the low number of participants in the studies.

We found 17 trials reporting on 22 treatment comparisons (2674 patients). The results of the trials showed that the addition of a drug to the regimen was beneficial for cancer response, but the results suggest no difference in survival time or time to progression. The positive effect on tumour response was also associated with increased toxicity.

The review found that patients discharged from a NLU were better prepared for discharge but it is unclear if this is only a product of an increased length of inpatient stay. The cost of care on the NLU was higher for UK studies but lower for US based studies.

We included 11 studies with 414 participants in the review. Two studies compared therapeutic ultrasound with placebo, two compared one ultrasound regimen with another, two compared ultrasound with other non-surgical intervention, and six compared ultrasound as part of a multi-component intervention with other non-surgical intervention (for example, exercises and splint). The risk of bias was low in some studies and unclear or high in other studies, with only two reporting that the allocation sequence was concealed and six reporting that participants were blinded to treatment. There is insufficient evidence that one therapeutic ultrasound regimen is more effective than another. Only two studies reported the primary outcome of interest, short-term overall improvement (any measure in which patients indicate the intensity of their complaints compared with baseline, for example, global rating of improvement, satisfaction with treatment), within three months post-treatment. One low quality study with 68 participants found that when compared with placebo, therapeutic ultrasound may increase the chance of experiencing short-term overall improvement at the end of seven weeks treatment, but losses to follow-up and failure to adjust for the correlation between wrists in participants with bilateral CTS in this study suggest that this data should be interpreted with caution. Another low quality study with 60 participants found that at three months post-treatment therapeutic ultrasound plus splint increased the chance of short-term overall improvement (patient satisfaction) when compared with splint alone, but decreased the chance of short-term overall improvement when compared with low-level laser therapy plus splint. However, the results were not statistically significant for symptoms, function, and neurophysiology parameters. No studies reported any adverse effects of therapeutic ultrasound, but this outcome was only measured in three studies. More adverse effects data are required before any firm conclusions on the safety of therapeutic ultrasound are made.

We included eight studies involving about 10 000 participants. The active interventions were pravastatin, atorvastatin, simvastatin, clofibrate, and conjugated oestrogen. The studies showed that statin therapy alone had a moderate benefit in reducing follow-up vascular events in patients with a history of ischaemic stroke or TIA. There was no evidence that this intervention reduced all-cause mortality or sudden death. Three studies showed a reduction in follow-up serious vascular events. There is evidence that statin therapy in patients with a history of ischaemic stroke or TIA significantly reduces following major heart events but only marginally reduces the risk of stroke recurrence. There is no clear evidence of beneficial effect from statins in those with previous haemorrhagic stroke and it is unclear whether statins should be started immediately post stroke or later. In view of this and the evidence of the benefit of statin therapy in patients with a history of heart disease, patients with ischaemic stroke or TIA, with or without a history of established heart disease, should receive statins.

The review of studies found that the effectiveness of statewide tobacco control programmes, including mass media campaigns, is based on the results of a small number of studies. There is evidence that comprehensive tobacco control programmes which include mass media campaigns can be effective in changing smoking behaviour in adults, but the evidence is from a heterogeneous group of studies of variable methodological quality. One state-wide tobacco control programme (Masachusetts) showed positive results up to eight years after the campaign. One (Caifornia) showed positive results during the period of adequate funding and implementation and in final evaluation since the start of the programme. Six of nine studies performed in communities or regions showed some positive effects on smoking behaviour and at least one significant change in smoking prevalence (Sydney). The intensity and duration of mass media campaigns may influence effectiveness, but length of follow-up and concurrent secular trends and events can make this difficult to quantify. No consistent relationship was observed between campaign effectiveness and age, education, ethnicity or male.

We included 24 studies with a total of 2166 participants. The evidence is current to January 2016. Seventeen studies that compared yoga versus no therapy showed that yoga improved health-related quality of life, reduced fatigue and reduced sleep disturbances in the short term. The funnel plot for health-related quality of life was asymmetrical, favouring no therapy, and the funnel plot for fatigue was about symmetrical. We included one randomised controlled trial (RCT) that compared early post-operative imaging with no early post-operative imaging in 125 people who had surgery for glioblastoma. The study was at high risk of bias in the study design and the results were not well reported. The study showed that early post-operative imaging may be no difference to survival in patients who will receive combined chemoradiation treatment. However, the study did not report on other outcomes. We found no evidence on the effectiveness of other imaging schedules. In addition, we found no relevant economic evaluations assessing the effectiveness of the different imaging strategies. The effect of different imaging strategies on survival and other health outcomes remains largely unknown. The current imaging schedules in glioma are pragmatic than evidence-based.

We included three studies involving 161 people with schizophrenia. The evidence is current to January 2016. There was no clear difference between chlorpromazine and metiapine for clinically important improvement in global state, and there were no useable data available for the other key outcomes of clinically important improvement in mental state, readmission due to relapse, satisfaction with treatment, aggressive or violent behaviour, or cost of care.

The review found that nitroimidazole antibiotics, mesalamine and immunosuppressive therapy with azathioprine/6-MP or infliximab are better than placebo for the prevention of post-operative recurrence of Crohn's disease. However, the cost, toxicity and tolerability of these treatments are not well understood.

We included seven studies (a total of 825 participants) that compared the effects of a psychosocial intervention with treatment as usual or educational intervention only. The studies were conducted in the USA, the United States, the United States, and the United States. The interventions considered in the studies were: cognitive-behavioural coping skills training (one study), 12-step programme (one study), short intervention (three studies), motivational interviewing (two studies), and short motivational interviewing (one study). Two studies were considered in two comparisons. There were no data for the secondary outcome, alcohol-related harm. The results were as follow-up. Comparison 1: cognitive-behavioural coping skills training versus 12-step programme (one study, 41 participants) There was no significant difference between groups for either of the primary outcomes (alcohol abstinence assessed with Substance Abuse Calendar and breathalyser at one year) or retention in treatment, measured at end of treatment, or for any of the secondary outcomes reported. The quality of evidence for the primary outcomes was very low. Comparison 2: short intervention versus treatment as usual (three studies, 197 participants) There was no significant difference between groups for either of the primary outcomes (alcohol use, measured as scores on the Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test (AUDIT) or Alcohol, Smoking and Substance Involvement Screening Test (ASSIST) at three months; or for any of the secondary outcomes reported. The quality of evidence for the primary outcomes was low. Comparison 3: motivational interviewing versus treatment as usual or education only (three studies, 462 participants) There was no significant difference between groups for either of the primary outcomes (alcohol use, measured as scores on the AUDIT or ASSIST at three months); or for any of the secondary outcomes reported. The quality of evidence for the primary outcomes was low. The quality of evidence for the primary outcomes was low.

We included one study that compared a six-month, home-based, combined muscle strength and recumbent cycle ergometry training program with usual care in 14 people with SMA. The age range of the participants was between 10 years and 48 years. The study was evaluator-blind, but personnel and participants could not be blinded to the intervention, which placed the results at a high risk of bias. Participants performed strength training as prescribed, but 50% of the participants did not achieve the intended aerobic exercise training regimen. The study used change in walking distance on the six-minute walk test as a measure of function; a minimal detectable change is 24.0 m. The difference from baseline to six months' follow-up in the training group (9.4 m) was not detectable different from the change in the usual care group (-0.12 mL/kg/min). The study assessed heart exercise capacity, evaluated by the change from baseline to six months' follow-up in peak oxygen uptake (VO2max), was similar in the training group (-0.12 mL/kg/min). The study assessed function on the Hammersmith Functional Motor Scale - expanded (HFMSE), which has a range of possible scores from 0 to 66, with an increase of 3 or more points indicating clinically significant improvement. The training group showed a small improvement in muscle strength, expressed as the manual muscle testing (MMT) total score, which ranges from 28 (weakest) to 280 (strongest). The study showed a small improvement in muscle strength, expressed as the manual muscle testing (MMT) total score, which ranges from 28 (healing most) to 280 (strongest). The review of trials found that surgery may provide pain relief more quickly than physiotherapy or high-quality collars in patients with cervical radiculopathy; but there is little or no difference in the long-term. There is very low quality evidence that patients with mild myelopathy feel subjectively better shortly after surgery, but there is little or no difference in the long-term.

We found nine studies that compared telephone consultation with normal care. Six studies compared telephone consultation by a doctor, one by a nurse and one by a clinic clerk. Three studies compared telephone consultation by different types of health care workers; two compared nurses with doctors and one compared health assistants with doctors or nurses. Three of five studies found a reduction in visits to GP's but two found a significant increase in return consultations. In general at least 50% of calls were treated by telephone advice alone. Seven studies looked at accident and emergency department visits, six showed no difference between the groups and one, of nurse telephone consultation, found an increase in visits. Two studies reported deaths and found no difference between nurse telephone triage and normal care. Telephone consultation may reduce the number of surgery contacts and out-of-hours visits by general practitioners. However, questions remain about its effect on service use, safety, cost and patient satisfaction.
