We searched for studies that compared the effects of inhaler training with no training. We found 29 studies (n = 2210) that compared inhaler training with a control group or with a training group. The studies were conducted in the USA, the United States, the UK and the USA. The studies included people with asthma, and follow-up ranged from 2 to 26 weeks. Most studies were at low or unclear risk of biases and at high risk of biases associated with blinding. We found that inhaler training with education and multi-media training improved technique in most studies after the intervention and at follow-up. However, the number of people who demonstrated right or 'good enough' technique was more effective than review scores. The quality of the evidence was moderate to low for the effect of inhaler training on asthma-related adverse events, and low for the effect of inhaler training on asthma-related absence from work or school.

We included three small trials, involving 226 participants. One trial included patients with presumed ischaemic stroke without computerised tomography (CT) verification, and the other two trials included patients with CT-verified ICH. The data on the primary outcome measure (death and dependency) were not available in any of the trials. Death and disability could be measured in the larger ICS trial without differences between the mannitol and control groups. Case death was not reported in the trial of ischaemic stroke. Case death did not differ between the mannitol and control groups in the ICS trials. Adverse events were either not found or not reported. The change in clinical condition was reported in two trials, and the proportion of people with worsening or not improving condition did not differ significantly between mannitol-treated patients and control groups. There is currently not enough evidence to support the routine use of mannitol in acute stroke patients. Further trials are needed to confirm or refute whether mannitol is beneficial in acute stroke.

We found six trials with 425 patients randomized to D-penicillamine and 258 to placebo. The results showed that D-pinicllamine was effective in reducing the disease activity of patients with rheumatoid arthritis. However, it was more likely to be a toxicity-related effect than a clinically significant benefit. The effects on long-term functional status and radiological progression are not clear from this review.

We included four studies. All of them were of low quality. The studies compared the decoction of Huangqi compounds with chemotherapy alone, and the fourth study compared the decoction of Huangqi compounds with two other Chinese herbal interventions. The results suggest that decoctions of Huangqi compounds may stimulate immune-competent cells and reduce side effects in patients treated with chemotherapy. However, there is no robust evidence of benefit. We found no evidence of harm resulting from the use of Chinese herbs. We need high quality randomised controlled trials investigating the effects of decoctions of Chinese herbs, particularly Astragalus sp. (as in Huangqi), on chemotherapy-related side effects.

We searched for studies that compared vitamin A and/or DHA with placebo or no treatment in people with RP. We found three randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that were included in this review. The trials included a total of 866 participants aged four to 55 years with RP of all types of genetic predisposition. One trial evaluated the effect of vitamin A alone, one trial evaluated DHA alone, and a third trial evaluated DHA and vitamin A versus vitamin A alone. No trial reported a statistically significant benefit of vitamin supplementation on the progression of visual field loss or visual acuity loss. Two of the three trials reported statistically significant differences in ERG amplitudes between some subgroups of participants, but these results have not been replicated or substantiated by findings in any of the other trials. The evidence is current to January 2016. There is no clear evidence for benefit of treatment with vitamin A and/or DHA for people with RP, in terms of the average change in visual field and ERG amplitudes at one year and the average change in visual acuity at five years follow-up. In future RCTs, as some of the studies in this review included unplanned subgroup analysis that suggested different effects based on previous vitamin A exposure, investigators should consider examining this issue. Future trials should take into account the changes observed in ERG amplitudes and other outcome measures from trials included in this review, in addition to previous cohort studies, when calculating sample sizes to ensure adequate power to detect clinically and statistically significant difference between treatment groups.

We included three randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with a total of 414 participants at risk of work loss. The majority of participants had IA, most with RA and to a lower level as well as AS. The interventions aimed to prevent work loss and improve work functioning in several ways: firstly by evaluating work changes or adaptations and secondly by providing any person-directed interventions including vocational counselling, advice or education. Interventions directly targeted at the work environment were minimal and included work visits (one trial) or any actions by an occupational physician (one trial). The duration or dose of the interventions varied from two 1.5-hour sessions (one trial) over five months, two consultation and multidisciplinary treatments during three months (one trial), to six to eight individual or group sessions over six months (also one trial). All participants were recruited through rheumatology clinics, both in or outside hospital. The studies investigated work loss (n = two RCTs; 382 participants), work absenteeism (n = one RCT; 32 participants) and work functioning (n = one study; 32 participants). We evaluated the two small trials as having a high risk of bias and the large trial as having a low risk of bias. Trials showed marked differences in how they performed on risk of bias items, particularly on performance bias. We judged the quality of the evidence using the GRADE approach and judged there to be very low quality evidence across the three reported outcomes. of the two RCTs investigating work loss, the larger one (n = 242 participants) reported a large statistically significant reduction in work loss (relative risk (RR) = 0.35, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.418 to 0.68) and the other RCT (n = 140 participants) reported similar effects in both groups, but the CI was very wide (RR = 1.05, 95% CI 0.553 to 21.6). The last one probably suffered from performance bias and we judged it to have a high risk of bias. The one small trial investigating sickness absenteeism found uncertain results at six months' follow-up. We found three studies that included a total of 285 newborns (140 received arginine) from three countries. The studies were of high quality and included a total of 285 infants. The studies showed that arginine supplementation reduced the risk of development of NEC in preterm newborns. The number needed to treat for an additional beneficial outcome (NNTB) as required to prevent the development of NEC was 6 (95% CI 4 to 10). The studies showed that arginine supplementation reduced the risk of development of NEC in stage 1 and stage 3 in preterm newborns. The results showed that arginine supplementation reduced the risk of death related to NEC in preterm newborns. The studies did not show any significant side effects, including low blood pressure or alterations in glucose homeostasis.

We found four studies involving 43 participants with acute sinusitis. The trials were well-designed and double-blind and studied in the adolescent group (13 to 21 days) or the adult group (15 to 21 days). The rates of loss to follow-up were 7%, 11%, 41% and 10%. The results from the three trials included in the meta-analysis showed that participants receiving INCS were more likely to have resolution or improvement in symptoms than those receiving placebo or no intervention for 15 or 21 days. Higher doses of INCS had a stronger effect on improvement of symptoms or total relief: for mometasone furoate 400 g versus 200 g. No significant adverse events were reported and there was no significant difference in the drop-out and recurrence rates for the two treatment groups and for groups receiving higher doses of INCS. The evidence is current to January 2016.

The review of trials found that the use of vancomycin in low doses reduces the incidence of nosocomial sepsis in the newborn. However, the methods of these studies may have contributed to the low rate of sepsis in the treated groups, as the blood cultures taken from central lines may have failed to grow due to the low levels of vancomycin in the infusate. There is insufficient evidence to ascertain the risk of development of vancomycin resistant organisms in the nurseries involved in these trials.

We found 13 trials that compared dressings or topical agents for postoperative wounds healing by secondary intention. The evidence is current to January 2016. The evidence is current to February 2015. The results of this review suggest that a plaster cast applied to an amputation stump accelerated wound healing compared with elastic compression. There were no significant differences in healing for other dressing comparisons (e.g. gauze, foam, alginate; 11 trials). PAIN: Gaze was associated with significantly more pain for patients than other dressings (4 trials). Patient SATISFACTION: Patients treated with gaze were less satisfied compared with those receiving alternative dressings (3 trials). COSTS: Gauze is inexpensive but its use is associated with significantly more nursing time than foam 2 trials. LENS of hospital stay: Four trials showed no difference in length of hospital stay. One trial found shorter hospital stay in people after amputation when plaster casts were used compared with elastic compression (WMD -30.10 days). The evidence is current to May 2014.

We included four studies involving 494 participants. Three studies involving 383 participants provided data on the proportion of participants who had achieved a therapeutic INR by day five. The review authors analyzed each study separately because it was not possible to perform a subgroup analysis by inpatient or outpatient status. One study showed significant benefit of a 10 mg warfarin nomogram for the proportion of outpatients with VTE who had achieved a therapeutic INR by day five, with the number needed to treat for an additional beneficial outcome (NNTB = 3), another study showed significant benefit of a 5 mg warfarin nomogram in outpatients with VTE, and a third study, which included both inpatient and outpatient status, showed no difference. No difference was found in recurrent venous thromboembolism at 90 days, major bleeding at 14 to 90 days, minor bleeding at 14 to 90 days, or length of hospital stay. There was no difference in recurrent venous thromboembolism at 90 days, major bleeding at 14 to 90 days, small bleeding at 14 to 90 days, or length of hospital stay. There was no difference in recurrent venous thromboembolism at 90 days, major bleeding at 14 to 90 days, small bleeding at 14 to 90 days, or length of hospital stay. The quality of the evidence was moderate to very low.

We included seven randomised controlled trials with a total of 550 participants. Three trials compared models of improved care in the inpatient setting with conventional care. Two trials compared an improved care model provided in inpatient settings and at home after discharge with conventional care. Two trials compared geriatrician-led care in-hospital to conventional care led by the orthopaedic team. There were no data from any study for our primary outcome of health-related quality of life. There was only very low certainty for our other primary outcome, activities of daily living and functional performance, therefore we were unable to draw any conclusions with confidence. There was low-certainty that improved care and rehabilitation in-hospital may reduce rates of postoperative delirium and very low-certainty associating it with lower rates of some other complications. There was also low-certainty that, compared to orthopaedic-led management, geriatrician-led management may lead to shorter hospital stay. We found limited evidence that some of the models of improved rehabilitation and care used in the included trials may show benefits over usual care for preventing delirium and reducing length of stay.

The review included 11 trials. Seven trials compared early versus delayed nutrition, data on death and disability were available for all seven trials (284 participants). The relative risk (RR) for death or disability at the end of follow-up was 0.67 (95% CI 0.41 to 1.07). Data on disability were available for three trials (388 participants). Seven trials compared parenteral versus enteral nutrition. Because early support often involves parenteral nutrition, three of the trials are also included in the previous analyses. Five trials (207 participants) reported mortality. The RR for death at the end of follow-up period was 0.66 (0.41 to 107). Two trials provided data on death and disability. One trial compared gastric versus jejunal enteral nutrition, there were no deaths and the RR was not estimateable. This review suggests that early feeding may be associated with a trend towards better outcomes in terms of survival and disability. Further trials are required. These trials should report not only nutritional outcomes but also the effect on death and disability.

We found three studies, involving 451 participants. The trials were conducted in the USA, the USA, and the USA. Two studies compared dexamethasone to placebo, and the third study compared a number of additional interventions in different combinations, including metoclopramide, chlorpromazine, tropisetron, and dexamethasone. The duration of the studies ranged from seven to 14 days. We included two studies (127 participants) with data at eight days in the meta-analysis for nausea intensity; no data were available that incorporated the same outcome measures for the third study. Corticosteroids with dexamethasone resulted in less nausea (measured on a scale of 0 to 10 with a lower score indicating less nausea) compared to placebo at eight days. However, this results was not statistically significant. The outcomes of nausea and vomiting were not significant between groups, and the interventions were well tolerated. Factors limiting statistical analysis included the lack of standardised measurements of nausea, and the use of different agents, doses, and comparisons. Subgroup analysis by type of cancer was not possible due to insufficient data. The quality of this evidence was downgraded by three levels, from high to very low due to imprecision, likely selection bias, attrition bias, and the small number of participants in the included studies.

We included 10 studies that compared antibiotics with no antibiotics to prevent LRTIs in children. The studies were conducted in the USA, the United States, the UK and the USA. The studies were conducted for seven days to three years. The studies included children with HIV infection, cystic fibrosis or cancer. The studies included children with low birth weight infants with underlying respiratory disorders. The studies were of moderate quality. The evidence is current to January 2015. In children with HIV infection, a systolic blood pressure test showed no difference in the risk of LRTIs with antibiotics or with a systolic blood pressure test. There was no difference in the risk of death with antibiotics or with a systolic blood pressure test. There was no difference in the rate of hospital admissions with antibiotics or with a systolic blood pressure test. There was no evidence of increased adverse events due to antibiotic prophylaxis. However, there was scant reporting of antibiotic resistance - the one study that did assess this found no increase.

We included nine randomised controlled trials (RCTs) involving 519 participants. The studies compared different gases for establishing pneumoperitoneum: nitrous oxide (three trials), helium (five trials), or room air (one trial) was compared to carbon dioxide. Three trials randomised participants to nitrous oxygen (100 participants) or carbon dioxide pneumoperitoneum (96 participants). There was insufficient evidence to determine the effects of nitrous oxide and carbon dioxide on heart and lung complications, or surgical death. There were no serious adverse events related to either nitrous oxide or carbon dioxide pneumoperitoneum (three studies; 196 participants). We could not combine data from two trials (140 participants) which individually showed lower pain scores (a difference of about one visual analogue score on a scale of 1 to 10 with lower numbers indicating less pain) with nitrous oxide pneumoperitoneum at various time points on the first postoperative day, and this was rated asvery low quality. Four trials randomised participants to helium pneumoperitoneum (69 participants) or carbon dioxide pneumoperitoneum (75 participants) and one trial involving 33 participants did not state the number of participants in each group. There was insufficient evidence to determine the effects of helium or carbon dioxide on heart and lung complications, or pain scores (visual analogue score on a scale of 1 to 10 with lower numbers indicating less pain). There were three serious adverse events (subcutaneous emphysema) related to helium pneumoperitoneum. The review of trials found that there was no evidence of benefit from steroid treatment in patients with acute rheumatic fever. However, the quality of the studies was low and the risk of bias was significant. Further research is needed to assess the effects of corticosteroids and intravenous immunoglobulins in patients with acute rheumatic fever.

We included six studies with a total of 12294 participants from 79 communities. Two studies found that insecticide spray is effective in reducing trachoma, but this effect was not demonstrated in another study that used insecticides. One study found that other fly control measure, latrine provision, reduced trachoma by 29.5% compared to no intervention; this was, however, not statistically significant different and findings have not been confirmed by a more recent study. One study revealed that health education reduced the incidence of trachoma. This findings were not confirmed by a second study, however, which found that a modest health education programme with moderate water supply did not reduce trachoma. However, all the studies have some methodological concerns. There is some evidence from two trials that insecticides are effective in reducing trachoma, but this effect was not demonstrated in another trial that used insecticides. Two trials on latrine provision as a fly control measure have not demonstrated significant trachoma reduction. Health education had showed significant reduction of trachoma in one study but another study did not demonstrate similar findings. Generally, there is a dearth of data to determine the effectiveness of all aspects of environmental sanitation in the control of trachoma.

We searched for studies that compared CBT with usual care, other psychological therapies, or other treatments for fatigue. We found 15 studies (1043 participants) that compared CBT with usual care and other psychological therapy. The results of the studies were not consistent. The evidence is current to January 2016. CBT is effective in reducing the symptoms of fatigue at post-treatment compared with usual care, and may be more effective in reducing fatigue symptoms compared with other psychological therapies. The evidence base at follow-up was limited to a small group of studies with inconsistent findings. There is a lack of evidence on the comparative effectiveness of CBT alone or in combination with other treatments, and further studies are needed to inform the development of effective treatment programmes for people with CFS.

We found only one randomised controlled trial (RCT) that compared two treatments for children with CAS. The study included 26 children aged 4 to 12 years, with mild to moderate CAS of unknown cause. The treatment groups were the Nuffield Dyspraxia Programme-3 (NDP-3), and the Rapid Syllable Transitions Treatment (rest). The treatments were delivered intensively in one-hour sessions, four days a week for three weeks, in a university clinic in Australia. Speech pathology students delivered the treatments in the English language. The results showed that both the NDP-3 and ReST therapies showed improvement at one month post-therapy. However, the effect was slightly higher for NDP-3 than ReST in the outcomes of accuracy of production on treated words, speech production consistency, and accuracy of connected speech. The quality of the evidence was moderate due to the small number of participants in the study and the small number of participants in the study.

We included 43 trials involving predominantly older people with mainly trochanteric fractures. Twenty-two trials (3749 participants) compared the Gamma nail with the sliding hip screw (SHS). The Gamma nail was associated with increased risk of operative and later fracture of the femur and increased reoperation rate. There were no major differences between implants in wound infection, mortality or medical complications. Five trials (623 participants) compared the intramedullary hip screw (IMHS) with the SHS. Fracture fixation complications were more common in the IMHS group. Results for post-operative complications, mortality and functional outcomes were similar in both groups. Three trials (394 participants) showed no difference in fracture fixation complications, reoperative complications, wound infection and length of hospital stay for proximal femoral nail (PFN) versus the SHS. No of the 10 trials (1491 participants) of other nail versus extramedullary implant comparisons for trochanteric fractures provided sufficient evidence to establish definite differences between the implants under test. Two trials (65 participants) found intramedullary nails were associated with fewer fracture fixation complications than fixed angle plates for unstable fractures at the level of the lower trochanter. Two trials (124 participants) found a tendency to less fracture healing complications with the intramedullary nails compared with fixed angle plates for subtrochanteric fractures. The SHS is better for preventing fractures from reoperatively causing pain and pain-related complications. Further studies are needed to confirm whether more recently developed designs of intramedullary nails prevent the complications of previous nails.

We searched for studies that compared palliative surgery and medical management for bowel obstruction in ovarian cancer. We found only one study that was included in the review. The study included 47 women who received either palliative surgery (n = 27) or medical management with Octreotide (n = 20). The study reported on the overall survival and perioperative mortality and morbidity. Women with poor performance status were excluded from surgery. The study found that women who received surgery had significantly better survival than women who received Ocreotide, after adjustment for important prognostic factors. However, the magnitude of this effect was not reported. Quality of life (QoL) was not reported and adverse events were incompletely reported. We found only low quality evidence comparing palliative surgery and medical management for bowel obstruction in ovarian cancer. Therefore we are unable to reach definite conclusions about the relative benefits and harms of the two forms of treatment, or to identify sub-groups of women who are likely to benefit from one treatment or the other. However, there is weak evidence in support of surgical management to prolong survival.

The review included four trials with a total of 244 women with PCOS receiving 12 weeks or 6 weeks of treatment. Two trials (184 women randomised) studied the effects of simvastatin and two trials (60 women randomised) studied the effects of atorvastatin. There was no good evidence that statins improved menstrual regularity, spontaneous ovulation rate, hirsutism or acne, either alone or in combination with the combined oral contraceptive pill (OCP). No significant effects on body mass index (BMI). Statins were effective in lowering testosterone levels (nmol/L) when used alone or with the OCP. Statins also improved total cholesterol, low-density lipoprotein (LDL) and triglycerides but had no significant effect on high-density lipoprotein (HDL) levels, high sensitivity (HS) C-reactive protein (HS-CRP), fasting insulin or homeostatic model assessment (HOMA) insulin resistance. No serious adverse events were reported in any of the included studies.

We searched for studies that compared the use of palliative treatments for vaginal bleeding in women with advanced cervical cancer. We found no new studies. We found no evidence from controlled trials to support or refute the use of any of the proposed interventions compared with radiotherapy. Therefore, the choice of intervention will be based on local resources. Radiotherapy techniques for managing vaginal bleeding are not readily available in resource-poor settings, where advanced cases of cervical cancer are predominant.

We found two studies that compared the effectiveness of a private contract with international nongovernmental groups (NFGs) for district health systems management. The results of one study conducted in Cambodia show that private contracts with international nongovernmental groups (NFGs) for district health system management may improve health care access and use. The findings of the other study provide low quality evidence that intermittent training courses over 18 months may improve district health system managers' performance. In three countries in Latin America, managers who did not receive the intermittent training courses had between 2.4 and 8.3 times more management deficiencies than managers who received the training courses. No studies that aimed to investigate interventions for retaining district health systems managers met our study selection criteria for inclusion in this review.

We included three studies that included 123 people. The methods used for blinding the participants and researchers to the treatment group were not reported, but as the comparison is surgical treatment with medical treatment this bias is difficult to avoid. There was no clear evidence of a difference in mortality between treatment groups (risk ratio (RR) 0.56, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.13 to 242); however, the analysis was underpowered to detect a difference between groups. Out of the 123 people randomized and treated, six people died. Among people randomized to surgery, there was some evidence from three small studies that surgical treatment was preferable to nonsurgical management in reducing pneumonia, chest deformity, tracheostomy, duration of mechanical ventilation, length of intensive care unit stay, and length of hospital stay. Chest pain, chest tightness, body pain, and adverse effects were each measured in one study. There was some evidence from three small studies that surgical treatment was preferable to nonsurgical management in reducing lung infection, chest deformity, tracheostomy, duration of mechanical ventilation, and length of intensive care unit stay. Further well-designed studies with a sufficient sample size are needed to confirm these results and to detect possible surgical effects on mortality.

We searched for studies that compared cerivastatin with placebo or other drugs to reduce the risk of heart disease. We found 50 trials (23 randomised controlled trials and 31 before-and-after studies) that compared cerivastatin with placebo, atorvastatin, fluvastatin and rosuvastatin. The trials were of any age with and without heart disease and the trials studied cerivastatin effects in a treatment period of three to 12 weeks. The participants were of any age with and without heart disease and the trials studied cerivastatin effects within a treatment period of three to 12 weeks. The evidence is current to January 2016. Cerivastatin was about 250-fold more potent than fluvastatin, 20-fold more than atorvastatin and 5.5-fold more potent than rosuvastatin at reducing LDL cholesterol, total cholesterol and triglyceride. Cerivastatin was about 230-fold more potent than fluvastatin, 18-fold more potent than atorvastatin and six-fold more potent than rosuvastatin at reducing total cholesterol, and 125-fold more potent than fluvastatin, 11-fold more potent than atorvastatin and 13-fold more potent than rosuvastatin at reducing triglycerides. There was no dose-related effect of cerivastatin on HDL cholesterol, but overall cerivastatin increased HDL cholesterol by 5%. The evidence is current to February 2015.

We included 28 studies with a total of 6851 patients. The evidence is current to January 2016. The evidence is current to February 2015. The results of this review suggest that remote ischaemic preconditioning did not reduce the number of patients who had a blood clot in their blood, the need for dialysis, or the length of hospital stay. However, it was uncertain whether remote ischaemic preconditioning by cuff inflation led to increased adverse effects. The evidence is current to May 2017. The results of this review suggest that remote ischaemic preconditioning did not reduce the need for dialysis, the length of hospital stay, or the number of patients who had a death from any cause. The evidence is current to October 2014.

We found 12 trials with 703 participants. Eight trials mainly investigated the effectiveness of interventions to treat or prevent fatigue after stroke, of which six trials with seven comparisons provided data suitable for meta-analysis (five pharmacological interventions: fluoxetine, enerion, (-)-OSU6162, citicoline and a combination of Chinese herbs); and two non-pharmacological interventions: a fatigue education programme and a mindfulness-based stress reduction programme. The fatigue severity was lower in the intervention groups than in the control groups (244 participants), but the beneficial effect was not seen in trials that had used adequate allocation concealment (two trials, 89 participants) or trials that had used adequate blinding of outcome assessors (four trials, 198 participants). No trial primarily investigated the efficacy in preventing fatigue. Four trials (248 participants) did not mainly investigate the effectiveness of interventions to treat or prevent fatigue but other symptoms after stroke. None of these interventions showed any benefit on reducing fatigue, which included tirilazad mesylate, continuous positive airway pressure for sleep apnoea, antidepressants and a self management programme for recovery from chronic diseases. There was insufficient evidence on the effectiveness of any intervention to treat or prevent fatigue after stroke. Trials to date have been small and heterogeneous, and some have had a high risk of bias. Some of the interventions described were feasible in people with stroke, but their efficacy should be evaluated in RCTs with a more robust study design and adequate sample sizes.

We searched for studies that compared the combination of insulin and glucose with cation-exchange resin in preterm infants. We found three randomised trials, involving 74 preterm infants. The trials were small and of uncertain quality. One study (Malone 1991), compared the combination of insulin and glucose to cation-exchange resin, showed that the combination of insulin and glucose reduced the risk of death and the risk of intraventricular haemorrhage. In the study of Hu (Hu 1999), the combination of insulin and glucose reduced the risk of intraventricular haemorrhage  grade 2 and reduced the risk of intraventricular haemorrhage  grade 2 by a total of 5%. The combination of insulin and glucose was not associated with any serious side effects. The trials did not show any difference between the two groups in the risk of death or other clinical outcomes. The results of the three trials are not clear.

We found 12 trials that compared IVIg with PEV in 623 severe affected participants. The evidence is current to January 2016. Seven trials with a variable risk of bias compared IVIg with PEV in 623 severe affected participants. In five trials with 536 participants for which the outcome was available, the total number of people who improved in a seven-grade disability scale after four weeks was not significant different between the two treatments: MD of 0.02 of a grade more improvement in a seven-grade disability scale after four weeks was not significant different, but not preventing the possibility of significant extra benefit. Three studies including a total of 75 children suggested that IVIg significantly hastens recovery compared with supportive care. The primary outcome for this review, available for only one trial with 21 mildly affected children, showed significantly more improvement in disability grade after four weeks with IVIg than supportive care alone. In one trial involving 249 participants comparing PEV followed by IVIg with PEV alone, the total number of people who improved in a seven-grade disability scale after four weeks was 0.2 (no clinically significant difference) more in the combined treatment group than in the PEV alone group; not clinically significant different, but not preventing the possibility of significant additional benefit. One trial with 34 participants comparing immunoabsorption followed by IVIg with immunoabsorption alone did not show significant extra benefit from the combined treatment. Adverse events were not significantly more frequent with either treatment but IVIg is significantly more likely to be completed than PEV. One trial in altogether 51 children showed no significant difference when the standard dose was given over two days than five days. There are no adequate comparisons of IVIg with placebo in adults, but this review provides moderate quality evidence that, in severe disease, IVIg started with two weeks from onset hastens recovery as much as PEV. Adverse events were not significantly more frequent with either treatment but IVIg is significantly more likely to be completed than PEV. Also, due to moderate quality evidence, giving IVIg after PEV did not confer significant extra benefit. In children, due to low quality evidence, there was no significant difference when the standard dose was given over two days than five days.

We included 28 randomized clinical trials (involving 9330 participants) that compared a high fraction of inspired oxygen with a routine fraction of inspired oxygen. The evidence is current to January 2016. We found that a high fraction of inspired oxygen was not associated with a higher risk of death or a lower risk of surgical site infection. The quality of the evidence was very low for all outcomes. The quality of the evidence was very low for the risk of surgery-related adverse events. The quality of the evidence was very low for the risk of surgery-related adverse events.

We searched for studies that compared antibiotics with no treatment for acute otitis media in children. We found 23 trials (3258 children) that included a range of antibiotics, participants, outcome measures and time points for evaluation. The evidence is current to January 2015. We found moderate quality evidence (six trials including 484 children) that children treated with oral antibiotics are more likely to have complete resolution at two to three months post-randomisation than those allocated to the control treatment. However, there is evidence (albeit of low quality; five trials, 742 children) indicating that children treated with antibiotics are more likely to have diarrhoea, vomiting or skin rash. In terms of other secondary outcomes, only two trials (849 children) reported on hearing levels at two to four weeks and found conflicting results. No of the trials reported data on speech, language and cognitive development or quality of life. Low quality evidence did not show that oral antibiotics were associated with a decrease in the rate of ventilation tube insertion (two trials, 121 children) or in tympanic membrane follow-up (one trial, 103 children). Low quality evidence showed that children treated with antibiotics were less likely to have acute otitis media episodes within four to eight weeks and within six months post-randomisation. However, it should, however, be noted that the beneficial effect of oral antibiotics on acute otitis media episodes is not known.

We included 12 studies, nine randomised controlled trials and three before and after studies. The results show that reducing protein intake may slightly reduce the risk of kidney failure in patients with type 1 diabetes but not statistically significant. However, questions about the level of protein intake and compliance remain. Further longer-term research on large representative groups of patients with both type 1 and type 2 diabetes mellitus is needed.

We found 19 studies involving 3480 people. The evidence is current to January 2016. The studies were of good methodological quality and seven were of lower quality. The results suggest that most people had a good recovery when appropriate information was given, without the need for additional specific interventions. For moderate to severe brain injury, 'strong evidence' showed benefit from formal intervention, and 'limited evidence' indicated that starting rehabilitation early after injury leads to better outcomes. For participants with moderate to severe brain injury already in rehabilitation, 'strong evidence' revealed that more intensive programmes are associated with earlier functional gains, and 'moderate evidence' suggested that continued outpatient therapy may help to sustain gains made in early post-acute rehabilitation. The context of multi-disciplinary rehabilitation appears to influence outcomes. 'Stony evidence' suggests that a milieu-oriented model for patients with severe brain injury is an effective approach for patients requiring neuropsychological rehabilitation after severe brain injury. 'Limical evidence' shows that specialist in-patient rehabilitation and specialist multi-disciplinary community rehabilitation may provide additional functional gains, but studies serve to show that there are no effective alternatives to specialist intervention for patients with severe brain injury.

We included one trial (involving 176 women) in this review. This trial included four groups with a factororial design. The factororial design took into account two factors, i.e. infant location in relation to the mother and the type of infant apparel. We combined three of the groups as the intervention (rooming-in) group and the fourth group acted as the control (separate care). The primary outcome, duration of any breastfeeding was reported by authors as average rates because the distribution was found to be skewed. They reported the total duration of any breastfeeding to be four months, with no difference found between groups. The number of breastfeeds per day on day four postpartum was slightly higher in the rooming-in group than the different care group, i.e. seven times per day. However, between-group comparison of this outcome was not appropriate as all infants in the different care group were breastfed at a fixed schedule of seven times per day, resulting in no estimateable comparison. The rate of exclusive breastfeeding on day four postpartum before discharge from hospital was significantly higher in the rooming-in group 86% (99 of 115) compared with different care group, 45% (17 of 38). No other pre-specific outcomes were reported. We found little evidence to support or refute the practice of rooming-in versus mother-infant separation. Further well-designed trials to investigate full mother-infant rooming-in versus partial rooming-in or different care groups including all important outcomes are needed.

The review included eight randomised controlled trials involving 660 participants. Seven of the eight studies were of poor quality. Follow-up time was less than one month in six trials. Only two trials provided data for the number of participants who were death or dependent at the end of 28 days of treatment, indicating a significantly lower rate of death and dependency in the sanchi group than in the control group. One trial reported higher Barthel index scores in the sanchi group. Pooled analysis of seven trials showed that sanchi may improve neurological deficit more than control with a significant difference. The total case mortality rate was lower than 1% indicating that participants likely had mild strokes. Few adverse events were reported. Data were limited in respect of stroke recurrence and quality of life.

The review of trials found that there is insufficient evidence to determine possible benefits or disadvantages of immediate, immediate-delayed or delayed implants. There is a suggestion that immediate and immediate-delayed implants may be at higher risk of implant failures and complications than delayed implants on the other side the aesthetic outcome might be better when placing implants only after teeth extraction. There is not enough reliable evidence supporting or refuting the need for augmentation procedures at immediate implants placed in new extraction sockets or whether any of the augmentation techniques is superior to the other.

We found two trials that compared clioquinol (PBT1) with placebo. One trial compared clioquinol (PBT1) with placebo in 36 patients and 32 had sufficient data for per protocol analysis. There was no significant difference in cognitive function (as measured on the Alzheimer's Disease Assessment Scale - Cognition (ADAS-Cog)) between the active treatment and placebo groups at 36 weeks. The difference in total memory (as measured on the Neuropsychological Test Battery (NTB) composite or memory) between the active treatment and placebo group at weeks 24 and 36 was a difference of 7.37 (95% confidence interval (CI) 1.51 to 13.24 and 66 (95% CI -0.50 to 13.23), respectively. There was no significant effect on non-cognitive symptoms or clinical global impression. One participant in the active treatment group developed neurological symptoms (impaired visual acuity and colour vision) which resolved on cessation of treatment and were likely attributable to the drug. In the second trial a follow-up time of 78 participants with mild Alzheimer's dementia was compared with placebo. There was no significant difference in the neuropsychological Test Battery (NTB) composite or memory between placebo and PBT2 in the least squares mean change from baseline at week 12. However, two executive function component tests of the NTB showed significant improvement over placebo in the PBT2 250 mg group from baseline to week 12: category fluency test (2.8 words, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.1 to 5.4; P = 0.041) and trail making part B (-48.0 s, 95% CI -83.o to -13.o; P = 0.29). In the executive factor Z score, the difference in least squares mean change from baseline at week 12 for PBT2 250 mg compared with placebo was 027 (101 to 053); p+- a total of 58 participants. There was no significant effect on cognitive function on Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) or ADAS-Cog scales. PBT2 had a favourable safety profile. There is an absence of evidence as to whether clioquinol (PBT1) has any positive clinical effects on Alzheimer's dementia.

We included 36 studies with 2999 participants in the final review. The trials were conducted for 14 weeks on average, with some as long as 12 months. Two trials also included children. Nineteen trials included group 1 PAH participants. PDE5 inhibitors were more likely to improve their WHO functional class (a 'functioning' group) and to walk 48 metres further in 6MWD, and were 22% less likely to die over a total of 14 weeks compared to placebo. The number needed to treat to prevent one additional death was 32 participants. There was an increased risk of adverse events with PDE5 inhibitors, especially headache, gastrointestinal upset, flushing, and muscle aches and joint pains. Compared to placebo, PDE5 inhibitors plus combination therapy walked 19.66 metres further in six minutes compared to placebo. There was no evidence of a difference in WHO functional group or mortality between PDE5 inhibitors and other PAH-specific therapy. There were limited trials comparing PDE5 inhibitors directly with other PAH-specific therapy (endothelin receptor antagonists) (low-certainty evidence). There was no evidence of a difference in WHO functional group or mortality between PDE5 inhibitors and other PAH-specific therapy. Five trials compared PDE5 inhibitors with other PAH-specific therapy (endothelin receptor antagonists) (low-certainty evidence). There was no evidence of a difference in WHO functional group or mortality between PDE5 inhibitors and other PAH-specific therapy.

We included 22 trials involving 2193 participants. The evidence is current to January 2016. The trials compared multiple injections with single injections, and compared multiple injections with two injections. The results showed that multiple injections reduced the risk of primary anaesthesia failure. The time for block performance was longer with multiple injections than with two injections. However, there was no difference in time to readiness for surgery. The time for block performance was shorter with multiple injections than with two injections. However, there was no difference in time to readiness for surgery. The quality of the evidence was high for all of the outcomes, including the number of patients who had a primary anaesthesia failure, the number of patients who had an incomplete motor block, the time for block performance and the time to readiness for surgery.

We searched for studies that compared beclomethasone with placebo or non-steroids in children with mild-moderate asthma. We found three studies that compared beclomethasone with placebo or non-steroidal asthma therapy. The studies lasted a total of 54 weeks, so it is unclear whether the decrease in growth is sustained or whether it reverses with 'catch up' after therapy is discontinued. We are unable to comment on growth effects of other inhaled steroids that have likely less systemic effects.

We found three studies (139 patients) that compared APD with CPD. The studies were conducted in the USA, the USA, the UK and the USA. APD did not have significant advantages over CPD in terms of important clinical outcomes. APD may however be considered beneficial in select group of patients such as in the younger PD population and those in work or education due to its psychosocial benefits. There is a need for a randomised controlled trial comparing CPD with APD with sufficiently large patient numbers looking at important clinical outcomes including residual kidney function, accompanied by an economic evaluation to clarify the relative clinical and cost-effectiveness of both modalities.

We found only one trial with a total of 212 participants, all with spinal cord injury and open pressure ulcers classed as stage III and IV. The participants were mainly male (98.2%, 106/108) with a average age of 58.4 years in the oxandrolone group and were all male (105%, 104/104) with a average age of 57.3 years in the placebo group. This trial compared oxandrolone (20 mg/day, administered orally) with a dose of placebo (an inactive substance including 98% starch and 2% magnesium stearate) and reported data on complete healing of ulcers and adverse events. There was very low-certainty evidence that oxandrolone improves or reduces the complete healing of pressure ulcers. There was low-certainty evidence that the treatment with oxandrolone may increase the risk of non-serious adverse events reported in participants. There was very low-certainty evidence that the treatment with oxandrolone may increase the risk of serious adverse events reported in participants. The quality of the evidence was very low for the effect of oxandrolone on complete healing of ulcers and for the risk of non-serious adverse events reported in participants.

We included six randomised controlled trials involving 8372 people. The studies were conducted in the USA, Australia, America, and the United States. Four trials compared email to standard mail and two compared email to usual care. There was no difference between email and standard mail for the primary health service outcome of uptake of preventive screening. There was inconclusive evidence for patient or caregiver understanding and support. Results were inconclusive for patient or caregiver behaviours and actions. For email versus usual care only, there was no significant difference between groups for the primary outcome of patient health status and well-being. No data were reported relating to healthcare professionals or harms. The evidence on the use of email for the delivery of information on disease prevention and health promotion was weak, and therefore inadequate to inform clinical practice. The available trials mostly provide inconclusive, or no evidence for the outcomes of interest in this review. Future research needs to use high-quality study designs that take advantage of the most recent development of information technology, with consideration of the complexity of email as an intervention.

The review included 11 trials involving 855 participants. A total of nine studies used postural restrictions as their modification of the Epley manoeuvre. There was no evidence of a difference in the results for post-treatment vertigo intensity or subjective assessment of improvement in individual or pooled data. All nine studies included the conversion of a positive to a negative Dix-Halpike test as an outcome measure. The addition of postural limitations did not expose the majority of patients to risk of harm, does not pose a major inconvenience, and can be routinely discussed and advised. The addition of postural restrictions does not expose the majority of patients to risk of harm, does not pose a major discomfort, and can be routinely discussed and recommended. The review found that the addition of postural restriction did not provide any significant benefit to the treatment of BPPV. The addition of postural restrictions does not expose the majority of patients to risk of harm, does not present a major inconvenience, and can be routinely discussed and advised.

The review included four studies (a total of 231 participants) that compared different types of surgery with different types of steroids and antibiotics. The studies compared different types of surgery versus different types of steroids and antibiotics. There were three comparison groups: (one study, n = 109), (two studies, n = 87), and (one study, n = 35). All participants also received topical steroids but doses and types were the same between the treatment arms of each study, except for the study using antibiotics. In that study, the medical treatment arm had higher doses than the surgical arm. In two of the studies, the authors failed to report the outcomes of interest. However, there were important differences in the types of surgery and comparisons used in these studies, the results were similar. First, there were no significant differences between groups in the patient-reported disease-specific symptom scores or the health-related quality of life scores. Two studies (one comparing ESS plus topical steroid versus antibiotics plus high-dose topical steroid, the other ESS versus systemic steroids) did not find a difference in general health-related quality of life scores. Two studies (one comparing ESS plus topical steroid versus antibiotics plus high-dose topical steroid, the other ESS versus systemic steroids) did not find a difference in general health-related quality of life scores. The quality of this evidence is low or very low. The results of one study (ESS versus systemic steroids) showed that the surgery was more effective than the antibiotics plus high-dose topical steroid. The other study (ESS plus topical steroid versus antibiotics plus high-dose topical steroid) did not show a difference in the number of patients who had recurrences. No differences were found between the groups in the number of people who had recurrences. The quality of this evidence is low or very low.

We included eight studies (with 709 participants) that compared zinc-fortified staple foods with unfortified foods. The studies lasted between one and nine months. The studies were conducted in middle-income countries of Asia, Africa, Europe, and Latin America. The studies compared zinc-fortified staple foods with unfortified foods, or with zinc-fortified staple foods in combination with other nutrients/factors with the same foods containing other nutrients or factors without zinc. The evidence is current to January 2016. The studies compared zinc-fortified staple foods with unfortified foods, or with zinc-fortified staple foods in combination with other nutrients/factors with the same foods containing other nutrients or factors without zinc. The studies compared zinc-fortified staple foods with unfortified foods, or with zinc-fortified staple foods in combination with other nutrients/factors with the same foods containing other nutrients or factors without zinc. The evidence is current to February 2015. The evidence is current to May 2014. The evidence is current to October 2017. The evidence is current to September 2018. The evidence is current to April 2016. The evidence is current to July 2016. The evidence is current to August 2016.

The evidence is current to January 2016. We included 11 studies involving 3060 participants. The quality of the evidence was hampered by risk of bias. The results showed that use of non-steroidal antiandrogens reduced overall survival, clinical progression and treatment failure in men with metastatic disease. The use of non-steroids also increased the risk of treatment discontinuation due to adverse events, including events such as breast pain, gynaecomastia and asthenia. The risk of other adverse events, such as hot flashes, was moderate. The quality of the evidence for overall survival, clinical progression and treatment failure was rated as moderate.

We found eight trials that included 690,000 women in the analyses. The trials included a total of 600,000 women in the age range 39 to 74 years. Three trials with adequate randomisation did not show a significant reduction in breast cancer mortality at 13 years. Four trials with suboptimal randomisation showed a significant reduction in breast cancer mortality at 10 years. The RR for all seven trials combined was 0.81. We found that breast cancer mortality was an unreliable outcome that was biased in favour of screening, mainly because of different misclassification of cause of death. The trials with adequate randomisation did not find an effect of screening on total cancer mortality, including breast cancer, after 10 years or on all-cause mortality after 13 years. The number of lumpectomy and mastectomy surgery was significantly larger in the screened groups. The use of radiotherapy was also increased whereas there was no difference in the use of chemotherapy (data available in only two trials). We found that breast cancer mortality was an unreliable outcome that was biased in favour of screening, mainly due to differences in the classification of cause of death. We included four studies, with a total of 522 women in the review. One of these studies did not report outcomes per woman, and so was not included in formal analysis. Three studies investigated 10 000 units hCG compared to no priming. One study investigated 20,000 units hCG compared to 10 000 units hCG. Three studies only included women with PCOS (N = 122), while this was an exclusion criteria in the fourth study (N = 400). We rated all four studies as having an unclear risk of bias in more than one of the seven domains assessed. The quality of the evidence was low, the main limitations were lack of blinding and imprecision. In the study comparing 20,000 units hCG with 10 000 units hCG, we found no evidence of a difference in the live birth rates per woman, miscarriage rate, or clinical pregnancy rate. However, we found that there was a reduction in clinical pregnancy rates; 22% of women who received no priming achieved pregnancy, but between 7% and 23% of women who received hCG remained pregnant. The study comparing 20,000 units hCG with 10 000 units hCG did not report enough data to allow us to calculate odds ratios. No studies reported on adverse events (other than miscarriage) or drug reactions. This review found no conclusive evidence that hCG priming had an effect on live birth, pregnancy, or miscarriage rates in IVM. There was low quality evidence that suggested that hCG priming may reduce clinical pregnancy rates, but these findings were limited by the small number of data included. As no data were available on adverse events (other than miscarriage) or drug responses, we found no conclusions about the effect of hCG priming on live birth, pregnancy, or miscarriage rates in IMV.

The review of trials found that both ERT and HRT do not prevent cognitive impairment in older postmenopausal women when given as short term or longer term (up to five years) therapy. However, it is not known whether either specific types of ERT or HRT have specific effects in subgroups of women, but there was evidence that combined hormone therapy in similar age women was associated with a decrement in a number of verbal memory tests and a small improvement in a test of figural memory. There is insufficient evidence to determine whether subgroups of women using specific types of hormone therapy could benefit from treatment. In the meantime, based on the available evidence, ERT or HRT can not be recommended for old women with cognitive decline.

We included two studies with 880 participants. We found one ongoing trial with planned recruitment of 80 participants. The studies lasted 12 weeks. The review included four short-term trials (169 participants) that compared the effectiveness of SRIs and CBT in treating BDD. The results of these trials need to be replicated. In addition, future controlled trials in other samples, such as adolescents, and using other selective SRIs, as well as a range of psychological therapy approaches and modalities (alone and in combination), are essential in supplementing the sparse data currently available.

We found three randomised controlled trials involving 268 adults. The studies showed that cotrimoxazole desensitization was better than cotrimoxazole rechallenge for preventing treatment discontinuation and reducing the risk of opportunistic infections. No severe hypersensitivity reactions were reported for any of the two methods.

We included 12 trials involving 767 participants in this review. There was no evidence of benefit for antibiotics in other types of bacterial disease, but there was a higher number of negative stool cultures in people who received antibiotic treatment for NTS. The quality of the evidence was very low.

We included 23 studies (n = 4192) comparing the accuracy of IL-6 for the diagnosis of sepsis in critically ill adults. The included participants were heterogeneous in terms of their distribution of age, male, main diagnosis, setting, country, positiveness threshold, sepsis criteria, year of publication, and origin of infection, among other factors. Prevalence of sepsis significantly varied between studies, ranging from 12% to 78%. We found that IL-6 is a effective test for the diagnosis of sepsis in adults. However, the evidence is not clear whether IL-6 is effective for the treatment of a group of adults with a high risk of sepsis. Further studies about the accuracy of IL-6 for the diagnosis of sepsis in adults that use rigorous methods for conducting diagnostic test accuracy studies are needed. The conclusions of the review will likely change after the 20 studies are fully published and evaluated.

We included two randomised controlled trials. One trial compared oral 100 microgram (g) selenium yeast tablets with placebo, taken from the first trimester to birth. The trial randomised 179 women but outcome data were only provided for 85 women. Eighty-three women were randomised to each arm of the trial. Sixty-one women completed the selenium arm, 44 of which completed an Edinburgh Postnatal depression scale (EPDS). In the placebo arm, 64 women completed the trial, 41 of which completed an EPDS. This included study found selenium had an effect on EPDS scores but did not reach statistical significance. There was a total of 153 women who received selenium and 129 women who received placebo. The other trial compared docosahexanoic acid (DHA) and eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) with placebo. The trial randomised 126 women at risk of postpartum depression to three arms: 42 were allocated to EPA, 42 to DHA, and 42 to placebo. Three women in the EPA arm, four in the DHA arm and one woman in the placebo arm were lost to follow-up. Women who were found to have major depressive disorder, bipolar disorder, current drug abuse or dependence, suicidal ideation or schizophrenia at recruitment were excluded from the study. The women who discontinued the intervention (five in the EPA arm, four in the DHA arm and seven in the placebo arm) were included in the intention-to-treat analysis, while those who were lost to follow-up were not. Women received supplements or placebo from recruitment at a gestational age of 12 to 20 weeks until their last review visit six to eight weeks postpartum. The primary outcome measure was the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) score at the five-week follow-up. The primary outcomes were the BDI score at the five-week follow-up. Women received supplements or placebo from recruitment at a gestational age of 12 to 20 weeks until their last review visit six to eight weeks postpartum. The primary outcome measure was the BDI score at the five-week follow-up. The results of this review are based on two randomised controlled trials.

We included 37 studies with a total of 3110 participants. Most studies commenced treatment immediately on admission to hospital or after craniotomys and all treatment was maintained for at least 24 hours. The evidence is current to January 2017. The studies included both adults and children with TBI. Most studies commenced treatment immediately on admission to hospital or after craniotomys and all treatment was maintained for at least 24 hours. The results for the outcomes reported were inconclusive, with differences in the direction of effect. We did not combine the data for these outcomes for meta-analysis. We considered duration of hypothermia therapy and the length of follow-up in collected data for these subgroups; differences in study data remained so that we did not perform meta-analysis. The studies were generally poorly reported and we were unable to assess risk of bias adequately. Inconsistency in results may be explained by heterogeneity between study participants or bias introduced by individual study methods but we did not explore this in detail in subgroup or sensitivity analyses. We downgraded the quality of the evidence for mortality and unfavourable outcome to very low. We downgraded the evidence for the pneumonia outcome to low.

We found three studies, involving 519 people with depression. The studies were very different in terms of interventions, participants, and measuring tools. The evidence is current to January 2016. The evidence for the effectiveness of family therapy for depression did not exceed level 3 (limited or conflicting evidence), but there was moderate evidence (level 2) from three studies, indicating that family therapy is more effective than no treatment or waiting list condition on decreasing depression, and on increasing family functioning. The current evidence base is too heterogeneous and sparse to draw conclusions on the overall effectiveness of family therapy in the treatment of depression. At this point, use of psychological interventions for which there is already an evidence-base would seem to be preferable to family therapy. Further high quality trials examining the effectiveness and comparative effectiveness of explicitly defined forms of family therapy are required.

The evidence is current to January 2016. We found seven randomised controlled trials (RCTs) involving 960 participants. The quality of the studies was generally low, with several studies at risk of selection bias, and no studies used blinding during treatment or outcome assessment. There was a high level of statistical variation between the studies, which therefore reduces the quality of the evidence. The results showed that insertion of a drain after axillary lymphadenectomy reduced the risk of developing a seroma and reduced the number of post-operative seroma aspirations. This benefit should be balanced against an increased length of hospital stay in the drained population.

The review included eight studies with 390,769 participants. The studies were of high quality, and two were of moderate quality. The two case-control studies were of moderate quality. The results from the studies assessing associations between flavonoid intake, colorectal cancer and adenomas were contradictory. There was no evidence that total flavonoid intake reduced the risk of colorectal neoplasms. The evidence for Isoflavones, Flavonoids, Flavonoides and Flavanones was conflicting. For Flavan-3-ols, the results from two studies suggested that increased intake of Flavan-3-ols reduced the risk of both CRC and colorectal adenomas. A statistically significant reduced risk of CRC was found with high intake of epiatechin. There was moderate quality evidence to support that increased intake of procyanidin and phytoestrogen could reduce the incidence of CRC. There was no evidence that suggested that high anthocyanin intake had an inverse association with colorectal adenoma.

We included seven trials with 1369 participants. All trials had high risk of bias. Five trials used our first definition of slow responders, and three other trials (including one that used both definitions) used the second definition. No of the included trials reported our primary outcomes. However, regarding the secondary outcomes, extension of the treatment period to 72 weeks increased the sustained virological response according to both definitions (71/217 (32.7%) versus 52/194 (26.8%), and 265/499 (53.1%) versus 207/496 (41.7%); RR 1.27, 95% confidence interval (CI) 1.07 to 192, P = 0.02, I2 = 8%); and 265/99 (53.1%) versus 207/496 (41.7%); RR 1.27, 95% CI 1.07 to 150, P = 0.11, I2 = 38%). The end of treatment response was not significant different between the two treatment groups. The number of participants who relapsed virologically was found to be lower in the groups that had been treated for 72 weeks using both definitions (27/84 (32.1%) versus 46/91 (50.5%), 3 trials; and 85/350 (24.3%) versus 146/353 (1.4%)). The length of treatment did not significantly affect the adherence (247/279 (88.5%) versus 252/274 (92.0%). In the single trial that reported adverse events, no significant difference was found between the two treatment groups. This review demonstrates higher a proportion of sustained virological response after extension of treatment from 48 weeks to 72 weeks in HCV genotype 1 infected patients in which HCV RNA was still detectable but decreased by  2 log after 12 weeks and became negative after 24 weeks of treatment, and in patients with detectable HCV RNA after four weeks of treatment.

The review of studies found that the quality of the evidence was low in two studies and unclear or high in the other study. The results of this review suggest that the diagnostic test accuracy of EUS is not effective in assessing the resectionability of pancreatic cancers.
