We included seven studies, with 766 participants. Four studies used intracutaneous injections, two used subcutaneous injections, and one both. All reported on low back pain in labour only. The studies were of good quality, but four studies were at high risk of bias due to small size of treatment groups, incomplete outcome data, and performance bias. All studies reported treatment group average or median scores, finding greater reduction in pain for sterile water. However, failure to demonstrate a normal distribution for pain intensity or relief, and use of different scales, meant meta-analysis was inappropriate. No study reported primary dichotomous efficacy outcomes. One reported the number self-coring 4/10 cm or more reduction in pain; significantly more had this outcome with sterile water (55% to 60%) than with placebo (20% to 25%). There was no significant difference between sterile water and saline for rates of caesarean section, instrumental delivery, rescue analgesia, time of delivery, or Apgar scores. Two studies reported that more women treated with sterile water will require the same analgesia in future. No study reported on women's satisfaction with pain relief, women's sense of control in labour, women's satisfaction with the childbirth experience, mother/baby interaction, rates of breastfeeding, maternal disease, infant long-term outcomes, or cost. No adverse events were reported other than transient pain with injection, which was worse with sterile water. The outcomes reported severely limit conclusions for clinical practice. We found little robust evidence that sterile water is effective for low back or any other labour pain. Neither did we find any difference in delivery or other maternal or fetal outcomes. Further large, methodologically rigorous studies are required to determine the effectiveness of sterile water to relieve pain in labour.

We included 12 studies with a total of 1932 participants in this review. The overall postoperative chronic pain was reduced by 37% in the glue group compared with the suture group. However, the results changed when we conducted subgroup analysis with regard to the type of mesh. Subgroup analysis of included studies using lightweight mesh showed the reduction of chronic pain was less profound and insignificant. Subgroup analysis of included studies using heavyweight mesh resulted in a significant benefit from the fixation with glue (OR 0.38, 95% CI 0.117 to 0.83). Hernia recurrence was similar between the two groups. Fixation with glue was better than suture regarding duration of the operation, haematoma, and recovery time to daily activities. We also investigated adverse events. There were no significant differences between the two groups for superficial wound infection, mesh/deficient infection, and persistent numbness. However, six studies involving 1009 participants reported postoperative length of stay, resulting in non-significant difference between the two groups.

We found 32 studies comparing the effects of technical editing and review studies comparing the effects of review studies on review quality. The evidence is current to January 2015. There is some evidence that the 'package' of technical editing used by biomedical journals does improve papers. However, there is no evidence that the 'package' of technical editing used by biomedical journals is effective in improving paper quality.

We included 15 studies with 721 participants with cancer pain due to different types of cancer. The studies were of adequate methodological quality, but all but one were judged to be at a high risk of bias because of small study size and six because of methods used to manage missing data or high withdrawal rates. Three studies used a parallel group design; the other was cross-over trials in which there was an adequate washesout period, but only one reported results for treatment periods separately. Most studies investigated the effect of a single dose of medication, while five used treatment periods of one, seven or 21 days. Most studies used codeine as a single agent and three combined it with paracetamol. Ten studies included a placebo arm, and 14 included one or more of 16 different active drug comparisons or compared different routes of administration. Most studies investigated the effect of a single dose of medication, but five used treatment periods of one, seven or 21 days. Most studies used codeine at doses of 30 mg to 120 mg. There were insufficient data for any pooled analysis. Only two studies reported our preferred responder outcome of 'participants with at least 50% reduction in pain' and two reported 'participants with no worse than mild pain'. In 11 studies reported treatment group average measures of pain intensity or pain relief; overall for these outcomes measures, codeine or codeine plus paracetamol was numberly better than placebo and equivalent to the active compareators. Side effects reporting was poor: only two studies reported the number of participants with any side effect specified by treatment group and only one reported the number of participants with any serious side effect. In multiple-dose studies nausea, vomiting and constipation were common, with somnolence and dizziness frequent in the 21-day study. Withdrawal from the studies, where reported, was less than 10% except in two studies. There were three deaths, in all cases due to the underlying cancer. We found only a small amount of data in studies that were both randomised and double-blind. Studies were small, of short duration, and most had significant shortcomings in reporting. The available evidence suggests that codeine is more effective than placebo, but with increased risk of nausea, vomiting, and constipation. Uncertainty remains as to the magnitude of the effect.

The review included 12 trials (11 randomized controlled trials and one quasi-randomized trial) of 563 people with HbSS, HbSC or HbSthal, aged six to 35 years old. The interventions ranged from a total of one hour to weekly sessions for eight weeks and the post-intervention assessments ranged from the end of the intervention period to 12 months after completion. The overall risk of bias was low for selective reporting, unclear for random sequence formation, allocation concealment, blinding of participants and blinding of outcome assessment. Insufficient data were reported for patient knowledge, caregiver knowledge, health services use and depression. The evidence is current to January 2016. The quality of the evidence was moderate for patient knowledge, moderate for caregiver knowledge and moderate for depression.

We searched for studies that compared brivaracetam with placebo or no treatment. We found six studies, which included 2411 participants. The studies were of short duration and included adults between 16 and 80 years old. The studies were conducted in the USA, the United States, the United States, and the United States. The studies were conducted in the USA, the United States, the United States, and the United States. The studies were conducted in the USA, the United States, the United States, the United States, and the United States. The results of the review suggest that brivaracetam is effective in reducing seizure frequency and can help patients in achieving seizure freedom. However, brivaracetam is associated with a greater proportion of treatment withdrawals due to adverse events compared with placebo.

We included 38 studies, most from high-income countries, which examined the effects of a 'vaccination communication' intervention on parents' perceptions of vaccine communication. The evidence is current to January 2016. In general, parents needed more information than they were receiving (high confidence in the evidence). Lack of information led to worry and regret about vaccination decisions between some parents (moderate confidence in the evidence). Parents needed balanced information about vaccination benefits and harms (high confidence in the evidence). Parents needed vaccination information to be available at a larger range of locations, including outside health services (low confidence in the evidence) and in good time before each vaccination appointment (moderate confidence in the evidence). Parents viewed health workers as an important source of information and had specific expectations of their interactions with them (high confidence in the evidence). Poor communication and negative relationships with health workers often influenced vaccination decisions (moderate confidence in the evidence). Parents generally found it difficult to know which vaccination information source to trust and difficult to find information they felt was unbiased and balanced (high confidence in the evidence). The number of information parents needed and the sources they felt could be trusted were associated with acceptance of vaccination, with parents who were more unsure wanting more information (low to moderate confidence in the evidence). We have high or moderate confidence in the evidence contributing to several review findings. Further research, especially in rural and low- to middle-income countries settings, may improve our confidence in the evidence for the findings where we had low or very low confidence.

We found 10 trials with a total of 599 participants. The trials tested different psychological therapies and the comparison was poor. The evidence is current to January 2016. The results suggest that treatment as usual (TAU) when delivered by a non-eating-disorder specialist or similar may be less effective than focal psychodynamic therapy. This was suggested for a primary outcome of recovery by achievement of a good or moderate outcome on the Morgan and Russell Scale. However, there were no differences between cognitive analytic therapy and TAU for this outcome, or for body mass index (BMI). There were no differences in overall dropout rates between individual psychological therapies and TAU. Two trials found a non-specific specialist therapy (Specialist Supportive Clinical Management) or an Optimised TAU delivered by therapists with eating disorder knowledge was similar in outcomes to cognitive behaviour therapy. In the context of TAU performing poorly, there was a suggestion in one trial that focal psychodynamic therapy might be better than TAU, but this is in the context of TAU performing poorly. An alternative control condition of dietary advice alone appeared to be unacceptable, but no adverse effects were identified. The quality of the evidence was very low for most of the trials.

The review included 516 participants from three randomised controlled trials. One study was conducted in the USA and included two trials: the first trial randomized 151 adults to receive either silicone oil or sulfur hexafluoride (SF6 gas tamponades); and the second trial randomized 271 adults to receive either silicone oil or perfluropropane (C3F8 gas tamponade). The third trial was a multi-center international trial and randomized 94 participants (age range not specified) to receive heavy silicone oil (a combination of perfluorohexyloctane (F6H8) and silicone oil versus standard silicone oil (either 1000 centistokes or 5000 centistokes, per the surgeon's preference). In participants with RD associated with PVR, outcomes after pars plana vitrectomy and infusion of either silicone oil, perfluropropane gas, or sulfur hexafluoride gas were similar for a broad variety of cases. There were no significant differences between silicone oil and perfluropropane gas in terms of the proportion of participants achieving at least 5/200 visual acuity or achieving macular attachment at a minimum of one year. However, sulfur hexafluoride gas was reported to be associated with significantly worse anatomic and visual outcomes than was silicone oil at one year, but there were no significant differences between silicone oil and sulfur hexafluoride gas in terms of achieving at least 5/200 visual acuity at two years. For macular attachment, participants treated with silicone oil received significantly more favourable outcomes than did participants who received sulfur hexafluoride at both one year and two years. The first two trials did not perform any sample size analysis or power detection. In the third trial, which had a power detection, there was a significant difference between silicone oil and sulfur hexafluoride gas in terms of achieving at least 5/200 visual acuity at two years.

We included five studies (involving 1819 women) in this review. The evidence is current to January 2015. We found that women who received planned early delivery had a lower risk of death and severe disease in the mother, but there were no clear differences between groups. There was no clear difference between groups in the number of babies who were admitted to the hospital or the duration of hospital stay for the mother or the baby. There was no clear difference between groups in the number of infants who were admitted to the hospital or the duration of hospital stay for the baby. There was no clear difference between groups in the number of infants who were admitted to the hospital or the duration of hospital stay for the baby. There was no clear difference between groups in the number of infants who were admitted to the hospital or the duration of hospital stay for the mother or the baby. The evidence is current to February 2016.

The review of studies found that prophylaxis reduces the number of bleeds in children with hemophilia and reduces the number of bleeds in adults. However, the evidence is not enough to confirm the effect of prophylaxis on the number of bleeds in adults.

We included 13 trials involving 1824 participants in this review. However, data in usable format were only available in 10 trials 732 participants. Insufficient data were available to assess the effectiveness of the interventions evaluated in the studies. Eight studies aimed to induce remission; overall survival did not differ significantly between treatment groups. Five studies aimed to maintain remission. In two out of three studies reporting survival, this was significant but the difference was not statistically significant between treatment groups. More aggressive treatment schedules appear to produce similar effects with less adverse event profiles. However, the evidence for the relative effectiveness of interventions to treat BL is not strong as studies were small, underpowered and prone to both systematic and random error.

The review included two studies involving 447 (with sample sizes 14 and 432) RhD negative women. The studies compared IM and intravenous administration of anti-D prophylaxis. In both studies the women received a 1500 IU (300 microgram) dose of Rhophylac during week 28 of gestation. There was no incidence of RhD alloimmunization in either of the studies, as the sample size was insufficient for meaningful comparison of this uncommon outcome. One study found that the average anti-D IgG concentrations after intravenous and IM administration differed up to seven days (36.1 (2.6) g/mL intravenous; 21.8 (8.7) g/mL IM on day seven). However, from two to three weeks post-administration, the concentrations were similar for both routes of administration. No women involved in the studies developed antibodies against the RhD antigen. It appears that IM and intravenous administration of anti-D are equally effective. The number of included studies and the number of participants are not enough to assess whether there are any differences. Anti-D can be administered by IM or intravenous injection. The choice of IM or intravenous route of administration will depend on the available preparations, the dose to be administered and also on the patients' preferences. This review found insufficient information on which to guide practice due to the limited number of included studies, small sample sizes and methodological limitations.

The review included eight studies with a total of 21379 patients with diabetes. Three included studies investigated ticlopidine compared to aspirin or placebo. Five included studies examined clopidogrel compared to aspirin or a combination of aspirin and dipyridamole, or compared clopidogrel in combination with aspirin to aspirin alone. All trials included patients with previous heart disease other than the CHARISMA trial which included patients with multiple risk factors for heart disease. The total duration of follow-up ranged from 365 days to 913 days. Data for diabetes patients on all-cause mortality, vascular mortality and myocardial infarction were only available for one trial (355 patients). This trial compared ticlopidine to placebo and did not demonstrate any statistically significant differences for all-cause death, vascular mortality or myocardial infarction. Diabetes outcome data for stroke were available in three trials (31% of total diabetes participants). Overall pooling of two (statistically heterogeneous) studies showed no statistically significant reduction in the combination of fatal and non-fatal stroke (59/3194 (11.2%) versus 356/3146 (11%)), random effects odds ratio (OR) 0.81; 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.44 to 1.49) for ADP receptor antagonists versus other antiplatelet drugs. There were no data available from any of the trials on peripheral vascular disease, health-related quality of life, adverse events specifically for patients with diabetes, or costs. The available evidence for ADP receptor antagonists in patients with diabetes mellitus is limited and most trials do not report outcomes for patients with diabetes separately. Therefore, recommendations for the use of ADP receptor antagonists for the prevention of heart disease in patients with diabetes are based on available evidence from trials including patients with and without diabetes. Trials with diabetes patients and subgroup analyses of patients with diabetes in trials with combined populations are needed to provide a more robust evidence base to guide clinical management in patients with diabetes.

We included four studies, involving 1190 women. The evidence is current to January 2015. There was no clear effect of induction of labour on the risk of caesarean section or instrumental delivery. There were no clear differences between groups for shoulder dystocia, fracture and asphyxia. There was no clear difference between groups for measures of newborn asphyxia, low five-minute infant Apgar scores (fewer than seven) or low blood pressure in the cord. The total birthweight was lower in the induction group, but there was significant differences between studies for this outcome. In one study with data for 818 women, third- and fourth-degree perineal tears were increased in the induction group. The quality of the evidence was moderate to high for the outcomes of birthweight, asphyxia, asphyxia, asphyxia and asphyxia. The quality of the evidence for other outcomes was low or unclear.

We found 56 randomised clinical trials with 95,286 participants. The age of participants ranged from 18 to 107 years. Most trials included women older than 70 years. The average proportion of women was 77%. Four trials included healthy volunteers, nine trials included postmenopausal women and 35 trials included older people living on their own or in hospital. The remaining eight trials randomly assigned 795 participants with neurological, cardiovascular, respiratory or rheumatoid diseases. Vitamin D was administered for a weighted average of 4.4 years. More than half of the trials had a low risk of bias. All trials were conducted in high-income countries. The total number of participants in the trials ranged from 94,491 to 90,286. Most trials included women older than 70 years. The average proportion of women was 77%. For 40-eight of the trials randomly assigned 94,491 healthy participants. of which four trials included healthy volunteers, nine trials included postmenopausal women and 35 trials included older people living on their own or in hospital. The rest of the trials randomly assigned 795 participants with neurological, cardiovascular, respiratory or rheumatoid diseases. Vitamin D was administered for a weighted average of 4.4 years. More than two-thirds of the trials had a low risk of bias. All trials were conducted in high-income countries. The total number of participants in the trials ranged from 94,491 to 97,286. Most trials included women older than 70 years. The average proportion of women was 77%. For 50-eight of the trials randomly assigned 94,491 healthy participants. of which four trials included healthy participants, nine trials included postmenopausal women and 35 trials included older people living on their own or in hospital. The other eight trials randomly assigned 795 participants with neurological, cardiovascular, respiratory or rheumatoid diseases. Vitamin D was administered for a weighted average of 4.4 years. More than two-thirds of the trials reported the baseline vitamin D status of participants based on serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels. Participants in 19 trials had vitamin D adequacy (at or above 20 ng/mL). Participants in the remaining 26 trials had vitamin D insufficiency (lower than 20 ng/mL). Participants in the remaining 26 trials had vitamin D insufficiency (lower than 20 ng/mL). Vitamin D decreased mortality in all 56 trials analysed together (5,920/47,472 (13.5%) versus 6,077/47,814 (11.7%). More than 8% of participants dropped out. The results of this review suggest that vitamin D may be associated with a significant increase or decrease in mortality.

The review included 21 randomized controlled trials with a total of 296 participants. The results showed that fluoxetine, orlistat, and sibutramine can reduce weight over 12 to 57 weeks. However, the number of side effects was small, and the long-term health benefits are unclear. The safety of sibutramine is uncertain. There is a paucity of data on other drugs for weight loss or control in people with type 2 diabetes.

We searched for studies of a total of 734 participants. We found five studies involving a total of 734 participants. The studies were of low quality and were not well conducted. The results showed no significant differences between VGB and CBZ in terms of time to treatment withdrawal and time to achieve six-month remission after dose stabilisation from randomisation, but results did show a disadvantage for VGB on time to first seizure after randomisation. Compared with CBZ, VGB was associated with more occurrences of weight gain and fewer occurrences of skin rash and drowsiness. No differences in visual field defects or visual disturbances were noted. The results of this review are not sufficient to assess the risk-benefit balance of VGB versus CBZ monotherapy for epilepsy.

The studies were conducted in the USA, the USA, the USA, and the USA. The studies included a total of 600 participants with multiple myeloma or non-Hodgkin lymphoma. We included 23 trials involving 1806 women, of which 717 received cones. The trials were small and in many the quality was difficult to judge. The results were difficult to combine. Some trials reported high drop-out rates with both cone and comparison treatments. Seven trials were published only as abstracts. The evidence is current to January 2015. The trials showed that weighted vaginal cones are better than no active treatment in women with SUI and may be of similar effectiveness to PFMT and electrostimulation. This conclusion should remain uncertain until larger, high-quality trials, that use similar and relevant outcomes, are completed.

We searched for studies that compared the effects of education, decision aids, and self-monitoring plus education on TTR in AF patients receiving oat. We found 11 studies with a total of 2246 AF patients (ranging from 14 to 712 by study). The evidence is current to January 2016. We found that the effect of self-monitoring plus education on TTR was uncertain compared with usual care. We found small but positive effects of education on anxiety and depression compared with usual care. The effect of decision aids on decision conflict favoured usual care. The quality of the evidence was very low for the effect of self-monitoring plus education on TTR. The quality of the evidence for the effect of education on anxiety and depression was very low.

The review of trials found that PGE2 may increase the chance of vaginal delivery in 24 hours, it may increase the risk of uterine hyperstimulation with fetal heart rate changes, and it may reduce the risk of caesarean section rates. However, these effects are not likely to be significant. The review of trials found that PGE2 tablets, gels and pessary (including sustained release preparations) are as effective as each other, any differences between formulations are moderate but may be important.

The review of trials found that cot-nursing with warming of the nursery was similar to incubator care in terms of temperature control and weight gain. However, the results of the trial that included 38 infants showed that the weight gain was smaller in the cot-nursing group. The review of trials found that cot-nursing with warming of the nursery was similar to incubator care in terms of temperature control and weight gain.

We included three studies, involving 146 participants. Two studies were assessed as being at high risk of bias. The main finding of the review was that the two techniques may be equally effective at exposing PDCs (low- to very low-quality evidence). One surgery failure was due to detachment of the gold chain (closed group). One study reported on complications following surgery and found two in the closed group: a post-operative infection requiring antibiotics and pain during alignment of the canine as the gold chain penetrated through the gum tissue of the palate. We were unable to combine data for dental aesthetics, patient-reported pain and discomfort, periodontal health and treatment time; however, individual studies did not find any differences between the surgical methods. Currently, the evidence suggests that neither the open or closed surgical technique for exposing palatally displaced maxillary canine teeth is better for any of the outcomes included in this review; but we considered the evidence to be low quality, with two of the three included studies being at high risk of bias. This suggests the need for more high-quality studies. Three ongoing clinical trials have been identified and it is hoped that these will produce data that can be pooled to increase the degree of certainty in these findings.

We included three studies, involving 244 women. The studies were considered to be at high risk of bias. The protaglandins used were PG E2 analogue (sulprostone) in 50 participants and PG E1 analogue (misoprostol) in 194 participants at a dose of 250 mcg and 800 mcg respectively. The protaglandins compared with placebo were not better in reducing the rate of manual removal of placenta, severe postpartum haemorrhage, need for blood transfusion, total blood loss and the average time from injection to placental removal (minutes). Side-effects were no different between groups (vomiting, headache, pain and nausea between injection and discharge from the labour ward), with the exception of shivering, which was more frequent in women receiving protaglandins. We did not receive any data for the primary outcomes of maternal mortality and the need to add another therapeutic uterotonic. Currently there is limited, very low-quality evidence relating to the effectiveness and the safety using protaglandins for the management of retained placenta. The use of protaglandins resulted in less need for manual removal of placenta, severe postpartum haemorrhage and blood transfusion but no of the differences reached statistical significance. Many larger, adequately powered studies are needed to confirm that these clinically important beneficial effects are not only chance findings. Moreover, no differences were found between protaglandins and placebo in total blood loss or the average time from injection to placental removal (minutes) or side-effects (vomiting).

We included six studies (157 participants) in this review. The studies were small and/or had limited follow-up times. The studies showed that foam dressings did not improve the healing of diabetic foot ulcers compared with basic wound contact dressings. The studies found no difference in the number of diabetes foot ulcers healed when foam dressings were compared with hydrocolic (matrix) dressings. The studies were small and/or had limited follow-up time. Currently there is no research evidence to suggest that foam wound dressings are more effective in healing foot ulcers in people with diabetes than other types of dressing but all trials in this area are very small. Decision makers may wish to consider aspects such as dressing cost and the wound management properties provided by each dressing type e.g. exudate management.

We found three trials that compared surgery with endoscopy. Two trials compared endoscopy with surgical intervention and included a total of 111 participants: 55 in the endoscopic group and 56 in the surgical group. The surgical group had a higher proportion of participants with pain relief, both at middle/long-term follow-up (two to five years) and long-term follow-up ( five years). Surgical intervention resulted in improved quality of life and improved preservation of exocrine pancreatic function at middle/long-term follow-up (two to five years), but not at long-term follow-up ( 5 years). No differences were found in terms of major post-interventional complications or mortality, although the number of participants did not allow for this to be reliably evaluated. One trial, including 32 participants, compared surgical intervention with conservative treatment: 17 in the surgical group and 15 in the conservative group. The trial showed that surgical intervention resulted in a higher percentage of participants with pain relief and better preservation of pancreatic function. The trial had methodological limitations, and the number of participants assessed in the present evidence was very small.

We found only one randomised controlled trial that included 857 patients undergoing minor skin excision surgery in the primary care setting. The wounds were sutured after the excision. Patient undergoing early post-operative bathing (dressing to be removed after 12 hours and normal bathing restarted) or delayed post-operative bathing (dressing to be held for at least 48 hours before removal and resumption of normal bathing) were compared with patient undergoing delayed post-operative bathing (dressing to be retained for at least 48 hours after removal and resumption of normal bathing). The results of this trial showed no significant difference in the proportion of patients who developed surgical site infection (SSI). The proportions of patients who developed SSIs were 8.5% in the early bathing group and 8.8% in the delayed bathing group. There is currently no conclusive evidence available from randomised controlled trials regarding the benefits or harms of early versus delayed post-operative showering or bathing for the prevention of wound complications, as the confidence intervals around the point estimate are wide, and, therefore, a clinically significant increase or decrease in SSI by early post-operative bathing can not be ruled out. We recommend running further randomised controlled trials to compare early versus delayed post-operative showering or bathing.

We found one randomised controlled trial (RCT) comparing prednisone with no treatment in 35 participants. The trial did not report side effects in detail, but one prednisone-treated participant died. A double-blind RCT comparing daily standard-dose oral prednisolone with monthly high-dose oral dexamethasone in 40 participants reported no of the prespecific outcomes for this review. The trial had a low risk of bias, but the quality of evidence was limited as it was from a single small study. There was little or no difference in number of participants who received remission or change in disability or impairment after one year. Change of grip strength or Medical Research Council (MRC) scores showed little or no difference between groups. Eight of 16 people in the prednisolone group and seven of 24 people in the dexamethasone group deteriorated. Side effects were similar with each regimen, but the side effects were more common with monthly dexamethasone. We are very uncertain about the effects of oral prednisone compared with no treatment, because the quality of evidence from the only RCT that is available is very low. Nevertheless, corticosteroids are commonly used in practice, supported by very low-quality evidence from observational studies. We also know from observational studies that corticosteroids have the long-term use of the drug.

We found six studies with a total of 2100 participants. The studies were conducted in the USA, the UK, the USA, the USA and the USA. The studies included people with asthma taking regular medications and included people with COPD or severe asthma. One study compared the two types of check-up for oral steroid tapering in severe refractory asthma. The studies were not able to be blinded and the number of people who had a remote check-up was very high. We could not say whether more people who had a remote check-up needed oral corticosteroids for an asthma exacerbation than those who were seen face-to-face. In the face-to-face check-up groups, 21 participants out of 1000 had exacerbations that required oral steroids over three months, compared to 36 (95% CI nine to 139) out of 1000 for the remote check-up group. Exacerbations that needed treatment in the Emergency Department (ED), hospital admission or an unscheduled health care visit all happened too infrequently to detect whether remote check-ups are a safe alternative to face-to-face consultations. The larger implementation study that compared two general practice populations showed that providing telephone check-ups and proactively phoning participants increased the number of people with asthma who received the review.

The review included three studies with a total of 212 participants. The studies were conducted in the USA, the USA, the UK and the USA. The studies were of low quality and were not well conducted. The results suggest that the outcome measures all favoured the exercise therapy but no were statistically significant. No of the studies reported negative effects of the exercise therapy. The review found no clinically important or statistically significant evidence that exercise therapy can improve functional ability, quality of life, aerobic capacity or pain. The low number of available RCTs limits the generalisability. The included and excluded studies were all consistent about the adverse effects of exercise therapy; no short-term adverse effects of exercise therapy were found in any study. Both included and excluded studies showed that exercise does not exacerbate arthritis.

We included nine studies with a total of 1414 participants. The studies were conducted in the United States, Australia, Africa, and Africa. The studies were of low quality and were of small number of participants. The studies did not report on the effect of whole grain diets on total heart disease or heart attack or death. The studies reported the effect of whole grain diets on risk factors for heart disease including blood lipids and blood pressure. The studies were at unclear or high risk of bias with small sample sizes and very short-term interventions. The evidence is current to January 2015.

We searched for studies that compared the use of an air-stirrup (a treatment in which the lung is resuscitated) with an orthosis or a walking cast. We found 30 studies with a total of 1896 participants. The evidence is current to January 2015. Three small studies investigated rehabilitation interventions during the immobilisation period after conservative orthopaedic management. There was limited evidence from two studies (106 participants in total) of short-term benefit of using an air-stirrup versus an orthosis or a walking cast. One study (12 participants) found 12 weeks of hypnosis did not reduce activity or improve other outcomes. Thirty studies examined rehabilitation interventions during the amyosis period after surgery. In 10 studies, the use of a removable type of amyosis combined with exercise was compared with cast amyosis alone. Using a removable type of amyosis to allow controlled exercise significantly reduced activity limitation in five of the eight studies reporting this outcome, reduced pain and improved ankle dorsiflexion range of motion. However, it also led to a higher rate of mainly minor adverse events. During the amyosis period after surgical fixation, starting weight-bearing made a small improvement in ankle dorsiflexion range of motion. Evidence from one small but likely biased study (60 participants) showed that neurostimulation, an electrotherapy method, may be beneficial in the short-term. There was little and inconclusive evidence on which type of support or amyosis was the best. One study found no amyosis improved ankle dorsiflexion and plantarflexion range of motion compared with cast.

The review of trials found that a home-based nursing programme has the potential to improve ART adherence, but more evidence is needed. Medication diary interventions do not show an effect on adherence or disease outcomes. Two interventions, an lopinavir-ritonavir regimen and peer support therapy for adolescents, did not demonstrate improvement in adherence, but showed greater viral load suppression compared to control groups, suggesting a different mechanism for improved health outcomes.

The review of studies found that fenoprofen 200 mg is effective at treating moderate to severe acute postoperative pain. However, the evidence is limited and the results are not clear about the effectiveness of other doses or active comparisons.

The review of trials found that quinolone antibiotics are better than no drug treatment or topical antiseptics at reducing the risk of aural discharge. However, the evidence is insufficient to suggest any difference between quinolone and non-quinolone antibiotics in reducing the risk of aural discharge.

We included 21 studies with a total of 6253 participants (5515 were included in the analysis). Studies were conducted from 1974 to 2011, with 80% of the studies conducted in the 1970's, 1980's or 1990's. Most studies did not report study methods sufficiently and many had high applicability concerns. In 20 studies, FRS identified schizophrenia from all other diagnosis, and a specificity of 81.4% (705% to 87.1%). In seven studies, FRS identified schizophrenia from non-psychotic mental health disorders, and a specificity of 94.1% (89% to 97.2%). In 16 studies, FRS identified schizophrenia from other types of psychosis. The synthesis of old studies of limited quality in this review suggests that FRS correctly identifies people with schizophrenia 75% to 95% of the time. The use of FRS to diagnose schizophrenia in triage will incorrectly diagnose about five to 19 people in every 100 who have FRS as having schizophrenia and professionals will not agree with this diagnosis. These people will still need specialist assessment and help due to the severity of disturbance in their behaviour and mental state. However, with a sensitivity of 57%, reliance on FRS to diagnose about 40% of people that specialists will consider to have schizophrenia will not correctly diagnose about 40% of people that professionals will consider to have schizophrenia. Some of these people may experience a delay in getting appropriate treatment. Other people, who professionals will consider to have schizophrenia, could be prematurely discharged from care, if triage based on the presence of FRS to identify schizophrenia. We hope that newer tests - to be included in future Cochrane reviews - will show better results. However, symptoms of first rate can still be useful where newer tests are not available - a situation which applies to the initial screening.

The review of trials found that HBOT is associated with improved outcome for people with LRTI affecting tissues of the head, neck, anus and rectum. HBOT also appears to reduce the risk of LRTI after tooth extraction in an irradiated field. There was no evidence of any important clinical effect on neurological tissues. The use of HBOT to selected participants and tissues may be justified. Further research is needed to establish the optimum participant selection and time of any therapy. An economic evaluation should be undertaken.

We found 10 studies involving 2961 participants that compared the use of blunt needles to the use of sharp needles. The studies were conducted in the USA, the USA, the UK, the USA and the USA. The studies compared the use of blunt needles to the use of sharp needles for a range of surgery. The studies compared the use of blunt needles to the use of sharp needles for a range of surgery. The studies compared the use of blunt needles to the use of sharp needles for a range of surgery. The evidence is current to January 2016. The use of blunt needles reduces the risk of a glove perforation in three surgery events. The use of blunt needles will also prevent one glove perforation in every six surgery events. The use of blunt needles will also reduce the number of needle stick injuries in four surgery events. The quality of the evidence was rated as high. There is high quality evidence that the use of blunt needles areciably reduces the risk of exposure to blood and body fluids for surgeons and their assistants over a range of surgery events. It is likely that future research will change this conclusion.

The review included seven randomised trials involving 422 participants that compared trifluoperazine with low-potency antipsychotic drugs. The size of the included studies was between 20 and 157 participants with a study length between four and 52 weeks. Overall, sequence development, allocation methods and blinding were poorly reported. The results did not show a difference in efficacy between trifluoperazine and low-potency antipsychotics. Trifluoperazine produced more movement disorders. The number of people who had at least one adverse effect was significantly more frequent in the trifluoperazine group. The quality of the evidence was moderate to very low.

We found nine randomised controlled trials (RCTs) involving 593 infants in total. The trials were generally small and included different methods, including lack of blinding and incomplete assessment of all randomised participants. The evidence is current to January 2016. The trials showed that preterm infants fed in response to feeding and satiation cues achieve full oral feeding earlier than infants fed prescribed volume at scheduled intervals. This finding should be interpreted cautiously because of methodological problems in the included trials. A large RCT will be needed to confirm this finding and to determine if responsive feeding of preterm infants affects other important outcomes.

The review of trials found that folic acid and 5-methyltetrahydrofolate were effective in improving the ankle brachial index (ABI) in people with peripheral arterial disease. However, the studies did not report on mortality or rate of limb loss.

We searched for studies that compared the effects of financial incentives on clinical activity in primary care dentists. We found two studies, with data from 503 dental practices, including 821 dentists and 4771 patients. The studies were conducted in the four most deprived areas of Scotland, so the applicability of the findings to other settings may be limited. The second study used a parallel group design conducted over a three-year period to compare the effect of capitation payments with fee-for-service payments on primary care dentists' clinical activity. The study reported on measures of clinical activity (mean percentage of children receiving active preventive advice, health service utilisation (mean number of visits), patient outcomes (mean number of filled teeth, mean percentage of children having one or more teeth extracted and the mean number of decayed teeth) and healthcare costs (mean costs). Teeth were restored at a later stage in the disease process in the capitation system and the clinicians tended to see their patients less frequently and tended to carry out fewer fillings and extractions, but also tended to give more preventive advice. There was insufficient information about the cost-effectiveness of the different remuneration methods.

We included 21 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) involving over 17,000 women and their babies. One trial did not provide data. The trials were generally at low risk of bias. Zinc supplementation resulted in a small reduction in preterm birth (risk ratio (RR) 0.86, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.76 to 0.97 in 16 RCTs; 16 trials of 7637 women). This was not accompanied by a similar reduction in numbers of babies with low birthweight (RR 0.93, 95% CI 0.78 to 1.12 in 14 trials of 5643 women). No clear differences were found between the zinc and no zinc groups for any of the other primary maternal or newborn outcomes, other than induction of labour in a single trial. No difference was found in the subgroups of women with low versus normal zinc and nutrition levels or in women who complied with their treatment versus those who did not. The quality of the evidence was moderate for preterm birth, small-for-gestational age, and low birthweight, and low for stillbirth or neonatal death and birthweight.

The trials compared probiotics with conventional treatment and compared probiotics with antifungal drugs. We included seven trials (involving 696 women) in this update of the review. The included trials were conducted in different countries, covering the full spectrum of the World Bank's economic classification, which improves the applicability of evidence drawn from this review. Two trials were conducted in Germany and Italy which are high-income countries, while four trials were conducted in upper-middle income countries; two in Iran, one in Malaysia and the fourth in Turkey, and the seven trial was conducted in Jordan, which is a lower-middle income country. In six trials all the participants met the inclusion criteria and in the seven trial, we included in the meta-analysis only the subgroup of participants who met the inclusion criteria. We assessed the quality of the evidence between very low to moderate. Downgraded evidence was based on the high risk of bias in six of the seven included trials and a small number of events and large confidence intervals for some outcomes. Treatment of miscarriage with progestogens compared to placebo or no treatment probably reduces the risk of miscarriage; (risk ratio (RR) 0.64, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.47 to 0.87); 7 trials, 696 women; moderate-quality evidence. Treatment with oral progestogen compared to no treatment also likely reduces the miscarriage rate; but treatment with vaginal progesterone compared to placebo may have little or no effect in reducing the miscarriage rate; (risk ratio (RR) 0.57, 95% CI 0.38 to 0.85; 3 trials, 408 women; moderate-quality evidence). However treatment with vaginal progesterone compared to placebo, probably has little or no effect in reducing the miscarriage rate; (risk ratio (RR) 0.75, 95% CI 0.46 to 1.21; 4 trials, 288 women; low-quality evidence). The subgroup interaction test showed no difference between the oral and vaginal subgroups of progesterone. We found five studies that compared laser photocoagulation with no treatment (or deferred) or with a laser treatment. The studies included a total of 4786 people (9503 eyes). The majority of participants in four of these trials were people with proliferative diabetes; one trial recruited mainly people with non-proliferative diabetes. Four of the studies evaluated panretinal photocoagulation with argon laser and one study investigated selective photocoagulation of non-perfusion areas. Three studies compared laser treatment to no treatment and two studies compared laser treatment to deferred laser treatment. All studies were at risk of bias because the treatment and control were different and no study attempted to produce a sham treatment. Three studies were considered to be at risk of attrition bias. The quality of the evidence was low for the risk of loss of 15 or more letters of visual acuity, moderate for the risk of severe visual loss and low for the risk of a sham treatment. The quality of the evidence was moderate for the risk of progression of diabetic retinopathy and low for the risk of vitreous haemorrhage.

We included two trials involving 269 participants. The participants were most often men (67%); the average age was 65 years. The trials were conducted in China and Italy (one was a multicentre trial). Both trials included adults with acute breathing failure after upper abdominal surgery. We judged both trials at high risk of bias. The results suggest that CPAP or bilevel NPPV may reduce the rate of tracheal intubation, and reduce the length of stay in the intensive care unit. We found no differences for mortality or hospital length of stay. There was insufficient evidence to be certain that CPAP or bilevel NPPV had an effect on anastomotic leakage, lung infection, and sepsis or infections. The results from one trial of 60 participants suggested that bilevel NPPV, compared to oxygen therapy, may improve blood gas levels and blood pH one hour after the intervention. The studies did not present data on the outcomes that we intended to assess: gastric insufflation, fistulae, lung infection, bleeding, skin failure, eye irritation, sinus congestion, oronasal drying, and patient-ventilator asynchrony.

We included 15 studies with a total of 1048 participants. Most of the studies were conducted in India, followed by Europe and the United States. The majority of participants were adults of both sexs with mild to moderate asthma for six months to more than 23 years. Five studies included yoga breathing alone, and the other studies evaluated yoga interventions that included breathing, posture, and meditation. The intervention lasted from two weeks to 54 months, for no more than six months in the most of the studies. The risk of bias was low across all domains in one study and unclear or high in at least one domain for the rest. There was some evidence that yoga may improve quality of life, improve symptoms, and reduce medication use in people with asthma. The number of people who had improved symptoms was not significant, but the number of people who had improved symptoms was uncertain. The effects of yoga on change from baseline forced expiratory volume in one second were not statistically significant. Two studies reported improved asthma control, but due to very significant heterogeneity (I2 = 98%) we did not pool data. No serious adverse events associated with yoga were reported, but the data on this outcome was limited. We found moderate-quality evidence that yoga probably leads to small improvements in quality of life and symptoms in people with asthma. There is more uncertainty about potential adverse effects of yoga and its effect on lung function and medication use. The evidence is current to January 2016.

We searched for studies that compared different treatments for a group of people with cancer. We found 10 trials (249 participants) that compared different treatment regimens. The trials were conducted in the USA, the USA, and the USA. The trials compared different treatments with different effects. The evidence is current to January 2015. Two trials of interferon beta-1a (n = 58) and one trial of methotrexate (n = 44) reported no important difference in normalised manual muscle strength sum scores from baseline to six months. A single trial of methotrexate (n = 44) reported moderate-quality evidence that MTX did not arrest or slow disease progression. No of the five trials that were published were well-designed or well-designed. Three trials (n = 78) compared intravenous immunoglobulin (combined in one trial with prednisone) to a placebo, but we were unable to perform meta-analysis because of differences in study analysis and presentation of trial data, with no access to the primary data for re-analysis. An open trial of anti-T lymphocyte immunoglobulin (ATG) combined with MTX versus MTX provided very low-quality evidence in favour of the combined therapy, based on percentage change in quantitative muscle strength sum scores at 12 months. Data from trials of oxandrolone versus placebo, azathioprine (AZA) combined with MTX versus MTX, and arimoclomol versus placebo did not allow us to report either normalised or percentage change in muscle strength sum scores. A total analysis of the effects of arimoclomol is pending data publication. Studies of simvastatin and bimagrumab (BYM338) are ongoing.

We included nine randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with 3144 participants. The evidence is current to January 2016. The results of this review suggest that linezolid may be more effective than vancomycin for treating people with SSTIs, including SSTIs caused by MRSA. The evidence is at high risk of bias and is based on studies that are supported by the pharmaceutical company that makes linezolid. Further well-designed, independently-funded studies are needed.

We included eight randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with a total of 512 participants. The evidence is current to January 2016. The evidence is current to February 2015. The results suggest no significant differences in mortality, morbidity, and survival between the two surgery, but some perioperative outcomes significantly favour the PPW procedure.

We searched for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) involving 1862 participants. We found six studies involving 1862 participants. The results of the studies showed that calcium channel blockers were effective in reducing the risk of death and severe disability in patients with acute traumatic head injury. However, the number of adverse events was higher in the treatment group. The results of the studies showed that nimodipine was effective in reducing the risk of death in patients with subarachnoid haemorrhage. However, the number of adverse reactions was higher in the treatment group.

The review included 15 trials. Three trials were eligible for inclusion. Ten trials involving eight different comparison groups have been included. Only one trial reported live birth rates. The number of oocytes retrieved were significantly less in the conventional GnRHa long protocol compared to stop protocol and GnRH antagonist protocol. Total dose of gonadotrophins used was significantly higher in the GnRHa long protocol group compared to the Stop protocol and GnRH antagonist groups. Weirdness rates were significantly higher in the GnRHa flare up group compared to the GnRH long protocol group. No study reported a difference in the miscarriage or ectopic pregnancy rates. There is insufficient evidence to support the routine use of any particular intervention either for pituitary down regulation, ovarian stimulation or adjuvant therapy in the management of poor responders to controlled ovarian stimulation in IVF. More robust data from good quality RCTs with relevant outcomes are needed.

We found 53 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that compared family intervention with no treatment. The results of this review suggest that family intervention may reduce the number of relapse events and hospital admissions, but the results of these trials may be overestimated due to the poor methodological quality of the studies. Further data from trials that describe the methods of randomisation, test the blindness of the study evaluators, and implement the CNS guidelines would allow greater confidence in these findings.

We found three trials that compared debols with uncoated balloon angioplasty. The trials were conducted in Germany and Austria and used paclitaxel as the agent in the drug-elutig balloons. Two of the three trials were industry funded. Two companies produced the drug-elutig balloons (Eroscor, Bonn, Germany; Medtronic, Fridley, Minnesota, USA). The trials examined both anatomical and clinical endpoints. We found no difference in the incidence of amputation, target lesion revascularization, binary restenosis, death, or improvement of one or more Rutherford groups at six and 12 months. The trials showed no clear differences in death between debols and uncoated balloon angiectomy. The evidence is current to January 2016.

We found 11 trials (with 753 participants) that compared supplementary oxygen to room air or no oxygen. The evidence is current to January 2016. The evidence is current to February 2015. The low quality of the evidence showed no significant differences in average Apgar scores at one minute and at five minutes. No of the 11 trials reported maternal deaturation. The very low quality of the evidence showed that in comparison to room air, women in labour receiving supplementary oxygen had higher maternal oxygen saturation, maternal PaO2 (oxygen pressure in the blood), UaPO2 (foetalumbilical venous blood) and UvPO2 (foetalumbilical venous blood). There was high heterogeneity between these outcomes. A subgroup analysis showed no significant difference in UaPO2 between the two intervention groups in low-risk studies, whereas the high-risk studies showed a benefit for the newborn oxygen group. Overall, we found no convincing evidence that giving supplementary oxygen to healthy term pregnant women during elective caesarean section under regional anaesthesia is either beneficial or harmful for either the mother or the foetus' short-term clinical outcome as assessed by Apgar scores. However, there were significant higher maternal and infant blood gas levels and markers of free radicals when extra oxygen was given, but the results should be interpreted with caution due to the low grade quality of the evidence.

We included 12 studies involving 2196 participants. The studies were conducted in the USA, the USA, the USA, and the USA. The studies compared the effect of reminder packaging on the number of pills taken, the number of patients who had been able to take their pills, and the number of patients who had not been able to take their pills. We found that reminder packing increased the number of pills taken, but the effect was not significant. We found that reminder packaging reduced blood pressure and glycated haemoglobin levels. We found no evidence of an effect on the number of patients who had been able to take their pills. We found that the presence of a reminder packaging aid was preferred by patients with low literacy levels.

We found 15 randomised trials comparing 11 different drugs. The trials were of high risk of bias, and the number of participants was small. The trials did not show any significant differences between the groups in mortality, liver failure, or perioperative morbidity. The trimetazidine group had a longer hospital stay than control. There were no significant differences in any of the clinically relevant outcomes in the other comparisons. The use of these drugs should be restricted to well-designed randomised clinical trials before implementing them in clinical practice.

The review included two trials (182 participants) and two phytomedicines, Niprisan® (a bacterial drug) and Ciklvit+ (a plant drug). The trial showed that Niprisan+ was effective in reducing episodes of severe pain in people with sickle cell disease. However, it did not affect the risk of severe complications or the level of anaemia. No serious adverse effects were reported. The single trial of Cajanus cajan (Ciklvit+) reported a possible benefit to individuals with pain problems, but the results of this trial were not significant. The evidence is current to January 2015.

We found three studies involving 1999 participants. Two studies compared standard treatment (chemotherapy plus radiotherapy) with PET-adapted therapy (chemotherapy only). The other two studies compared no additional radiotherapy (PET-adapted therapy) versus radiotherapy (standard therapy). The study design of the third study was more complex. Participants with early-stage HL were divided into those with a favourable or unfavourable prognosis. They were then randomised to receive PET-adapted or standard treatment. Following a PET scan, participants were further divided into PET-positive and PET-negative groups. To date, data have been published for the PET-negative arms only, making it possible to perform a meta-analysis including all three studies. Of the 1999 participants included in the three studies only 1480 were analysed. The 519 excluded participants were either PET-positive, or were excluded because they did not match the inclusion criteria. One study reported no deaths. The other two studies reported two deaths in participants receiving PET-adapted therapy and two in participants receiving standard therapy. Progression-free survival was shorter in participants with PET-adapted therapy (without radiotherapy) than in those receiving standard treatment with radiotherapy. This difference was also apparent in comparisons of participants receiving no additional radiotherapy (PET-adapted therapy) versus radiotherapy (standard therapy). Short-term adverse events only were assessed in one study, which showed no evidence of a difference between the treatment arms. The quality of the evidence was moderate.

The review included nine studies with 260 participants. Six studies compared exercise therapy to no exercise therapy, and three studies compared two interventions that both met our definition of exercise therapy. The results of the present review suggest that exercise therapy can be beneficial for patients with MS not experiencing an exacerbation. There is an urgent need for consensus on a core set of outcome measures to be used in exercise trials. In addition, these studies should experimentally control for 'dose' of treatment, type of MS and should include sufficient contrast between experimental and control groups.

The review of trials found that people who had a cholecystectomy in the early period of time were more likely to have a serious adverse event than people who had a cholecystectomy in the delayed time period. The trial did not report quality of life or return to work. There was no significant difference in the proportion of people who required conversion. The trial did not report quality of life or return to work. There was no significant difference in the proportion of people who needed conversion.

We found 11 studies that compared long-term antibiotic prophylaxis with short-term antibiotic prophylaxis. The evidence is current to January 2015. The evidence is current to February 2016. The evidence is current to May 2014. The results of this review suggest that long-term antibiotic prophylaxis may reduce the risk of SSI in people undergoing orthognathic surgery. There is uncertainty about whether short-term antibiotic prophylaxis reduces SSi risk compared with a single pre-operative dose of prophylactic antibiotics. No reports reported adverse effects associated with the drugs in the studies that reported in this outcome. There was insufficient information to show whether a specific antibiotic is better than another.

We included one small trial, involving 40 infants and 42 women. The trial was underpowered to detect clinically important outcome differences between the two policies. There were no significant benefits or adverse effects of elective preterm birth at 36 weeks' gestation for fetal gastroschisis. The primary outcomes were caesarean section and newborn survival to discharge. Two babies died after birth but before discharge in the elective (intervention) group versus no in the spontaneous group. Seven women (33%) in the elective group and nine women (43%) in the spontaneous group delivered by caesarean section. Similarly, for the secondary outcomes, there were no statistical differences in birthweight, ventilation requirements, necrotising enterocolitis and requirement for repeat surgery between the two groups. No of our prespecific maternal secondary outcomes were reported in the included study. We also examined gestational age at birth as a non-prespecified outcome. There was a difference in gestational age at birth between the two arms of the trial (35.8 weeks (SD 0.7) in the elective group and 36.7 (SD 1.5) in the spontaneous group). Possible reasons for this small mean difference include a trend to spontaneous preterm birth in pregnancies complicated by fetal gastroschisis. This review is unable to draw any firm conclusions regarding preterm birth for infants with gastroschisis. It is not possible to say whether the intervention is beneficial or harmful for these babies or their mothers. Only one small trial is included. Further research is needed in this area.

We found three studies in adults, which were conducted in the USA and the USA. The studies were conducted in the last two months of the year. All studies used paracetamol as an add-on to established treatment with strong opioids (mean daily morphine equivalent doses of 60 mg, 70 mg, and 225 mg, with some participants taking several hundreds mg of oral morphine equivalents daily). Other non-paracetamol medication included non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), tricyclic antidepressants, or neuroleptics. The studies were at high risk of bias for incomplete outcome data and small size; no study was unquivotically at low risk of bias. No of the studies reported any of our primary outcomes: participants with pain reduction of at least 50%, and at least 30%, from baseline; participants with pain no worse than mild at the end of the treatment period; participants with Patient Global Ipression of Change (PGIC) of very much improved or similar wording. The evidence is current to January 2016. There is no high-quality evidence to support or refute the use of paracetamol alone or in combination with opioids for the first two steps of the three-step WHO cancer pain ladder. It is not clear whether any additional analgesic benefit of paracetamol could be identified in the available studies, in view of the doses of opioids used.

The review found 15 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that compared the effects of antimuscarinic drugs with placebo. The trials were conducted in China and 14 in hospital. The quality of reporting was poor with no studies clearly describing allocation concealment and many data were missing or unusable. All results were vulnerable to significant bias. Most often the primary outcome was the size of the wet patch on the pillow. The antimuscarinics (astemizole, diphenhydramine, propantheline, doxepin) were the most commonly evaluated drugs. For the outcome of 'no clinically important improvement' astemizole and diphenhydramine were more effective than placebo (a placebo) but the doses of astemizole used were those that can cause toxicity. Data involving propantheline were heterogeneous (I2+ 86.6%), but both studies showed benefit over placebo. Adverse effects were poorly reported. of the other interventions, oryzanol (rice bran oil and rice embryo oil extract) showed benefit over the antimuscarinic doxepin in terms of 'no clinically important change' (n=104, 1 RCT), the Chinese medicine suo quo wan (comprises spicebush root, Chinese yam and bitter cardamom) showed benefit over doxepin (n+70, 1 RCT). There are currently insufficient data to confidently inform clinical practice. The limitations of these trials are numerous and the risk of bias is high. These trials, however, are invaluable guides for current and future study design. well conducted randomised trials are possible. Some may be underway. Current practice outside of well designed randomised trials should be clearly justified.

The review included one randomised clinical trial in which 38 women (41 pregnancies) were randomised, with only 26 women (28 pregnancies) being analysed. This trial found no significant difference in the risk of newborn thrombocytopenia and bleeding. The review authors conducted an outcome-to-treat analysis which showed similar results. The review authors did not provide evidence about other medical treatments for ITP during pregnancy. The review also identifies the need for well-designed, adequately powered randomised clinical trials for this medical condition during pregnancy. Despite the evidence from this trial, there is insufficient evidence to support the use of betamethasone for treating ITP.

We found two studies, with a total of 111 participants (n = 30 and n = 81), which were conducted in Iran. The participants had moderate to severe keratoconus pre-operatively and were randomly allocated to receive either DALK or penetrating keratoplasty. Only one eye of each participant was treated as part of the trials. The small study had 12 month follow-up data for all participants. For the larger study, four DALK surgery had to be abandoned due to technical failure and visual and refractive outcomes were not measured in these participants. Follow-up length for the remaining 77 participants ranged from 6.8 to 36.4 months, with all 77 followed for at least three months post-suture removal. Results of the sensitivity analysis showed that rejection was less likely to occur in DALK (odds ratio (OR): 0.33, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.14 to 0.81). However, the data available from the Javadi 2010 study alone had a very large 95% CI, suggesting an imprecise estimate. In addition, the data available from the Javadi 2010 study only had a very large 95% CI, suggesting an imprecise estimate.

The evidence is current to January 2016. We included five studies with a total of 1726 patients. The most important results were that the combination chemotherapy did not reduce the risk of death from all causes. However, the combination chemotherapy was more effective in reducing the risk of progression-free survival. The quality of the evidence for overall survival was low and for progression-free survival was moderate. There were higher risks of toxicity outcomes grade 3 or 4 diarrhoea and grade 1 or 2 alopecia, and a lower risk of grade 3 or 4 neutropenia in control groups compared to the invervention group. Evidence for toxicity has been assessed to be low to moderate quality.

The review found that a transverse or oblique incision has less effect on pulmonary function and is less prone to rupture (wound dehiscence/incisional hernia). The data on pain is less clear and should be interpreted with caution but some data suggests a transverse incision is less pain. There was no difference seen in other early or late post-operative complications and recovery times were similar although the transverse incision may be cosmetically more acceptable. The analgesic use and the pulmonary compromise may be reduced with a transverse/oblic incision but this does not seem to be significant clinically as pulmonary complication rates and recovery time were the same. The risk of wound dehiscence and rupture appears to be reduced with a transverse incision and a transverse incision may look better. The methodological and clinical diversity and the potential for bias also suggest that the results in favour of a transverse/oblic incision (specifically with regard to analgesic use) should be treated with caution. The optimal incision for abdominal surgery still remains the preference of the surgeon.

We included nine studies with 981 participants. Five studies were conducted in Europe and four in North America. The total number of participants ranged from 33 to 335. The average age between studies ranged from 32.0 to 43.7 years. All included studies were judged as having high risk of performance bias and high risk of detection bias due to lack of blinding, and four of the nine studies suffered from at least one additional source of possible bias. In MBR compared to usual care for subacute LBP, people receiving MBR had less pain and less disability, as well as increased likelihood of return-to-work (three studies with 240 participants), and fewer sick leave days (two studies with 210 participants). The effect sizes for pain and disability were low in terms of clinical meaningfulness, but effects for work-related outcomes were in the moderate range. However, when comparing MBR to other treatments (i.e. short intervention with features from a light mobilization program and a graded activity program, functional restoration, short clinical intervention including education and advice on exercise, and psychological counselling) we found no differences between the groups in terms of pain, functional disability, and time away from work. However, when comparing MBR to other treatments (i.e. short intervention with features from a light mobilization program and a graded activity program, functional restoration, short clinical intervention including education and advice on exercise, and psychological counselling), we found no differences between the groups in terms of pain, functional disability, and time out of work. However, when comparing MBR to other treatments (i.e. short intervention with features from a light mobilization program and a graded activity program, functional restoration, short clinical intervention including education and advice on exercise, and psychological counselling), we found no differences between the groups in terms of pain, functional disability, and time out of work. However, when comparing MBR to other treatments (i.e. short intervention with features from a light mobilization program and a graded activity program, functional restoration, short clinical intervention including education and advice on exercise, and psychological counselling), we found no differences between the groups in terms of pain, functional disability, and time out of work. However, when comparing MBR to other treatments (i.e. short intervention with features from a light mobilization program and a graded activity program, functional restoration, short clinical intervention including education and advice on exercise, and psychological counselling), we found no differences between the groups in terms of pain, functional disability, and time out of work.

We included 15 randomised trials (1437 participants) that compared WDD with placebo or no treatment. The evidence is current to January 2016. We found that WDD improved the short-term global state of participants compared with placebo or no treatment. However, WDD was associated with fewer people having extrapyramidal effects (EPS) compared with other treatments. In the combination group, WDD and antipsychotics had better global state and mental state compared to antipsychotic alone. However, the combination group had more people with EPS compared with the antipsychotic alone group. The quality of the evidence was low or unclear.
