We included 13 trials with 1316 participants in a qualitative synthesis. Participants were incontinent for urine, stool, or both, and were residents in a nursing home or were hospitalised. The total number of participants in the studies was 1217. The evidence is current to January 2016. The evidence is current to February 2015. Nine trials compared different topical skin care products, including a combination of products. Two trials tested a structured skin care procedure. One trial compared topical skin care products alongside frequency of application. One trial compared frequency of application of topical skin care products. We found evidence in two trials, being of low and moderate quality, that soap and water performed poorly in the prevention and treatment of IAD. The first trial indicated that the use of a skin cleanser may be more effective than the use of soap and water. The second trial showed that a structured skin care procedure, being a waschcloth with cleansing, moisturising, and protecting properties, might be more effective than soap and water. Results from the other trials, all being of low to very low quality, suggest that applying a leave-on product (moisturisers, skin protectants, or a combination) and preventing soap may be more effective than withholding these products. The performance of leave-on products depends on the combination of ingredients, the overall formulation and the use (e.g. amount applied). No trial reported on the third primary outcome 'number of participants not satisfied with treatment' or on adverse effects. There is little evidence, of very low to moderate quality, on the effects of interventions for preventing and treating IAD in adults.

We included seven studies with a total of 333 participants in our review. Three studies studied hospitalised patients, two studies were conducted in an outpatient setting, and the trial setting was unclear in two studies. Participants' ages ranged from two years to young adults. The type of antiviral, administration route, and treatment duration varied between the studies. The antiviral drugs in the included studies were acyclovir, valomaciclovir and valacyclovir. Follow-up varied from 20 days to six months. The diagnosis of IM was based on clinical symptoms and laboratory parameters. The risk of bias for all included studies was either unclear or high risk of bias. The quality of evidence was graded as very low for all outcomes and so the results should be interpreted with caution. There were statistically significant improvements in the treatment group for two of the 12 outcomes. These improvement may be of limited clinical importance. There was a total of five days less time to clinical recovery as assessed by doctor in the treatment group but with large confidence intervals (CIs) (three studies, 87 participants). Future studies suggest that clinical signs and symptoms may take one month or more to resolve and that fatigue may be persistent in about 10% of patients at six-month follow-up. The number of adverse events and side effects of medication was reported in only five studies. In some reports authors were unsure whether an adverse event was related to medication or complication of disease. This effect was not sustained when treatment stopped. For all other outcomes there was no significant difference between treatment and control groups. There was no significant difference between treatment and control groups for any of the other outcomes. The quality of evidence was graded as very low for all outcomes and so the results should be interpreted with caution.

We searched for studies that compared insulin detemir with insulin glargine in people with high blood pressure. We found four trials that were conducted in the USA, Australia, Africa and Africa. The trials compared insulin detemir with insulin glargine in people with high blood pressure. The trials were conducted in the years 2015 to 2016. The trials included 2250 people with high blood pressure. The trials compared insulin detemir with insulin glargine in people with high blood pressure. The trials compared insulin detemir with insulin glargine in people with high blood pressure. The results showed no significant differences in overall, nocturnal and severe low blood pressure between treatment groups. Insulin detemir was associated with less weight gain. Treatment with insulin glargine resulted in a lower daily basal insulin dose and a lower number of injection site reactions. There was no significant difference in the variability of FPG or glucose levels in 24 hours profiles between treatment groups. It was not possible to draw conclusions on quality of life, costs or mortality. Only one trial reported results on health-related quality of life and showed no significant differences between treatment groups.

The review included six trials and two parallel group trials. Six trials assessed the effects of SNS for FI. In the parallel group trial conducted by Tjandra, 53 participants with severe FI in the SNS group experienced fewer episodes of faecal incontinence compared to the control group who received optimal medical therapy (mean difference (MD) 5-20.20, 95% confidence interval (CI) 9.15 to 1.25 at 3 months; mean difference (MD) 6.30, 95% CI 1034 to 226 at 12 months). Adverse events were reported in a proportion of participants: pain at implant site (6%), seroma (2%) and excessive tingling in the vaginal area (9%). In the parallel group trial performed by Thin, 15 participants with FI in the SNS group experienced fewer episodes of faecal incontinence compared with the percutaneous tibial nerve stimulation (PTNS) group (MD 3.00, 95% CI 6.1 to 0.41) at 3 months; MDA 3.20, 95% CI 7.4 to 0.74 at 12 months). Adverse events were reported in three participants: mild ipsilateral leg pain during temporary testing (n = 1); and stimulator-site pain after insertion of neurostimulator (n = 2) in the crossover trial by Leroi 7 of 34 recruited participants were excluded from the crossover due mainly to complications or immediate device failure. Twenty-four of the remaining 27 participants while still blinded chose the period of stimulation they had preferred. Outcomes were reported separately for 19 participants who preferred the 'on' and five who preferred the 'off' period. For the group of 19 and five, the average episodes of faecal incontinence per week decreased from 1.7 (1 to 9) during the 'off' period to 0.7 (1 to 5) during the 'on' period. For the group of five, however, the average episodes of 1.7 (1 to 11) during the 'off' period compared with 3.7 (1 to 11) during the 'on' period. Four of 27 participants experienced an adverse event resulting in removal of the stimulator.

The review of trials found that the failure of molar tubes bonded with either a chemically-cured or light-cured adhesive was significantly higher than that of molar bands cemented with glass ionomer cement. However, there are limited data for this outcome, and further evidence is needed to draw more robust conclusions.

We found 66 studies (published between 1988 and 2012) that were included in this review. We included 7747 patients with gastric cancer who were staged with EUS. The quality of the included studies was good: in particular, only five studies presented a high risk of index test interpretation bias and two studies presented a high risk of selection bias. For primary tumor (T) stage, results were stratified by the depth of invasion of the gastric wall. The meta-analysis of 50 studies (n = 4397) showed that the summary sensitivity and specificity of EUS in discriminating T1 to T2 (suppressive) versus T3 to T4 (advanced) gastric cancers were 0.86 (95% confidence interval (CI) 0.81 to 0.93) and 0.90 (95% CI 0.87 to 0.93) respectively. for the diagnostic capacity of EUS to distinguish T1 (early gastric cancer) versus T2 (muscle-infiltrating) cancers, the meta-analysis of 46 studies (n = 2742) showed that the summary sensitivity and specificity were 0.85 (95% CI 0.78 to 0.91) and 0.90 (95% CI 0.85 to 0.93) respectively. for the metastatic involvement of lymph nodes (N-stage), the meta-analysis of 44 studies (n = 3573) showed that the summary sensitivity and specificity were 0.83 (95% CI 0.79 to 0.87) and 0.67 (95% CI 0.61 to 0.72), respectively. Overall, as demonstrated also by the Bayesian nomograms, which allow authors to calculate post-test probabilities for any target condition prevalence, the EUS accuracy can be considered clinically useful to guide physicians in the locoregional staging of people with gastric cancer.

We included eight studies, involving 2488 participants. The studies were of generally good methodological quality; we judged only one study at high risk of bias, due to small size. Two studies used a placebo control and six used 0.04% topical capsaicin as an 'active' placebo to help maintain blinding. The results of the studies were inconsistent, resulting in analyses for most outcomes being based on less than complete data. For postherpetic neuralgia, we found four studies (1272 participants) that reported that about 10% more participants reported that they were much or very much improved with high-concentration capsaicin than with 'active' placebo. The number of participants needed to treat for an additional beneficial outcome (NNTs) was 8 and 12 weeks. More participants (about 10%) had average 2 to 8-week and 2 to 12-week pain intensity reductions over baseline of at least 30% and at least 50% with high-concentration capsaicin than control. The number of participants who were very or very much improved at 12 weeks was 27% with high-concentration capsaicin and 10% with 'active' placebo. The number of participants who were persistent with persistent pain after inguinal herniorrhaphy was 46. The quality of the evidence for efficacy outcomes was very low. We downgraded the quality of the evidence for efficacy outcomes by one to three levels due to sparse data.

We included three trials involving 6343 participants. The trials were different in the methods of measuring carotid artery stenosis and in the definition of stroke, and we did a group analysis of individual patient data on 6092 participants (35,000 patient years of follow-up). We found no significant differences between the trials in the risk of any of the main outcomes in either the treatment groups, or in the effects of surgery. Surgery increased the five-year risk of ipsilateral ischaemic stroke in participants with less than 30% stenosis, had no significant effect in participants with 30 to 49% stenosis, was of benefit in participants with 50% to 69% stenosis, and was highly beneficial in participants with 70 to 99% stenosis without near-occlusions. However, there was no evidence of benefit in people with a stenosis of the internal carotid artery that was not very high.

We included one randomised controlled trial (RCT) with a total of 37 participants. The participants underwent enterostomy placement in the frame of an operation for: rectal cancer (37/60), ulcerative colitis (14/60), familial adenomatous polyposis (7/60), and other (2/60). The results between the lateral pararectal and the transrectal approach groups were inconclusive for the incidence of parastomal herniation, development of ileus or stenosis, or skin irritation. The results were also inconclusive for the subgroup analysis in which we compared the effect of ileostomy versus colostomy on parastomal herniation. The study did not measure other stoma-related adverse events, or stoma-related mortality, but did measure quality of life, which was not one of our outcomes of interest. The evidence is current to January 2016.

We searched for studies that compared support therapy with standard care. We found 24 studies, with 2126 participants. The evidence was of very low quality. We found no significant differences in the primary outcomes of relapse, hospitalisation and general functioning between supportive therapy and standard care. However, there were significant differences favouring other psychological or psychosocial treatments over supportive therapy. These included hospitalisation rates (four studies, n = 306), clinical improvement in mental state (three studies, n = 194) and satisfaction of treatment for the recipient of care (one study, n = 45). For this comparison, we found no evidence of significant differences for rate of relapse, leaving the study early and quality of life. In the comparison of support therapy to cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT), we also found no significant differences in primary outcomes. There were very limited data to compare support therapy with family therapy and psychoeducation, and no studies provided data about clinically important change in general functioning, one of our primary outcomes of interest. There are insufficient data to identify a difference in outcome between supportive therapy and standard care. There are several outcomes, including hospitalisation and general mental state, indicating advantages for other psychological therapies over supportive therapy but these findings are based on a few small studies where we graded the evidence as very low quality. Future research would benefit from larger trials that use support therapy as the main treatment arm than the comparator.

We searched for studies that compared NSAIDs with other treatments for cancer pain. We found 11 studies, which were of very low quality. The studies included 949 participants with most moderate or severe pain. The studies were randomised and were conducted in the USA, the USA, the USA, and the USA. The studies compared NSAIDs with placebo, a placebo, or a combination of NSAIDs and opioids. The studies were of very low quality. The studies did not report the number of people who completed treatment or had results of treatment. The studies did not report the number of people who received NSAIDs as a group with another treatment, or one NSAID with another NSAID. The evidence is current to January 2016. There was no high-quality evidence to support or refute the use of NSAIDs alone or in combination with opioids for the three steps of the three-step WHO cancer pain ladder. There is very low-quality evidence that some people with moderate or severe cancer pain can receive significant levels of benefit in one or two weeks.

The review included two studies with 287 participants. One study (with an overall unclear to high risk of bias) included 253 participants and the quality of the evidence for most outcomes was very low. This study reported that pain severity at day two and day three was lower in the tinzaparin group than in the placebo group and also at day 4 (P  0.01 (ANV)). Thus tinzaparin resulted in more rapid resolution of pain, as measured with a number of pain scales. The total duration of pain crises was -1.78 days in favour of the tinzaparin group. Participants treated with tinzaparin had statistically significant fewer hospitalisation days than participants in the group treated with placebo. Two minor bleeding events were reported as adverse events in the tinzaparin group, and no adverse events were reported in the placebo group. The second study (unclear risk of bias) including 34 participants and was a conference abstract with limited data and only addressed one of the predefined outcomes of the review; i.e. pain intensity. After one day pain intensity reduced more, as reported on a visual analogue scale, in the dalteparin group than in the placebo group, total difference -1.30 (ANV) (quality of evidence rated very low). The most important reasons for downgraded the quality of evidence were serious risk of bias and imprecision (due to low sample size or low occurrence of events).

We found no randomised controlled trials comparing either anticoagulants or antiplatelet drugs with control. There were also no randomised trials that directly compared anticoagulants with antiplatelet drugs and the reported non-randomised studies did not show any evidence of a significant difference between the two.

We found 26 trials (involving 2066 patients) that compared silver-containing dressings or topical agents with non-silver dressings. The evidence is current to January 2016. We found no evidence that silver-containing dressings or topical agents improve wound healing or prevent wound infection. There is insufficient evidence to determine whether silver-containing dressings or topical agents promote wound healing or prevent wound infection.

We included 12 studies with 3571 participants. The studies compared one antimicrobial regimen versus another, but the different drugs examined by each study varied. There was potential for bias because some studies did not report outcomes for all participants. All but one study reported sources of funding or authors affiliations with pharmaceutical companies. We found no statistical difference in all-cause mortality, adverse events, and length of stay in ICU or adverse events between monotherapy and combination therapy. We found no statistical difference in all-cause mortality, adverse events, and length of ICU stay between carbapenem and non-carbapenem therapies. We found that carbapenems are associated with a higher risk of adverse events than non-carbapenems. We found no evidence that carbapenems are associated with a lower risk of adverse events than non-carbapenems. We found no evidence that carbapenems are associated with a higher risk of adverse events than non-carbapenems. We found no evidence that carbapenems are associated with a higher risk of adverse events than non-carbapenems. We found no evidence that carbapenems are associated with a higher risk of adverse events than non-carbapenems.

We included 29 studies (with a total of 12 studies) that evaluated the effects of policies targeting 11 drug groups for restriction. Participants were most often older people or low income adults, or both, in publically subsidized or administered pharmaceutical benefit plans. The effect of policies varied by drug group and whether restrictions were implemented or relaxed. In the case of gastric-acid suppressant and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, decreased drug use and significant costs were found after the policy was used. In the case of second generation antipsychotic drugs, treatment discontinuity and the use of other health services did not increase the use of other health services. Relaxing limitations for reimbursement of antihypertensive and statins increased appropriate use and reduced overall drug costs. Two studies which measured health outcomes directly were inconclusive.

We searched for studies that compared one-to-one oral health treatment with no oral health treatment. We found 18 studies with a total of 4232 participants. The included studies reported a wide variety of interventions, study populations, clinical outcomes and outcome measures. There was significant clinical heterogeneity between the studies and it was not deemed appropriate to pool data in a meta-analysis. We summated data by categorising similar interventions into comparison groups. Comparison 1: All form of one-to-one oral health treatment versus no oral health treatment Four studies compared any form of one-to-one oral health treatment versus no oral health treatment. Two studies reported the outcome of gingivitis. However, one small study had contradictory results at 3 months and 6 months, the other study showed very low-quality evidence of a benefit for one-to-one oral health treatment at all time points (very low-quality evidence). The same two studies reported the outcome of plaque. There was low-quality evidence that these interventions showed a benefit for plaque reduction at all time points. Two studies reported the outcome of dental caries at 6 months and 12 months respectively. There was very low-quality evidence of a benefit for one-to-one oral health treatment at 12 months. Comparison 2: Patient-based one-to-one oral health treatment versus routine one-to-one oral health treatment Four studies compared personalised one-to-one oral health treatment versus routine one-to-one oral health treatment. There was little evidence available that any of these interventions demonstrated a difference on the outcomes of gingivitis, plaque or dental caries (very low-quality evidence). Comparison 3: Self-management versus professional oral health treatment Five studies compared some form of self-management with some form of professional oral health treatment. There was little evidence available that any of these interventions demonstrated a difference on the outcomes of gingivitis or plaque (very low-quality evidence). No of the studies measured dental caries. Comparison 4: Enhanced one-to-one oral health treatment versus one-to-one routine oral health treatment Seven studies compared some form of improved one-to-one oral health treatment with some form of routine oral health treatment. There was little evidence available that any of these interventions demonstrated a difference on the outcomes of gingivitis, plaque or dental caries (very low-quality evidence). There was insufficient high-quality evidence to recommend any specific one-to-one oral health method as being effective in improving oral health or being effective in improving oral health.

We included five small trials (a total of 30 and 49 participants) that compared oral steroids with placebo or no treatment. The trials were of different quality (only one of high quality) and some were poorly reported. One trial reported significant short-term benefits of oral steroids versus placebo: 48% more participants reported success (RR = 2 (95% CI 1.3 to 3.1, NNT)); overall improvement in pain 2.7 (95% CI 11.3 to 35.3), total shoulder abduction increased by 23.3 degrees (95% CI 11.3 to 35.3), Shoulder Pain and Disability Index (SPADI) score improved by 18.1 (95% CI 7.6 to 28.6) on a 0 to 100 point scale. However, benefits were not maintained at 6 weeks. A second trial reported no significant differences between oral steroid and placebo in pain or range of movement but it suggested improvement occurred earlier in the steroid treated group. A third trial reported that oral steroids provided a more rapid initial improvement in pain compared to no treatment but non-specific differences by five months. There were minimal adverse effects reported.

We included three randomised controlled trials with a total of 50 participants in the review. The trials compared rTMS with placebo. The trials were of low quality and were insufficiently homogeneous to allow the grouping of results. The trials did not report any adverse events associated with the use of rTMS. However, in view of the small sample size, the methodological limitations and incomplete outcome data, treatment with rTMS can not be judged as completely safe.

We included 10 randomised controlled trials (with a total of 1049 participants) of moderate to high risk of bias. All studies included different comparisons, no had a placebo group. In one trial plasma exchange plus prednisone given significantly better disease control at 1 month (0.3 mg/kg) than prednisone alone (1.0 mg/kg), while another trial showed no difference in disease control at 6 months. No differences in disease control were seen for different doses or formulations of prednisone (one trial each), for azathioprine plus prednisone compared with prednisone alone (one trial), for prednisone plus mycophenolate mofetil or plus azathioprine (one trial), for tetracycline plus nicotinamide compared with prednisone (one trial). Chinese traditional medicine plus prednisone was not effective in one trial. There were no significant differences in healing in a comparison of a standard regimen of topical steroids (clobetasol) with a milder regimen (clobetasol showed significantly more disease control than oral prednisolone in people with extensive and moderate disease (one trial), with significantly reduced mortality and adverse events (one trial). The evidence is current to January 2016.

The review included 70 randomised trials, involving 7957 participants. The quality of the included trials was high, but the quality of the remaining trials was generally low. Seventy-one different herbal medicines were tested in the included trials, in which herbal medicines were compared with placebo or conventional pharmacological therapy. Herbal medicines were also combined with conventional therapy and compared to conventional therapy alone. Compared with placebo, a Standard Chinese herbal formula, individualised Chinese herbal medicine, STW 5 and STW 5-II, Tibetan herbal medicine Padma Lax, traditional Chinese formula tongxie Yaofang, and Ayurvedic preparation showed significantly improvement of global symptoms. Compared with conventional therapy in 65 trials testing 51 different herbal medicines, 22 herbal medicines demonstrated a statistically significant benefit for symptom improvement, and 29 herbal medicines were not significantly different from conventional therapy. In nine trials that evaluated herbal medicine combined with conventional therapy, six tested herbal preparations showed additional benefit from the combination therapy compared with conventional monotherapy. No serious adverse events from the herbal medicines were reported.

We found 15 studies with 561 participants. The studies were conducted in Europe, India, China, South Korea and the USA. The age range of patients was often restricted to adolescents or young adults, but the participants of two studies were from a more large age range (12 to 54 years). The distribution of males and females was similar in eight of the studies, with a predominance of female patients in seven studies. Eight studies were assessed to be at high overall risk of bias; six studies at unclear risk of bias; one study at low risk of bias. Ten studies with 407 randomised and 390 analysed patients compared surgical anchorage with conventional anchorage for the primary outcome of mesiodistal movement of upper first molars. We performed a random-effects model meta-analysis for the seven studies that fully reported this outcome. There was strong evidence of an effect of surgical anchorage on this outcome. Compared with conventional anchorage, surgical anchorage was more effective in the reinforcement of anchorage by 1.68 mm (moderate quality evidence). This results should be interpreted with some caution as there was a significant degree of heterogeneity for this comparison. There was no evidence of a difference in total duration of treatment between surgical and conventional anchorage (-0.15 years; 95% confidence interval (CI) -0.37 years to 0.07 years); three studies, 111 analysed patients). Information on patient-reported outcomes such as pain and acceptability was limited and inconclusive. When direct comparisons were made between two types of surgical anchorage, there was a lack of evidence to suggest that any one technique was better than another. No included studies reported adverse effects.

We found nine studies with 379 participants. The participants had brain palsy (CP) in five of the studies and osteogenesis imperfecta (OI) in the other four. Participants across the trials ranged in age from 2 to 19 years. All studies, other than one cross-over trial, were parallel designed RCTs. Three of the studies on CP evaluated intrahecal baclofen (ITB) and two botulinum toxin A (BoNT-A). All of the OI trials evaluated the use of bisphosphonates (two alendronate and one pamidronate). No trials were identified that evaluated a commonly used analgesic in this patient group. Pain was a secondary outcome in five of the eight identified studies. Overall the quality of the studies was mixed. Only one study included over 100 participants. For the two ITB studies for pain in CP, in the same study population but assessed at different time points in their disease, both found an effect on pain favouring the intervention compared to the control group (standard care or placebo), but most of the adverse events related to the procedure or device for administration than the drug, such as swelling at the pump site. In one trial there were also eight serious adverse effects; these included difficulty swallowing and an epileptic seizure. The trial did not state if these occurred in the intervention group. At follow-up in both BoNT-A trials there was no evidence of a difference in pain between the trial arms between CP participants. The adverse events in the BoNT-A trials mostly involved those who received the intervention drug and involved seizures. Gastrointestinal problems were the most frequent adverse event in those who received aalendronate. The trial investigating pamidronate found no evidence of a difference in pain compared to the control group. No adverse events were reported in this trial.

We found seven trials involving 1697 participants. Three trials involving 1243 patients were included in analyses of efficacy outcomes, and four trials involving 1034 patients were included in analyses of safety and tolerability outcomes. The evidence is current to January 2016. There was low quality evidence favouring latrepirdine on the Clinician's Interview - Based Ipression of Change plus Caregiver Input after 26 weeks (CIBIC-Plus). There was no evidence to suggest that latrepirdine had any effect on cognitive or function outcomes. However, there was some high quality evidence showing a very small benefit of latrepirdine on the Neuropsychiatric Inventory (NPI) at study endpoint (26 or 52 weeks). There was moderate quality evidence suggesting that latrepirdine and placebo were similar in adverse events, serious adverse events.

We included 45 studies, with a total of 40 participants. The studies compared PEMs to no intervention or to other interventions. The studies were conducted in the USA, the United States, the United States, and the United States. The studies compared PEMs to other interventions, or to PEMs as part of a multifaceted intervention. The results of this review suggest that when used alone and compared to no intervention, PEMs may have a small beneficial effect on professional practice outcomes. There is insufficient information to reliably estimate the effect of PEMs on patient outcomes, and clinical importance of the observed effect sizes is not known. The effectiveness of PEMs compared to other interventions, or of PEMs as part of a multifaceted intervention, is uncertain.

We searched for studies that compared the effects of a behavioural intervention for women who were undergoing family planning. We found 23 studies that included a total of 5271 studies. The majority of the studies provided information about STIs and taught safer sex skills (e.g. communication), occasionally supplemented with provision of resources (e.g. free sexual health services). The results showed that behavioural interventions for young women which aim to promote sexual behaviours protective of STI transmission can be effective, mainly at increasing condom use. Further research should include a greater focus on HPV and its link to cervical cancer. The evidence is current to January 2015.

We included 20 studies with a total of 2337 participants in this review. Nineteen studies compared short psychoeducation with routine care or conventional delivery of information. One study compared short psychoeducation with cognitive behavior therapy. Participants receiving short psychoeducation were less likely to be non-compliant with medication than those receiving routine care in the short term (n = 448, 3 studies compared short psychoeducation with routine care) and moderate term (n = 118, 1 study compared short psychoeducation with cognitive behavior therapy). Compliance with follow-up was similar between the two groups in the short term (n = 30, 1 study compared short psychoeducation with routine care) and long term (n = 386, 2 studies compared short psychoeducation with cognitive behavior therapy). Relapse rates were significantly lower between the two groups in the short term (n = 406, 2 studies compared short psychoeducation with cognitive behavior therapy) but not in the long term. Data from a few individual studies showed that short psychoeducation can improve the long-term global state (n = 59, 1 study compared short psychoeducation with routine care), promote improved mental state in short term (n = 60, 1 study compared short psychoeducation with routine care), reduce the incidence and severity of anxiety and depression, social function such as rehabilitation status and social disability, and improve quality of life. There was no difference found in quality of life as measured by GQOLI-74 in the short term (n = 62). There was no difference found in quality of life as measured by GQOLI-74 in the short term (n = 62).

The review included eight studies that compared the use of a combination of a small subset of red flags to screen for vertebral fracture in patients with LBP. The evidence is current to January 2016. The evidence is current to February 2015. The evidence is current to October 2017. The results of the review show that a combination of a small subset of red flags may be useful to screen for vertebral fracture. However, it should also be noted that many red flags have high false positive rates; and if acted on uncritically there would be effects on the cost of management and outcomes of patients with LBP. Further research should focus on appropriate sets of red flags and adequate reporting of both index and reference tests.

The review found two randomised controlled trials that compared the effects of urethral dilatation and optical urethrotomy in 210 adult men with urethral stricture disease. There was no significant difference in the proportion of men being stricture free at three years or in the average time to recurrence. The second trial compared the outcomes of urethroplasty and urethrectomy in 50 men with traumatic stricture of the posterior urethra after pelvic fracture injury. In the first six months, men were more likely to require further surgery in the urethroplasty group than in the primary urethroplasty group. After two years, 16 of 25 (64%) men initially treated by urethrotomy required continued self-dilation or further surgery for stricture recurrence compared to 6 of 25 (24%) men treated by primary urethrectomy. There were insufficient data to perform meta-analysis or to reliably determine effect size. There were insufficient data to determine which intervention is best for urethral stricture disease in terms of balancing efficacy, adverse effects and costs.

The review of trials found that there was no significant difference between the two groups in the risk of having an exacerbation requiring oral corticosteroids. There was no significant difference between the two groups in the risk of having a serious adverse health event. The trials were of high methodological quality and included a total of 1211 patients with confirmed, or suspected, persistent asthma. The trials were of high methodological quality. A total of 1211 patients included in the review included in the review were with a total of 1211 patients with confirmed, or suspected, persistent asthma. The trials were of high methodological quality. The trials were of high quality. The trials were of high quality. The results of the review suggest that intermittent ICS may be effective in reducing the risk of an exacerbation requiring oral corticosteroids. The trials were of high methodological quality.

We included 17 studies involving 1639 people with CKD. Three studies enrolled 341 people treated with dialysis, four studies enrolled 168 kidney transplant recipients and 10 studies enrolled 1130 people with CKD stages 1 to 5. Eight studies (900 people) evaluated dietary counselling with or without lifestyle advice and six studies (79 people) evaluated dietary patterns (739 people). The evidence is current to January 2016. The studies were not designed to examine all-cause mortality or heart disease. In absolute terms, dietary interventions may prevent one person in every 3000 treated for one year preventing heart disease. Across all 17 studies, outcome data for heart disease were sparse. Using the results of the included studies, we found that dietary interventions may reduce blood pressure and diastolic blood pressure, and increase the rate of glomerular filtration rate (eGFR). The quality of the evidence was very low. The evidence was based on a small number of studies and the quality of the evidence was very low.

We found only one study (156 children aged between seven weeks and 24 months with signs and symptoms of bronchiolitis) that was included in this review. The study found that nebulised salbutamol was an effective intervention for young children with bronchiolitis but mist in a tent did not lead to a significant reduction in respiratory distress symptom (RDS) score. The study did not report on adverse effects of the interventions.

We found four studies (in a total of 1154 participants) that compared statins with placebo or no treatment. The studies were conducted in the USA, the USA, the USA and the USA. The studies included people with a diagnosis of likely or possible AD and most participants were established on a cholinesterase inhibitor. The results of the studies showed no benefit from statins on the primary outcome measures of Alzheimer's disease Assessment Scale - cognitive subscale (ADAS-Cog). There was no significant benefit from statins in Mini Mental State Examination (MMSE). Three studies reported treatment-related adverse effects. There was no significant difference between statins and placebo in behaviour, global function or activities of daily living in the statin and placebo groups. We found no studies assessing role of statins in treatment of VaD.

The review of studies found no evidence of significant benefit from any intervention for increasing ankle range of motion in children and adults with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 1A or Duchenne muscle dystrophy. Further research is needed.

We found 25 studies (5218 women) that compared upright and mobile positions with recumbent positions. The studies were of low quality, and the results should be interpreted with caution as the methodological quality of the studies was different. The results suggest that walking and upright positions in the first stage of labour reduces the duration of labour, the risk of caesarean section, the need for epidural, and does not seem to be associated with increased intervention or negative effects on mothers' and babies' well being.

We found three randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that compared treatment with a botulinum toxin (BTX) injection into the scalene muscles with a placebo injection. The first trial compared transaxillary first rib resection with supraclavicular neurectomy of the brachial plexus (SNBP). The trial had a high risk of bias. TFRR decreased pain more than SNBP. There were no adverse effects in either group. The second trial compared 37 people with tos of any type, comparing treatment with a botulinum toxin (BTX) injection into the scalene muscles with a saline placebo injection. This trial had a low risk of bias. There was no significant effect of treatment with the BTX injection over placebo in terms of pain relief or improvements in disability but it did significantly improve paresthesias at six months' follow-up. There were no adverse events of the BTX treatment above saline injection. This review was complicated by a lack of generally accepted diagnostic criteria for the diagnosis of tos and had to based only on the diagnosis of tos by the investigators in the reviewed studies. We identified one study comparing natural progression with an active intervention. We found three randomized controlled trials (RCTs), but only two of them had a follow-up of six months or more, which was the minimum required follow-up for inclusion in the review. The first trial compared 55 participants with the 'disputed type' of tos and compared transaxillary first rib resection with subclavicular neuroplasty of the brachial plexus (SNBP). The trial had a high risk of bias. TFRR reduced pain more than SNBP. There were no adverse effects in either group. The second trial compared 37 people with tos of any type, comparing treatment with a botulinum toxin (BTX) injection into the scalene muscles with a saline placebo injection. This trial had a low risk of bias. There was no significant effect of treatment with the BTX injection over placebo in terms of pain relief or improvement in disability but it did significantly improve paresthesias at six months' follow-up. There were no adverse events of the BTX treatment above saline injection. There is a need for an agreed definition for the diagnosis of tos, also the disputed form, and agreed outcome measures. There is no evidence from RCTs for the use of other currently used treatments.

The review included 21 trials involving 884 people. A hand brace significantly improved symptoms after four weeks and function after six months. In an analysis of 63 participants, ultrasound treatment for two weeks was not significant beneficial. However one trial showed significant symptom improvement after seven weeks of ultrasound which was maintained at six months. Four trials involving 193 people examined different oral drugs (steroids, diuretic, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs) versus placebo. Compared to placebo, steroids, diuretics or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs did not demonstrate significant benefit. In two trials involving 50 people, vitamin B6 did not significantly improve overall symptoms. In one trial involving 51 people yoga significantly reduced pain after eight weeks compared with wrist splinting. In one trial involving 21 people carpal bone mobilisation significantly improved symptoms after three weeks compared to no treatment. In one trial involving 50 people with diabetes, steroid and insulin injections significantly improved symptoms over eight weeks compared with steroid and placebo injections. Two trials involving 105 people compared ergonomic keyboards versus control and demonstrated equivoal results for pain and function. Trials of magnet therapy, laser acupuncture, exercise or chiropractic care did not show symptom benefit when compared with control.

We included six randomised trials involving a total of 394 patients. Five of the six trials showed that intranasal corticosteroids improved nasal obstruction symptoms and adenoid size. The first eight-week cross-over study showed that treatment with beclomethasone (336 mcg/day) yielded a greater improvement in mean symptom scores than placebo (-18.5 versus -8.5), and a larger reduction in average adenoid/choana ratio than placebo (right, -14% versus +0.4%, P = 0.02; left, -15% versus -2.0%). The second four-week cross-over study showed that the Nasal Obstruction Index decreased by at least 50% in 38% of patients treated with beclomethasone (400 mcg/day) between week 0 and week 2 compared with no improvement in the placebo group. The third four-week cross-over study showed that 77.7% of patients treated with mometasone (100 mcg/day) for 40 days demonstrated an improvement in nasal obstruction symptoms and adenoid size, so that adenoidectomy could be avoided compared with no significant improvement in the placebo group. The fourth parallel-group trial showed that eight weeks of treatment with flunisolide (500 mcg/day) was associated with a larger reduction in adenoid size than isotonic saline solution. The five-week parallel-group trial showed that eight weeks of treatment with fluticasone (400 mcg/day) significantly reduced nasal obstruction symptoms and adenoid size, and adenoidectomy was avoided in 76% of these patients compared with 20% of the patients treated with normal saline. In contrast, one parallel-group trial did not find a significant improvement in nasal obstruction symptoms or adenoid size after eight weeks of treatment with beclomethasone.

We included one small study (involving 24 women) in this review. This study was a single-centre randomised trial conducted in Mexico. The study compared a control group who received antihypertensive therapy, anticonvulsant therapy, plasma expanders, corticosteroids and dypyridamole with an intervention group that received epidural block in addition to the antihypertensive drugs, as well as all the other four drugs. Lumbar epidural block was given using 0.25% bupivacaine, 10 mg bolus and 5 mg each hour on continuous epidural infusion for six hours. The included study did not report on any of this review's important outcomes. Meta-analysis was not possible. For the mother, these were: death (death during pregnancy or up to 42 days after the end of the pregnancy, or death more than 42 days after the end of the pregnancy), development of eclampsia or recurrence of seizures; stroke; any serious disease: defined as at least one of stroke, kidney failure, liver failure, HELLP syndrome (haemolysis, elevated liver enzymes and low platelets), disseminated intravascular coagulation, lung oedema. For the baby, these were: death: stillbirths (death in utero at or after 20 weeks' gestation), perinatal deaths (stillbirths plus deaths in the first week of life), death before discharge from the hospital, newborn deaths (death in the first 28 days after birth, deaths after the first 28 days); preterm birth (defined as the birth before 37 completed weeks' gestation), and side effects of the intervention. The included study only reported on a single secondary outcome of interest to this review: the Apgar score of the baby at birth and after five minutes and there was no clear difference between the intervention and control groups. We included 16 trials (one of which was an abstract) that compared glucocorticosteroids with placebo or no intervention. The trials were conducted in the USA, the UK, and the USA. The participants were between 25 and 70 years old, had different stages of alcoholic liver disease, and 65% were men. Follow-up was up to the time of discharge from the hospital, until they died, or for at least one year. There was no evidence of effect of glucocorticosteroids on all-cause mortality or on health-related quality of life up to three months after randomisation. There was no evidence of effect on the occurrence of serious adverse events during treatment, liver-related death up to three months after randomisation, number of participants with any complications up to three months after randomisation, and number of participants with non-serious adverse events up to three months' follow-up after end of treatment. The evidence is current to January 2016.

We included one randomised controlled trial (RCT) and three ongoing trials that assess a specific transfusion strategy for bone marrow failure patients. The evidence is current to January 2016. The one included study included 13 participants and included only 13 participants. There was insufficient evidence to determine a difference in all-cause mortality or the number of red blood cell transfusions. There were no anaemia-related complications reported (cardiac failure) and no reported effect on activity levels (no data provided). The study did not report: mortality due to bleeding/infection/transfusion reactions or iron overload, quality of life, frequency and length of hospital admissions, serious infections (requiring admission to hospital), or serious bleeding (e.g. WHO/CTCAE grade 3 (or equivalent) or above).

We searched for studies that compared the effectiveness of a randomized controlled trial (RCT) to a standard based training strategy. We found two studies that compared a randomized controlled trial with a standard based training strategy. The first study compared a randomized controlled trial (RCT) with a standard based training strategy. The other study compared a randomized controlled trial (RCT) with a standard based training strategy. The results of this review do not confirm or refute the effectiveness of a randomized controlled trial (RCT) as a training strategy for health professionals. There is a need for additional high-quality research to investigate the effect of educational games on patient and performance outcomes.

The review included eight randomised controlled trials involving 475 participants. Two of the studies included a mixed group of participants with either bipolar or unipolar disorder. The results showed that lithium or antidepressants were effective in preventing relapse in unipolar affective disorder, but their relative efficacy was unknown. Patient and clinicians should take into account the patient's clinical history, the side-effects and the individual's likely adherence to the recommended treatment regimen.

The review of trials found that amifostine did not have any significant radioprotective effects on salivary glands in high-dose radioactive iodine treated different thyroid cancer patients. Moreover, no health-related quality of life and other patient-oriented outcomes were evaluated in the two included trials.

We included three studies involving 45 children aged between 29 months and six years with Down syndrome. Two studies compared parent-mediated interventions versus TAU; the third compared a parent-mediated plus clinician-mediated intervention versus a patient-mediated intervention alone. Treatment duration varied from 12 weeks to six months. One study provided nine group sessions and four individualised home-based sessions over a 13-week period. One study provided weekly, individual clinic-based or home-based sessions lasting 1.5 to 2 hours, over a six-month period. The third study provided one 2 to 3 hour group session followed by bi-weekly, individual clinic-based sessions plus one-weekly home-based sessions for 12 weeks. The results of the three included studies were inconsistent. Two studies found no differences in expressive or receptive language abilities between the groups, whether measured by direct assessment or parent reports. However, they did find that children in the intervention group could use more targeted vocabulary items or utterances with language targets in different settings postintervention, compared to those in the control group; this was not maintained 12 months later. The third study found gains for the intervention group on total-language measures immediately postintervention. One study did not find any differences in parental stress scores between the groups at any time point up to 12 months postintervention. All three studies noted differences in most measures of how the parents talked to and interacted with their children postintervention, and in one study, the parents were not reported on the target words for their children. The study authors did not report the methods used to generate the random sequence. We judged all three studies to be at high risk of bias in relation to blinding of participants (not possible due to the nature of the intervention), and at an unclear risk of reporting bias, as they did not report on one assessed outcome; and at high risk of detection bias, as only parents in the intervention group were made aware of the target words for their children. The number of children in the studies was very small, which suggests that they are likely to be representative of the target population.

We included two randomised clinical trials with a total of 148 participants who have had heart valve surgery. The studies were of high risk of bias, and the quality of evidence was very low. There was insufficient evidence at 3 to 6 months follow-up to judge the effect of exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation compared to no exercise on mortality and serious adverse events. The studies did not report on health-related quality of life (HRQoL), and the secondary outcomes of New York Heart Association class, left ventricular ejection fraction and cost. We found that exercise-based rehabilitation may improve exercise capacity. However, there was insufficient evidence at 12 months follow-up for the return to work outcome. Further high-quality randomised clinical trials are needed to assess the effect of exercise-based rehabilitation on patient-relevant outcomes, including mortality and quality of life.

We found five studies (in a total of 1130 participants) that evaluated interventions with spiritual or religious components for adults in the terminal phase of a disease. The studies evaluating meditation found no overall significant difference between people receiving meditation or usual care on quality of life or well-being. However, when meditation was combined with massage in the medium term it buffered against a reduction in quality of life. In the palliative care intervention studies there was no significant difference in quality of life or well-being between the trial groups. Cops with the disease was not evaluated in the studies. The quality of the studies was limited by under-reporting of design features. We found inconclusive evidence that interventions with mental or psychological components for adults in the terminal phase of a disease may or may not improve well-being. These interventions are under-evaluated. All five studies identified were conducted in the same country and in the multi-disciplinary palliative care interventions it is unclear if all participants received support from a chaplain or a mental or mental health counsellor. Moreover, it is unclear in all the studies whether the participants in the comparison groups received mental or mental health support, or both, as part of routine care or from other areas. The paucity of quality research suggests a need for more rigorous studies.

We included 12 studies (N = 446) that compared LASB to placebo/sham or other treatments. The evidence is current to January 2016. Two small studies compared LASB to placebo/sham. They did not show any benefit for LASB in the short term. One small study compared thoracic sympathetic block with corticosteroid and local anaesthetic versus injection of the same agents into the subcutaneous space, reporting significant and clinically important differences in pain intensity at one-year follow-up but not at short term follow-up. Eight small randomised studies compared LASB to different other active interventions. Most studies found no difference in pain outcomes between LASB versus other active treatments. One small study compared ultrasound-guided LASB with non-guided LASB and found no clinically important difference in pain outcomes. Six studies reported adverse events, all with minor effects reported. The quality of the evidence was low to very low. This is due to limitations, inconsistency, imprecision, indirectness, or a combination of these.

We found two studies, including 97 women, that were included in this review. One study compared leuprorelin with treosulfan (a chemotherapy) and the other compared decapeptyl with a placebo. The Currie 1994 study compared decapeptyl with placebo and reported total PFS of 16 weeks verus 11.2 weeks, respectively. No relative effects measures or P value at a particular time point were reported. Overall survival (OS) and quality of life outcomes were not reported. In addition, adverse events were only noted for the decapeptyl group. adverse events were incompletely reported (no adverse events).

The review included seven preventive studies (14,437 people) and eight treatment studies (1361 people). The evidence is current to January 2016. There was moderate evidence that lumbar support is not more effective than no intervention or training in preventing low-back pain, and conflicting evidence whether they are effective supplements to other preventive interventions. It is still unclear if lumbar support is more effective than no or other interventions for treating low-back pain. There is still a need for high quality randomised trials on the effectiveness of lumbar support. One of the most important issues to tackle in these future trials seems to be the realization of an adequate compliance. In addition, there is a need for high quality randomised trials on the effectiveness of lumbar support.

We included 57 studies with 16784 catheters and 11 types of impregnations. The total number of participants enrolled was unclear, as some studies did not provide this information. Most studies enrolled participants from the age of 18, including patients in intensive care units (ICU), oncology units and patients receiving long-term total parenteral nutrition. There were low or unclear risk of bias in the included studies, but no evidence of bias in the studies due to the catheters that were being evaluated having different appearances. The results of this review suggest that catheter impregnation reduces catheter-related blood stream infection (CRBSI) and catheter colonization (CVC colonization). However, catheter impregnation did not reduce the rates of clinically diagnosed sepsis, all-cause mortality and catheter-related local infections. However, catheter impregnation did not reduce the rates of clinically diagnosed sepsis, all-cause death and catheter-related local infections. The quality of the evidence was moderate for the outcomes of catheter-related blood stream infection (CRBSI) and catheter-related local infections (CVC colonization). The quality of the evidence for the outcomes of catheter-related local infections (CVC colonization) was moderate for the outcomes of catheter-related local infections (CVC colonization).

The review included 12 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with 1023 participants. The evidence is current to January 2016. There was no difference in healing outcomes between hydrocellular foam dressings and polyurethane foam dressings. There was no difference in healing outcomes between hydrocellular foam dressings and hydrocolloid dressings when foam dressings were compared with: paraffin gauze (two RCTs), hydrocapillary dressing (one RCT), knitted viscose dressing (one RCT), and protease modulating matrix (one RCT). No significant differences in the proportion of participants experiencing adverse events were found between foam dressings and other wound dressings. The evidence in this area is of low quality. Further evidence is needed from well-designed and rigorously-conducted RCTs, that use methods to reduce bias and report them clearly, before any definitive conclusions can be made about the effectiveness of foam dressings in the management of venous leg ulcers.

The review included two studies (n = 381 IBD patients with rheumatological manifestations) that compared etoricoxib to placebo. One study (n = 159) compared etoricoxib to placebo in IBD patients with quiescent or active ulcerative colitis or Crohn's disease. The other study (n = 222) compared celecoxib to placebo in IBD patients with quiescent ulcerative colitis. The two included studies were not pooled for meta-analysis due to differences in patient populations and treatment duration. There was no significant difference in the number of patients who had an exacerbation of IBD between etoricoxib and placebo. There was no significant difference in the number of patients who had an exacerbation of ulcerative colitis between celecoxib and placebo. The study comparing etoricoxib to placebo reported but did not report on adverse events. The proportion of patients who experienced AEs was similar in the celecoxib and placebo groups (21% and 17%, respectively). No patients in either group died or had serious adverse events. Eighty-one% of patients in the celecoxib and placebo groups had a severe adverse event. The quality of the evidence for the effect of etoricoxib on the risk of recurrence of IBD was low. The quality of the evidence for the effect of celecoxib on the risk of recurrence of IBD was low.

We included six studies that included 5193 children. The evidence is current to January 2016. We found that zinc supplementation reduced the incidence of pneumonia by 13% and the prevalence of pneumonia by 41%. We found no effect on the number of children who had a lower specificity lung disease (i.e. age-specific fast breathing with or without lower chest indrawing). We found no evidence that zinc supplementation reduced the risk of lung disease in children with a low specificity lung disease.

We searched for studies that compared vitamin A with other drugs to prevent acute LRTIs. We found 10 studies involving 33,179 participants. Eight studies found no significant effect of vitamin A on the incidence of acute LRTI, or prevalence of symptoms of acute LRTI. Vitamin A caused an increased incidence of acute LRTI in one study; an increase in cough and fever; and increased symptoms of cough and rapid breathing in two other studies. Three reported no differences and no protective effect of vitamin A. Two studies reported that vitamin A significantly reduced the incidence of acute LRTI in children with poor nutritional status or weight, but increased the incidence in healthy children. This unexpected results is outside our current understanding of the use of vitamin A for preventing acute LRTIs. However, vitamin A should not be given to all children to prevent acute LRTIs.

The review of studies found that theophylline has a moderate effect on FEV1 and FVC and slightly improves blood gas pressures in people with moderate to severe COPD. This benefit was seen in participants receiving a variety of different bronchodilator plus corticosteroid therapy. Theophylline did not improve exercise performance, but it was more effective than placebo. There was a very low dropout rate in the studies that could be included in this review, which suggests that recruited participants may have been known by the investigators to be theophylline tolerant . This may limit the generalisability of these studies.

We included one randomised controlled trial (RCT) that compared nebulised recombinant human deoxyribonuclease (rhDRNA) with placebo in 40 children with airway malacia and a respiratory tract infection. The study was conducted in the USA and included 40 children with airway malacia and a respiratory tract infection. The results of this review showed that there was no significant difference between groups for the primary outcome of proportion cough-free at two weeks. However, the total change in night time cough diary scores was significantly better in the placebo group. The total change in daytime cough diary scores from baseline was also better in the placebo group, but the difference between groups was not statistically significant. Other outcomes (dyspnoea, and difficulty in expectorating sputum scores, and lung function tests at two weeks) also favoured placebo over nebulised rhDRNA but did not reach levels of importance. There is currently an absence of evidence to support any of the therapies currently utilised for management of intravenous tracheomalacia. It is inconclusive whether the use of nebulised rhDRNA in children with airway malacia and a respiratory tract infection worsens recovery. It is likely that any RCT on surgically based management will one day be available for children with severe life-threatening disease associated with tracheomalacia. For children with less severe disease, RCTs on interventions such as antibiotics and chest physiotherapy are clearly needed. Outcomes of these RCTs should include measurements of the trachea and physiological outcomes in addition to clinical outcomes.

We included 21 studies with 2658 participants. All studies evaluated the effectiveness of some form of psychological therapy. We found no studies that included physical therapy. Fourteen studies evaluated forms of cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT), the other studies evaluated behaviour therapies, third-wave CBT (mindfulness), psychodynamic therapies, and integrative therapy. Fifteen included studies compared the studied psychological therapy with usual care or a waiting list. Five studies compared the intervention to improved or structured care. Only one study compared cognitive behavioural therapy with behaviour therapy. Across the 21 studies, the average number of sessions ranged from one to 13 over a period of one day to nine months. Duration of follow-up varied between two weeks and 24 months. Participants were recruited from different health settings and the open population. The duration of symptoms, reported by nine studies, was at least several years, suggesting most participants had chronic symptoms at baseline. The quality of the evidence was moderate to low. The evidence is current to January 2016. The results of this review are based on 21 studies with 2658 participants. The evidence is current to February 2015. The review included 21 trials involving 1742 participants. The studies were of different types of antipsychotics, and included the use of first-generation antipsychotics (flupenthixol decanoate, haloperidol, thiothixene), second-generation antipsychotics (aripirazole, olanzapine, ziprasidone), mood stabilisers (carbamazepine, valproate semisodium, lamotrigine, topiramate), antidepressants (amitriptyline, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, phenelzine sulfate, mianserin) and dietary supplementation (omega-3 fatty acid). The results were suggestive in supporting the use of second-generation antipsychotics, mood stabilisers, and omega-3 fatty acids, but need replication, as most effect estimates were based on single studies. The long-term use of these drugs has not been assessed. The results were suggestive in supporting the use of second-generation antipsychotics, mood stabilisers, and omega-3 fatty acids, but require replication, as most effect estimates were based on single studies. The long-term use of these drugs has not been evaluated.

We included seven trials involving a total of 349 participants, 217 of which completed the studies. Three trials were cross-over and four were parallel-group randomised controlled trials (RCTs). of these, two trials were added for this update (one parallel-group RCT with 40 participants and one cross-over RCT with 67 participants). The included studies reported data on six of the nine primary and secondary outcomes measures. No of the trials assessed: number of catheters used, washeout acceptability measures (including patient satisfaction, patient discomfort, pain and ease of use), or health status/measures of psychological health; very limited data were collected for health economic outcomes. The included studies reported data on only three of the eight intervention comparisons identified. Two trials reported in more than one comparison group. Four trials compared washeout solutions (saline or acidic) with no washeout. We are uncertain if washeout methods (saline or acidic), compared to no washeup, have an important effect on the rate of symptomatic urinary tract infection or length of time each catheter was in situ because the results are imprecise. Four trials compared different types of washeup solution; saline versus acidic solutions (2 trials); saline versus acidic solution versus antibiotic solution 1 trial); saline versus antimicrobial solution 1 trial. We are uncertain if type of washeup solution has an important effect on the rate of symptomatic urinary tract infection or length of time each catheter was in situ because the results are imprecise. One trial compared different compositions of acidic solution (stronger versus weaker solution). We are uncertain if different compositions of acidic solutions has an important effect on the rate of symptomatic urinary tract infection or length of time each catheter was in situ because the results are imprecise. One trial compared different compositions of acidic solution (stronger versus weaker solution). We are uncertain if different compositions of acidic solutions has an important effect on the rate of symptomatic urinary tract infection or length of time each catheter was in situ because the results are imprecise.

We included 36 trials involving 6914 people. The studies were of low quality and the results were not clear. The results showed that a combination of topical and systemic antibiotics reduces the risk of a recurrence of infection and the risk of death in adults receiving intensive care. The risk of resistance occurring as a negative effect of antibiotic use was appropriately investigated only in one trial which did not show any such effect.

The review included five studies. All of them investigated educational approaches. Two studies provided consultation and two other studies provided guidance for nursing staff in addition. Four studies examined nursing home residents and one study residents in group living units. No studies in community settings were included. Three studies included only one or two nursing homes per study condition. Overall, methodological quality of the studies was low. The studies revealed inconsistent results. One study in the nursing home setting reported an increase in both groups after eight months, but the other three studies found reduced use of PR in the intervention groups after seven and 12 months of follow up respectively. The single study examining residents in group living units found no change in PR use in the intervention group after six months whereas PR use increased significantly in the control group. There is insufficient evidence to support the effectiveness of education interventions targeting nursing staff for preventing or reducing the use of physical restraints in geriatric long-term care.

We found four randomised controlled trials (RCTs) comparing glucocorticoid supplementation in women undergoing controlled ovarian hyperstimulation for IVF/ICSI cycles (up to the day of oocyte retrievation). The trials compared glucocorticoid supplementation during IVF stimulation versus placebo. Two of the studies had data in a form that we could not enter into analysis, so results include data from only two trials (310 women). For the outcome of live birth, data were available for only 212 women as the larger study had data available from only one study centre. One of the studies given inadequate description of randomisation methods, but the other was at low risk of bias in all domains. The evidence was rated as low or very low quality for all outcomes, mainly due to imprecision, with low sample sizes and few events. There was insufficient evidence to determine whether there was any difference between the groups in live birth rate (OR 1.08 , 95% CI 0.45 to 258), the chance of live birth with placebo is assumed to be 15%, the chance following supplementation would be between 7% and 31%. There was no conclusions of a difference in the clinical pregnancy rate (OR 1.69 , 95% CI 0.98 to 290), the evidence suggests that if the chance of clinical pregnancy with placebo is assumed to be 24%, the chance following treatment with glucocorticoid supplementation will be between 23% and 47%. There was also insufficient evidence to determine whether there was any difference between the groups in multiple-pregnancy rate (OR 1.00 , 95% CI 0.05 to 1857), or miscarriage rate (OR 1.00 , 95% CI 0.05 to 1857). Neither of the studies reported OHSS or side-effects. The safety and effectiveness of glucocorticoid administration in women undergoing controlled ovarian hyperstimulation for IVF/ICSI cycles (up to the day of oocyte retrievation).

We included eight trials (a total of 291 participants) that compared rhGH therapy with no treatment. The trials lasted for one year and two months. The trials showed evidence of improvement in the anthropometric parameters (height, weight and lean body mass) with rhGH therapy, but there was no difference between standard and high-dose levels. We found improvement in height for all comparisons (very low- to low-quality evidence), but improvements in weight and lean body mass were only reported for standard-dose rhGH versus no treatment (very low-quality evidence). There is some evidence indicating a change in the level of fasting blood glucose with rhGH therapy, but this did not cross the clinical threshold for diagnosis of diabetes in the trials of short duration (low- to very low-quality evidence). There is low- to very low-quality evidence for improvement of pulmonary exacerbations with no further significant adverse effects, but this is limited by the short duration of trials and the small number of participants. One small trial provided inconsistent evidence on improvement in quality of life (very low-quality evidence). There is limited evidence from three trials in improvement in exercise capacity (low-quality evidence). No of the trials have systematically compared the cost of therapy on overall health care costs.

We included 13 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with 975 participants. The evidence is current to January 2016. We found that social skills training may improve the social skills of people with schizophrenia and reduce relapse rates, but at present, the evidence is very limited with data rated as very low quality. When social skills training was compared to discussion there was no difference on patients outcomes. Cultural differences might limit the applicability of the current results, as most reported studies were conducted in China. Whether social skills training can improve social functioning of people with schizophrenia in different settings remains unclear and should be investigated in a large multi-centre randomised controlled trial.

The review found three studies that included 2095 infants. The studies did not show a significant reduction in the incidence of definite NEC, suspected NEC, need for surgery or death from NEC. There are no randomized controlled trials of oral IgA alone for the prevention of NEC.

The review found that chemotherapy is associated with a small benefit in progression-free survival and overall survival despite the use of radiotherapy. This is based on trials of new platinum based chemotherapy regimens. The review found that chemotherapy may be less effective than radiotherapy in a direct comparison, but it may have added benefit when used with radiotherapy.

We included 35 studies, from a wide range of countries on six continents. The studies included a range of digital targeted patient communication, for example medication or appointment reminders, prenatal health information, support for smoking cessation while pregnant, or general sexual health information. The studies included a range of digital targeted patient communication, for example medication or appointment reminders, prenatal health information, support for smoking cessation while pregnancy, or general sexual health information. Our synthesis showed that patients' experiences of these types of programmes were mixed. Some felt that these programmes provided them with feelings of support and connectedness, as they felt that people were taking the time to send them messages. They also reported using the messages with their family and family. However, patients also noted problems when using these programmes. Some patients had poor access to cell networks and to the internet (high confidence in the evidence). Other patients had no phone, had lost or broken their phone, could not afford airtime, or had changed their phone number (moderate confidence in the evidence). Some patients, particularly women and adolescents, had their access to phones controlled by other people (moderate confidence in the evidence). The cost of messages may also be a problem, and many thought that messages should be free of charge (high confidence in the evidence). Language issues as well as skills in reading, writing, and using mobile phones may also be a problem (moderate confidence in the evidence). Patient-related health conditions such as HIV, family planning, or abortion care were also concerned about privacy and confidentiality (high confidence in the evidence). Some patients suggested strategies to work with these issues, such as using neutral language and tailoring the content, time, and frequency of messages (high confidence in the evidence). Patients wanted messages at a time and frequency that was convenient for them (moderate confidence in the evidence). They had preferences for different delivery channels (e.g. short message service (SMS) or interactive voice response) (moderate confidence in the evidence). They also had preferences about message content, including new knowledge, reminders, solutions, and suggestions about health issues (moderate confidence in the evidence). Patient-related health conditions such as HIV, family planning, or abortion care were also concerned about privacy and confidentiality (high confidence in the evidence). The studies included in this review were of low quality. The evidence is current to January 2016.

We included 21 studies involving 2467 people. The studies were of different quality, and the results were not similar between the two treatments. The studies showed that the medications used in the included studies are similar in terms of overall effectiveness, but symptoms experienced by participants differed due to the medication used and the program used. The studies included in this review show that slow tapering with temporary replacement of long- acting opioids, can reduce withdrawal severity. Nevertheless, the majority of patients relapsed to heroin use.

The review included nine studies (eight randomised controlled trials) involving 1109 participants. The results of this review suggest that sedation may be a significant factor in the intensity and duration of withdrawal, but the duration of anaesthesia may affect the severity of withdrawal. The quality of the evidence is low.

We included 14 studies. The duration of the studies ranged from very short (ten days) studies of the intramuscular preparation, to trials lasting over three months. We found no evidence of a difference in effectiveness between molindone and placebo. The study results suggest that molindone is not more or less likely than usual antipsychotics to cause movement disorders, but it does cause significantly more weight loss. The quality of the evidence involving this compound is limited, owing to small sample size, poor study design, limited outcomes and incomplete reporting.
