The review included 12 trials, which randomised 1319 participants. The quality of the studies was not high, including a high risk of detection bias in many of the trials. The results showed that a significant reduction in surgical failure in the first year after trabeculectomy was found in eyes at high risk of failure and those undergoing surgery for the first time receiving regular-dose 5Fu postoperative injections. No surgical failures were found in studies assessing combined surgery. No difference was found in the low-dose postoperative 5Fu injection group in patients undergoing primary trabeculectomy. Peroperative 5Fu in patients undergoing primary trabeculectomy significantly reduced risk of failure (RR 0.67, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.51 to 0.88). This translates to a number needed to treat for an additional beneficial outcome of 4.1 for the high risk of failure patients, and 5.0 for primary trabeculectomy patients receiving postoperative 5Fu. Intraocular pressure was also reduced in the primary trabeculectomy group receiving intraoperative 5Fu and regular-dose postoperative 5Fu. No significant change occurred in the primary trabeculectomy group receiving low-dose postoperative 5Fu. In addition, intraocular pressure was reduced in the high risk of failure group receiving regular-dose postoperative 5Fu. No difference was found in the combined surgery group. The review found that a significant reduction in surgical failure in the first year after trabeculectomy was found in eyes at high risk of failure and those undergoing surgery for the first time receiving regular-dose 5Fu postoperative injections. No surgical failures were found in studies evaluating combined surgery.

We included 80 randomised controlled trials (5820 women). The studies compared 20 different NSAIDs (18 non-selective and two COX-2-specific) versus placebo, paracetamol or each other. The evidence suggests that if 18% of women taking placebo achieve moderate or excellent pain relief, between 45% and 53% taking NSAIDs will do so. However, NSAIDs were associated with more adverse effects (overall adverse effects, gastrointestinal adverse effects, neurological adverse effects), which may be more serious than side effects. The evidence suggests that if 10% of women taking placebo experience side effects, between 11% and 14% of women taking NSAIDs will do so. NSAIDs versus other NSAIDs There was little evidence of the superiority of any individual NSAID for either pain relief or safety. However, the available evidence had little power to detect these differences, as most individual comparisons were based on very few small trials. NSAIDs versus paracetamol NSAIDs appeared to be more effective for pain relief than paracetamol. NSAIDs versus COX-2-specific inhibitors There was no evidence that COX-2-specific inhibitors were more effective or tolerable for the treatment of dysmenorrhoea than traditional NSAIDs; however data were very scanty. NSAIDs versus paracetamol NSAIDs were more effective for pain relief than paracetamol. However, the evidence is of low quality, as most of the included studies were based on very few small trials.

We included seven studies that compared high versus low levels of PEEP (2565 participants). In five of the studies (2417 participants), a comparison was made between high and low levels of PEEP with the same tidal volume in both groups, but in the remaining two studies (148 participants), the tidal volume was different between high- and low-level groups. We found no significant difference in mortality after hospital discharge. The data showed that high levels of PEEP did not reduce death before hospital discharge. The data also show that high levels of PEEP produced no significant difference in the risk of barotrauma, but more likely improved participants' oxygenation to the first, third, and seven days. This review suggests that the included studies were characterized by clinical heterogeneity.

We found nine studies that enrolled 682 participants. The studies were of low quality and did not report the group allocation methods. Seven studies compared Rheum officinale with no treatment and two compared Rheum officinale with captopril, an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor (ACEi). The studies showed that Rheum officinale had a positive effect on Cr and BUN levels in patients with CKD. However, the results were not significant for any outcome (BUN, CrCl, or patients' capacity to complete work). No data were available on all-cause mortality or cost of treatment. Only minor adverse events were reported in association with Rheum officinale. There is no current evidence to support any recommendation for its use in patients with CKD.

The review included four randomised clinical trials, involving 136 participants. Two trials compared lamivudine alone versus HBIg alone. The trials were performed one week after transplantation in one of the trials and after six months after transplantation in another; from transplantation to randomisation, HBIg alone was given to all patients in the two trials. A third trial compared combination treatment with lamivudine and HBIg versus lamivudine alone after one month of combination treatment, and a fourth trial compared the combination of lamivudine and HBIg versus a combination of lamivudine and adefovir dipivoxil after at least 12 months of lamivudine and HBIg combination treatment. Statistically significant differences were not found in any of the comparisons and outcomes. All trials were open-labelled, and no trials were adequately powered to show a difference in HBV recurrence. No meta-analysis was performed because the identified trials assessed different comparisons.

We searched for studies that compared sulphonylurea (SU) with insulin with or without metformin. We found 10 studies, involving 1019 participants who were followed between three months to 10 years (1060 randomised participants). The studies were of low quality and included a high number of participants. The results of this review suggest that sulphonylurea (Sulphonylurea (SU)) with insulin did not improve metabolic control more than insulin alone at three months and at 12 months of treatment and follow-up. SU (with or without metformin) had a worseer metabolic control compared to oral therapy. SU was also associated with an increased risk of insulin dependence. There was no evidence that SU influenced fasting C-peptide levels. However, insulin maintained stimulated C-peptide levels better than SU. One study comparing Chinese treatments with insulin showed stable fasting C-peptide levels in the vitamin D group but decreased fasting C-peptide levels in the vitamin D group. The results of this review are based on a small number of studies and the quality of the evidence is low.

We included three studies with a total of 492 participants who had received 530 THA. The evidence was of very low quality. One study (81 participants) compared outcomes for participants randomised to the provision of hip precautions, equipment and functional restrictions versus no provision of hip precautions, equipment or functional limitations. There were no incidences of hip dislocation or adverse events in either group during the initial 12 postoperative months. The study did not measure pain, global assessment of treatment success or total adverse events. One study (265 participants; 303 THAs) compared the provision of hip precautions with versus without the prescription of postoperative equipment and restrictions to functional activities. There was no perception of satisfaction in the rate of recovery compared to those prescribed equipment and restriction (113/152). There was no incidence of hip dislocation between participants provided with hip precautions with (1-152) compared to without providing equipment or restrictions post-THA (11/151). The study did not measure pain, function, health-related quality of life (HRQOL), re-operation rates or total adverse events. One study (146 participants) compared the provision of an improved postoperative education and rehabilitation service on hospital discharge to promote functional ADL versus a conventional rehabilitation intervention in the community. This study was of very low quality. We were uncertain if the provision of improved postoperative education and rehabilitation improved function at six months follow-up. The quality of the evidence was very low.

We found three randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that compared the use of LNG-IUD with other treatments for endometriosis. The evidence is current to January 2015. In two trials, the number of women who were satisfied with their treatment was also higher in the LNG-IUD group but did not reach statistical significance. The number of women reporting a change in menstruation was significantly higher in the LNG-IUD group but the number of women not completing the allocated treatment did not differ between groups. In one trial, women receiving LNG-IUD noted lower pain scores compared with women receiving gonadotrophin-releasing hormone agonists. There is limited but consistent evidence showing that postoperative LNG-IUD use reduces the recurrence of painful periods in women with endometriosis. Further well-designed RCTs are needed to confirm these findings.

We included 24 studies, with the most 20/24 giving concerns about risk of bias. All of the included studies investigated food products; no examined alcohol or tobacco. The majority were conducted in laboratory settings (14/24), with adult participants (17/24) and used between-participants designs (19/24). All studies were conducted in high-income countries, mainly in the USA (14/24). Six studies evaluated availability, of which two changed the total number of different options available, and four altered the relative proportion of less-healthy (to healthier) options. Most studies (4/6) manipulated snack foods or drinks. For selection outcomes, meta-analysis of three comparisons from three studies (n = 154) found that exposure to fewer options resulted in a large reduction in selection of the targeted food or drink. For consumption outcomes, meta-regression analyses showed that this effect was greater: the longer the food was placed, the more healthy the food was, and the more people were of low deprivation status. In addition, the longer the food was placed, the more healthy the food was, and the more people were of low deprivation status. The evidence is current to January 2016. Six studies assessed proximity interventions. Most (14/18) changed the distance at which a snack food or drink was placed from the participants, and four studies changed the order of meal components found on a line. For selection outcomes, meta-regression analyses showed that this effect was greater: the longer the food was placed, the more healthy the food was, and the more people were of low deprivation status. The evidence is current to February 2015. Four studies evaluated proximity interventions. Most (14/18) changed the distance at which a snack food or drink was placed from the participants, and four studies changed the order of meal components found on a line. For selection outcomes, meta-regression analyses showed that this effect was greater: the longer the food was placed, the more healthy the food was, and the more people were of low deprivation status. The evidence is current to October 2017.

The review found no randomized controlled trials that evaluated the effect of PEP on HIV serosis. However, there was a significant difference in the rate at which cases and control groups were given a combination of antiretroviral drugs after exposure to HIV. However, cases had significantly lower risk of having taken zidovudine after exposure compared to control groups. There was no evidence to support the use of multi-drug antiretroviral regimens after occupational exposure to HIV. However, due to the success of combination therapies in treating HIV-infected people, a combination of antiretroviral drugs should be used for PEP. Health care workers should be counseled about expected adverse events and the strategies for managing these. They should also be advised that PEP is not 100% effective in preventing HIV serosis.

The review found that CBT is effective in improving pain, disability, mood and catastrophising, but not on pain or mood. CBT has small to moderate effects on pain, disability, mood and catastrophising immediately post-treatment when compared with treatment as usual/waiting list, but all but a small effect on mood had disappeared at follow-up. At present there are insufficient data on the quality or content of treatment to investigate their effect on outcome. The quality of the trial design has improved over time but the quality of treatments has not. The results of this review suggest that CBT is a useful approach to the management of chronic pain.

We searched for studies that compared the effects of the adolescence and age-related changes in salt intake. We found 15 countries with a total of 260,798 participants. The studies were conducted in a total of 10 countries. The studies included a total of a total of a total of 66,798 people. Five of the studies showed a total of 11 grams/day less salt intake per person from pre-intervention to post-intervention. Two studies showed a total of 110 grams/day more salt intake per person in Canada and Switzerland. The other 10 studies did not show a total of a 115 grams/day less salt intake. Seven of the 10 interventions were multi-component and incorporated intervention activities of a structural nature (e.g. food product reformulation, food procurement policy in specific settings). Of these seven interventions, four showed a statistically significant average daily salt intake from pre-intervention to post-intervention, ranging from Finland to Ireland, and one showed a statistically significant average increase in salt intake from pre-intervention to post-intervention. Nine of the 10 interventions provided data for quantitative analysis of differences in salt intake by sex (men and women separately). For women, three interventions (China, Finland, France) showed a statistically significant average decrease in salt intake, four (Australia, Netherlands, Switzerland, United Kingdom) showed no significant change and two (Canada, United States) showed a statistically significant average increase in salt intake from pre-intervention to post-intervention. For men, five interventions (Austria, Netherlands, Switzerland, United States) showed a statistically significant average increase in salt intake from pre-intervention to post-intervention. For men, five interventions (Australia, Netherlands, Switzerland, United States) showed a statistically significant average increase in salt intake from pre-intervention to post-intervention. The evidence is current to January 2015.

We found seven studies that compared the drugs with placebo. The studies were small and included a small number of participants, ranging from seven to 16 people per study. The studies compared the drugs with placebo or no treatment. The results were imprecise and consistent with benefit, no difference or harm. The results for each of the drugs were consistent with benefit, no difference or harm. The results for amitriptyline, bromocriptine, clonidine, propranolol and levodopa were compared with placebo. The results were imprecise and consistent with benefit, no difference or harm.

We found 10 randomized controlled trials with 1015 participants. The studies compared an intravenous formula or additional supplemental omega-3 fatty acids (i.e. eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA), docosahexaenoic acid (DHA)), gamma-linolenic acid (GLA), and antioxidants. The evidence is current to January 2016. The studies were different in design, quality, and duration. The studies were different in the type of interventions given, a number of types of antibiotics, and the number of days that the patients were in the intensive care unit. The studies were different in the type of treatment given, the number of days that the patients were in the intensive care unit, and the number of days that the patients were in the ventilator. The evidence is current to February 2015. We are uncertain whether omega-3 fatty acids and antioxidants reduce the length of stay in the intensive care unit and the time that the patients are in the ventilator. We are also uncertain whether omega-3 fatty acids and antioxidants improve oxygenation, or whether they increase adverse events such as heart events, gastrointestinal events, or total adverse events. The quality of the evidence was low or very low for all outcomes.

We found 33 studies that compared different methods of elicitation. The studies compared the use of open questions with checklist-type questions or rating scales. Two studies included participant interviews. The studies compared different methods of elicitation, and the results showed that more specific questioning of participants led to more AEs reported. A subset of six studies suggested that more severe, bothersome, or other clinically relevant AEs were reported when an initial open question was used, while some less severe, bothersome, or clinically relevant AEs were only reported with a follow-up specific question. However, two studies showed that very severe or debilitating AEs were only reported by an interview, but other studies did not find a difference in the nature of AEs between elicitation methods. There was a risk for under-detection of AEs in studies using a more general elicitation method compared to those using a comprehensive method. These AEs may be important from a clinical point of point of view or for patients. This under-detection may cause failure to combine data. However, the effect on the nature of the AE reported by different methods is unclear.

We searched the literature for studies that compared nocturnal supervision with no supervision in people with epilepsy. We found one study that compared nocturnal supervision with no supervision in people with epilepsy. The study included 154 people with epilepsy and 616 people with epilepsy. The study found that nocturnal supervision was effective in preventing SUDEP. However, the quality of the evidence was very low. Further research is needed to identify the effectiveness of other current interventions, for example seizure detection devices, safety pillows, SSRIs, early surgical evaluation, educational programmes, and opiate and adenosine antagonists in preventing SUDEP in people with epilepsy.

The review included seven studies with a total of 208 participants. The study duration varied from four days to two years. The age of participants ranged from seven to 63 years. Six studies enrolled participants who were clinically stable, while participants in one study had been hospitalised with an infective exacerbation. All studies compared autogenic drainage to one (or more) other known airway clearance technique. Exercise is commonly used as an alternative therapy by people with cystic fibrosis; however, there were no studies identified comparing exercise with autogenic drainage. The review's primary outcome, forced expiratory volume in one second was the most common outcome measured and was reported by all seven studies; only three studies reported on quality of life (as well as a primary outcome of the review). One study reported on adverse events and reported a decrease in oxygen saturation levels whilst performing active cycle of breathing techniques, but not with autogenic drainage. Six of the seven included studies measured required vital capacity and three of the studies used mid peak expiratory flow (per cent predicted) as an outcome. Six studies reported sputum weight. More research is needed to better evaluate autogenic drainage in comparison to other airway clearance techniques in view.

The review included 25 studies (in a total of 1305 participants) that compared psychological therapy with other psychological therapies. The evidence is current to January 2016. The review found that psychological therapy, all using a CBT approach, was more effective than TAU/WL in achieving clinical response at post-treatment and also in reducing anxiety, worry and depression symptoms. No studies conducted longer-term evaluations of CBT against TAU/WL. Six studies compared CBT with supportive therapy (non-directive therapy and attention-placebo conditions). No significant difference in clinical response was identified between CBT and support therapy, but the number of therapy sessions was not significant. Further studies examining non-CBT models are needed to inform health care policy on the most appropriate forms of psychological therapy in treating GAD.

We included 12 trials (one,856 women) that compared progestogen treatment with placebo or no treatment. The trials were conducted in India, Jordan, UK and USA. In five trials women had had three or more consecutive miscarriages and in seven trials women had had two or more consistent miscarriages. The trials were a mix of multicenter and single-center trials, conducted in India, Jordan, UK and USA. In five trials women had had three or more consecutive miscarriages and in seven trials women had had two or more consecutive miscarriages. The results of the review suggest that there may be a reduction in the number of miscarriages for women given progestogen supplementation compared to placebo/controls. A subgroup analysis comparing placebo-controlled versus non-placebo-controlled trials, trials of women with three or more prior miscarriages compared to women with two or more miscarriages and different routes of administration showed no clear differences between subgroups for miscarriage. No evidence was found on any secondary maternal outcomes, including severity of morning sickness, thromboembolic events, depression, admission to a special care unit, or subsequent fertility. There was likely a small benefit for women receiving progestogen seen in the outcome of live birth rate. We are uncertain about the effect on the rate of preterm birth because the evidence is very low-quality. No clear differences were seen for women receiving progestogen for the other secondary outcomes including newborn death, fetal genital abnormalities or stillbirth. There may be little or no difference in the rate of low birthweight and trials did not report on the secondary child outcomes of teratogenic effects or admission to a teratogenic unit.

We found 31 studies (44 reports) involving 27071 participants. The evidence is current to January 2016. The transradial approach reduced short-term adverse clinical events (no-adverse clinical events) and heart death, but the risk of myocardial infarction was similar between both groups. The transradial approach had a lower procedureal success rate, but was associated with a lower risk of all-cause mortality, bleeding, and access site complications. There is insufficient evidence about the long-term clinical outcomes (i.e. after 30 days of follow-up).

We found two studies of palliative care interventions for people with advanced dementia. The two studies measured 31 different outcomes, but they did not measure the same outcome. We found three randomised trials involving 1166 women with breast cancer. The trials were conducted in the USA, the USA, the USA and the USA. The trials compared two different methods of sequencing CT and RT. The trials did not show any significant differences between the different methods of sequencing. The trials did not show any difference between the two types of sequencing in the number of women who had recurrence or survival. The results of one trial (244 women) suggest that RT before imaging was associated with an increased risk of neutropenic sepsis compared with imaging after RT, but other measures of toxicity did not differ. The results of this review suggest that different methods of sequencing CT and RT do not appear to have a major effect on recurrence or survival for women with breast cancer if RT is commenced within seven months after surgery.

We searched for studies that compared CrP with placebo or other treatments for weight loss in overweight or obese adults. We found nine studies involving 622 participants. The studies were conducted in the community setting, with interventions mainly delivered by health professionals, and had a short- to moderate-term follow up (up to 24 weeks). Three studies compared CrP plus resistance or weight training with placebo plus resistance or weight training, the other studies compared CrP alone versus placebo. We found that CrP was effective in reducing body weight in the long term, but there was no clear evidence of a difference between CrP and placebo for other weight loss measures (body weight, body mass index, percentage body fat composition, change in weight circumference). There were two serious adverse events and study dropouts in participants taking 1000 g CrP, and one serious adverse event in an individual taking 400 g CrP. Two participants receiving placebo discontinued due to adverse events; one event was reported as serious. No study reported on all-cause mortality, morbidity, health-related quality of life or socioeconomic effects. We found no current, reliable evidence to inform firm decisions about the efficacy and safety of CrP supplements in overweight or obese adults.

The evidence is current to January 2015. We included 11 studies with a total of 886 participants. The studies evaluated a range of comparisons in a range of surgical wounds healing by secondary intention. In general studies were small and some did not present data or analyses that could be easily interpreted or related to clinical outcomes. These factors reduced the quality of the evidence. Two comparisons compared different iodine preparations with no antiseptic treatment and found no clear evidence of effects for these treatments. The outcome data available were limited and how evidence was low quality. One study compared a zinc oxide mesh dressing with a plain mesh dressing. There was no clear evidence of a difference in time to wound healing between groups. There was some evidence of a difference in measures used to assess wound infection (wound with foul smell and number of participants prescribed antibiotics) which favoured the zinc oxide group. One study reported that sucralfate cream increased the likelihood of healing open wounds after haemorrhoidectomy compared to a petrolatum cream. This evidence was graded as being of moderate quality. The study also reported lower wound pain scores in the sucralfate group. There was a reduction in time to healing of open wounds following haemorrhoidectomy when treated with Triclosan post-operatively compared with a standard sodium hypochlorite solution. There was moderate quality evidence that more open wounds resulting from excision of pyomyositis abscesses healed when treated with a honey-soaked gauze compared with a EUSOL-soaked gauze over three weeks' follow-up. There was also some evidence of a reduction in the average length of hospital stay in the honey group. Evidence was taken from one small study that only had 43 participants. There was moderate quality evidence that more Dermacym®-treated post-operative foot wounds healed when treated with a honey-soaked gauze compared with a EUSOL-soaked gauze. We included five randomised trials with total of 1049 women evaluating five different technique changes during either amniocentesis (three studies) or CVS (two studies). For amniocentesis three interventions were evaluated - intramuscular progesterone, hexoprenaline and selecting high or low puncture sites for late 'blind' procedure - each intervention in a single small study. There was no conclusive evidence of benefit for any of them. The same is the case for terbutaline tocolysis and use of continuous vacuum aspiration during CVS. Overall, the quality of evidence summised in this review is not of sufficient quality to change current clinical practice. In the absence of clear evidence, the operators should continue to use methods and technique modifications with which they are most known with. The evidence is current to January 2015.

We included 10 studies, with a total of 437 participants. The studies were conducted in upper-middle-income countries (China, Mexico, South Africa), one study was conducted in a lower-middle-income country (Bangladesh) and three studies were conducted in a high-income country (Caas). Seven studies examined wheat flour fortified with folic acid alone or with other micronutrients. Three studies included maize flour fortified with folic acid alone or with other micronutrients. The duration of interventions ranged from two weeks to 36 months, and the ITS studies included postfortification periods of up to seven years. Most studies had unclear risk of bias for randomisation, blinding, and reporting, and low/unclear risk of attrition and contamination. The evidence is current to January 2016. No studies reported neural tube defects. In one non-RCT, wheat flour fortified with folic acid and other micronutrients was associated with significantly lower occurrence of total neurotubule defects, spina bifida, and encephalocoele, but not anencephaly, compared to unfortified flour. Using folic acid-fortified maize porridge, pregnant women who received folic acid-fortified maize recombinant erythrocyte folate levels and blood folate levels were more likely to have a recombinant erthrocyte folate level, compared to unfortified flour. Using folic acid-fortified maize recombinant erthrocyte folate levels and blood folate levels were more likely to have a recombinant erthrocyte folate level, compared to unfortified flour. The evidence is current to February 2015.

We found two randomised controlled trials involving 1124,483 newborns (210 with CF) with a total follow up of 17 years. The studies were of low quality and the results were not well reported. The results suggest that screening is less expensive than traditional diagnosis.

The review of trials found that non-removable casts are more effective in treating diabetes-related plantar foot ulcers than removable casts, or dressings alone. The use of a non-removable cast, when combined with Achilles tendon lengthening, was more effective in one forefoot ulcer study than the use of a non-removable cast alone.

We found six trials involving 343 participants. The evidence is current to January 2015. We included five trials, all from the 1970s, randomised 343 participants. We excluded one trial. The overall methodology and data reporting by the trials was poor. Only short-term data were available. Results from the included trials showed chlorpromazine and piperacetazine may have similar clinical efficacy, but data are based on very small numbers of participants and the evidence is very low quality. We can not make firm conclusions based on this data. Currently, should clinicians and people with schizophrenia need to choose between chlorpromazine and piperacetazine they should be aware there is no good quality evidence to base decisions. More high quality research is needed.

We found five randomised trials with a total of 207 participants, 102 to colorectal stenting and 105 to emergency surgery. The clinical success rate was statistically higher in emergency surgery group. However, use of colorectal stents seems to be as safe in the malignant colorectal obstruction as the emergency surgery with no statistically significant difference in the mortality and morbidity. Colorectal stents are associated with acceptable stent perforation, migration and obstruction rates. The benefits of colorectal stent includes shorter hospital stay and procedure time and less blood loss. However, due to the variability in the sample size and trial design in the included studies, further randomised trials with larger sample size and well defined trial design are needed to achieve the robust evidence.

The review included 10 studies involving 484 patients. There was no evidence suggesting superior efficacy of any one sclerosant over another, but there was evidence of superiority of sclerotherapy to placebo. The evidence did not suggest an increase in patient satisfaction with any one agent versus other, but there was evidence that patients were less satisfied with placebo. There was some evidence suggesting that polidocanol (POL) was more likely to cause adverse reactions at a concentration of 1% compared with lower concentrations or hypertonic saline, and that sodium tetradecyl sulfate (STS) was more likely to cause adverse reactions at a concentration of 1% compared with POL at 0.5%. There was some evidence suggesting that STS was more pain-related than POL, heparsal (20% saline mixed with heparin 100 units/mL) or placebo, and that POL was no more pain-related than placebo. Evidence from one study suggested that hypertonic saline (HS) was more pain-related than POL. The data were not suitable for meta-analysis. The evidence did not suggest superior efficacy or patient satisfaction for any one sclerosant over another, but there was evidence of superiority of sclerotherapy to placebo. The evidence did not suggest an increase in patient satisfaction with any one agent versus another, but there was evidence that patients were less satisfied with placebo. There was some evidence suggesting that polidocanol (POL) was more likely to cause adverse reactions at a concentration of 1% compared with lower concentrations or hypertonic saline, and that sodium tetradecyl sulfate (STS) was more likely to cause adverse reactions at a concentration of 1% compared with POL at 0.5%. There was some evidence suggesting that STS was more painful than POL, heparsal (20% saline mixed with heparin 100 units/mL) or placebo, and that POL was no more pain-related than placebo. Evidence from one study suggested that hypertonic saline (HS) was more pain-related than POL. The data were not suitable for meta-analysis.

We found three randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that compared the effect of sweet potato preparations with placebo. The trials were conducted in the USA, the USA, the USA and the USA. The trials compared the effect of sweet potato preparations with placebo or no treatment. The trials compared the effect of sweet potato preparations with placebo or no treatment. The trials were of low quality and the results were not clear. There was a significant improvement in glucose control at three to five months with 4 g/day sweet potato preparation compared to placebo. No serious adverse effects were reported. Diabetes complications and morbidity, death from any cause, health-related quality of life, well-being, functional outcomes and costs were not investigated. There is insufficient evidence about the use of sweet potato for type 2 diabetes mellitus. In addition to improvement in trial methodology, issues of standardization and quality control of preparations - including other types of sweet potato - need to be addressed. Further observational trials and RCTs evaluating the effects of sweet potato are needed to guide any recommendations in clinical practice.

We searched for studies that compared the active cycle of breathing technique with other therapies. We found 19 studies, of which 440 participants were included in the review. Five randomised controlled trials (192 participants) were included in the meta-analysis; three were of cross-over design. The 14 remaining studies were cross-over studies with inadequate reports for total assessment. The study size ranged from seven to 65 participants. The age of the participants ranged from six to 63 years (mean age 22.33 years). In 13 studies, follow up lasted a single day. However, there were two long-term randomised controlled trials with follow up of one to three years. Most of the studies did not report on key quality items, and therefore have an unclear risk of bias in terms of random sequence development, allocation concealment, and outcome assessor blinding. Due to the nature of the intervention, no of the studies blinded participants or the people using the interventions. However, most of the studies reported on all planned outcomes, had adequate follow up, assessed compliance, and used an intention-to-treat analysis. The studies compared the active cycle of breathing technique with autogenic drainage, airway oscillating devices, high frequency chest compression devices, conventional chest physiotherapy, and positive expiratory pressure. The quality of the evidence was unclear. Most of the studies did not report on key quality factors, and therefore have an unclear risk of bias in terms of random sequence formation, allocation concealment, and outcome assessor blinding. However, most of the studies reported on all planned outcomes, had adequate follow up, evaluated compliance, and used an intention-to-treat analysis.

The review included eight studies with 733 women in total. The evidence is current to January 2015. The review showed that short co-incubation of sperm and oocytes may improve the ongoing pregnancy and clinical pregnancy rates for infertile women undergoing IVF cycles. More studies are needed to assess whether short co-incubation will contribute to a higher live birth rate and a lower miscarriage rate compared to the standard overnight insemination protocol.

The review of studies found that a long iteration of the ventilator was associated with an increase in air leaks and deaths. However, there was no significant difference in the incidence of BPD. The results of this review should be used with caution in the use of long iterations of the ventilator in the current world of newborn intensive care.

We included 33 studies involving 5110 patients. The studies were of low methodological quality, which is a difference between the quality of the evidence and the number of participants in the studies. There was no evidence that TFU was effective for reducing the risk of a disease. However, there were some studies that found that TFU had favourable effects for some outcomes, but in the overall studies there were no adverse effects reported.

We found 38 trials that compared the effectiveness of different strategies to improve the retention of participants in trials. The trials were conducted in a range of disease areas, countries, health care and community settings. The results of this review suggest that the addition of a monetary incentive to the delivery of questionnaires may improve the retention of participants in trials. The use of a 'package' of postal communication strategies with reminder letters may be better than standard practices. The use of 'enhanced' letters or letters delivered by primary post may be better than standard methods. The evidence is current to January 2015. The evidence is current to February 2016. The evidence is current to May 2014. The evidence is current to October 2017. The evidence is current to September 2018.

We included 19 studies, reported in 27 papers with a total of 1453 participants. The studies were conducted in eight different countries and were recruited largely from gastrointestinal clinics. The average age at recruitment ranged from 6.3 years to 13.1 years. Girls outnumbered boys in most trials. Fourteen studies recruited children with a diagnosis under the broad umbrella of RAP or functional gastrointestinal disorders; five studies specifically recruited only children with irritable bowel syndrome. The studies were conducted in four groups: trials of probiotic-based interventions (13 studies), trials of fibre-based interventions (four studies), trials of low FODMAP (fermentable oligosaccharides, disaccharide, monosaccharides and polyols) diets (one study), and trials of fructose-restricted diets (one study). We found that children treated with probiotics reported a greater reduction in pain frequency at one to three months postintervention than those given placebo. There was also a decrease in pain intensity at the same time point. However, we judged the evidence for these outcomes to be of low quality due to an unclear risk of bias from incomplete outcome data and significant heterogeneity. We found that children treated with probiotics were more likely to experience improvement in pain at one to three months postintervention than those given placebo. The estimated number needed to treat for an additional beneficial outcome (NNTB) was eight, which means that eight children would need to receive probiotics for one to experience improvement in pain in this timescale. We judged the evidence for this outcome to be of moderate quality due to significant differences between the studies.

We included 85 studies in our review. The studies took place in 41 countries, including eight high-income countries, 18 middle-income countries and 15 low-income countries, in rural, urban and sub-urban locations. We developed 52 findings in total and grouped these into three thematic domains: socio-cultural context (11 findings, five moderate- or high-confidence), service design and provision (24 findings, 15 moderate- or high-confidence) and how important it is to women and health providers (17 findings, 11 moderate- or high-confidence). The third domain was sub-divided into two conceptual areas; personalised support, and information and safety. We also developed two lines of evidence, using high- or moderate-confidence findings: For women, initial or continued use of ANC depends on a perception that doing so will be a positive experience. This is a result of the delivery of good-quality local services that are not dependent on the payment of informal fees and that include continuity of care that is authentically personalised, good, caring, supportive, culturally sensitive, flexible, and respecting women's need for privacy, and that allow staff to take the time needed to provide relevant support, information and clinical safety for the woman and the baby, as and when they need it. Women's perceptions of the quality of ANC depend on their general beliefs about pregnancy as a healthy or a risky state, and on their response to being pregnant, as well as on local socio-cultural norms involving the benefits or otherwise of antenatal care for healthy pregnancies, and for women with complications. Whether they continue to use ANC or not depends on their experience of ANC design and provision when they access it for the first time. The capacity of healthcare providers to deliver the type of high-quality, relationship-based, locally available ANC that is likely to facilitate access by women depends on the provision of sufficient resources and staffing as well as the time to provide flexible personalised, private appointments that are not overloaded with organisational tasks. This provision also depends on organisational norms and values.

The review included 16 trials, involving 3361 patients. The evidence for 'poor outcome' is not beyond all doubt, but given the potential benefits and moderate risks of this treatment, oral nimodipine is currently indicated in patients with aneurysmal SAH. In addition to nimodipine, magnesium sulphate is a promising agent but more evidence is needed before definite conclusions can be drawn.

We found eight randomised clinical trials (involving 632 participants) that were at high risk of bias. The average age ranged from 16 years to 78 years. The proportion of men ranged from 60% to 75% and the proportion of people with stage III primary hepatic cancer ranged from 22% to 85%. The total follow-up duration was 12 months (2 months to 38 months). The number of deaths at three years was 552. The number of people with tumour response was complete response plus partial response. The number of people with health-related quality of life was favoured by the TACE followed by 3DCRT group, but the reported data were ill-defined. No of the trials reported serious adverse events. The rate of participants with serum alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) without decline or normalisation was significantly lower in the TACE followed by 3DCRT group than in the TACE alone group. We found no randomised controlled trials that examined the effects of increased versus standard energy intake for preterm infants with (or developing) CLD/BPD. However, two included trials provided some insights into the topic. One study showed that infants with CLD/BPD who were fed formula enriched with protein and minerals had improved growth parameters up to the cessation of the intervention at three months of corrected age. The other study compared different energy density of formula but identical energy intake by setting different feed volumes for both groups. It showed that both groups were unable to achieve the pre-designated feed volume and that there were no differences in growth, respiratory outcomes, oedema and the diuretic requirements. To date, no randomised controlled trials are available that examine the effects of increased versus standard energy intake for preterm infants with (or developing) CLD/BPD. Research should be directed at evaluating the effects of different levels of energy intake on this group of infants on clinically important outcomes such as mortality, breathing status, growth and neurodevelopment. The benefits and harms of different ways of increasing energy intake, including higher energy density of milk feed and/or fluid volume (clinically realistic target volume should be set), parenteral nutrition, and the use of different components of energy like carbohydrate, protein and fat for this purpose also need to be assessed.

We searched for studies that compared two-dimensional ultrasound with an anatomical landmark technique for subclavian (arterial puncture, haematoma) and femoral vein (femoral vein). We found 13 studies involving 2341 participants. The quality of the evidence was very low (subclavian vein N = 3) or low (subclavian vein N = 4 femoral vein N = 2) for most outcomes, moderate for one outcome (femoral vein N = 2) and high at best for two outcomes (subclavian vein N = 1 femoral vein N = 1) for most outcomes. For the subclavian vein (nine studies, 2130 participants, 2049 procedures), two-dimensional ultrasound reduced the risk of inadvertent blood clots and haematoma formation (three studies, 498 participants), no evidence was found of a difference in total or other complications (together, US, USD), overall (together, US, USD), number of attempts until success (US) or first-time (US) success rates or time taken to insert the catheter (US). For the femoral vein, fewer data were available for analysis (four studies, 311 participants, 311 procedures). No evidence was found of a difference in inadvertent blood clots or other complications. However, success on the first attempt was more likely with ultrasound (three studies, 224 participants, 224 procedures). No data on mortality or participant-reported outcomes were provided. On the basis of available data, we conclude that two-dimensional ultrasound provides small benefits in safety and quality when compared with an anatomical landmark technique for subclavian (arterial puncture, haematoma).

We included 15 studies involving 14 different methods of administration of physostigmine. Four studies, 29 people, used intravenous infusion; seven, 131 people, used a conventional oral form; four, 1456 people, used a controlled-release oral form, and one study of 181 people used a verum skin patch. The most important results from the trials of the conventional oral form showed no benefit of physostigmine compared with placebo. The most significant results from the trials of the controlled-release oral form are associated with improvement on the ADAS-Cog score at 6 weeks. There were statistically significantly higher numbers of patients from the physostigmine group withdrawing from the trial due to adverse events (nasal vomiting, diarrhoea, anorexia, dizziness, stomach pain, tremor, asthenia or sweating) compared with placebo at 12 weeks. In the trial with no attempt to identify responders and all relevant patients with Alzheimer's disease were randomized, fixed dose physostigmine was associated with a statistically significantly higher number withdrawaling (2334/358 versus 31/117). In the trial with no attempt to identify responders and all relevant patients with Alzheimer's disease were randomized, fixed dose physostigmine was associated with a statistically significantly higher number withdrawaling (31/117). In the trial with no attempt to identify responders and all relevant patients with Alzheimer's disease were randomized, fixed dose physostigmine was associated with a statistically significantly higher number withdrawaling (32/358 versus 31/117).

We found 12 studies (2494 participants: 1586 children and 908 old people) comparing amantadine and rimantadine with placebo, paracetamol or zanamivir to treat influenza A. Amantadine was effective in preventing influenza A in children (773 participants). The assumed risk of influenza A in the control group was 10 per 100. The corresponding risk in the rimantadine group was one per 100. However, the quality of the evidence was low and the safety of the drug was not well established. For treatment, rimantadine was beneficial in abating fever on day three of treatment in children: one included study with low risk of bias, moderate evidence quality and 69 participants. The assumed risk was 38 per 100. The corresponding risk in the rimantadine group was 14 per 100. Rimantadine did not show any prophylactic effect in the elderly. The quality of the evidence was very low: 103 participants. The assumed risk was 17 per 100. The corresponding risk in the rimantadine group was 7 per 100. There was no evidence of adverse effects caused by treatment with amantadine or rimantadine. We found no studies assessing amantadine in the old.

We found nine studies, which included a total of 1512 women. Most of these studies included women with unxplained infertility. In seven studies the women were undergoing IUI and in two studies the women were trying to conceive from sexual intercourse. Eight studies compared intentional endometrial injury with no injury/placebo procedure; of these two studies also compared intentional endometrial injury in the cycle prior to IUI with intentional endometrial injury in the IUI cycle. One study compared higher vs. lower degree of intentional endometrial injury. Intentional endometrial injury versus either no intervention or a sham procedure We are uncertain whether endometrial injury improves live birth/ongoing pregnancy as the quality of the evidence has been assessed as very low. The average pain experienced by participants undergoing endometrial injury was 6/10 on a one-to-one visual analogue scale (VAS) (standard deviation = 1.5). However, only one study reported this outcome. Higher vs. lower level of intentional endometrial injury When we compared hysteroscopy with endometrial injury to hysteroscopy alone, there was no evidence of a difference in ongoing pregnancy rate (low quality evidence) or clinical pregnancy rate (low quality evidence). The average pain experienced by participants undergoing endometrial injury was 6/10 on a one-to-one visual analogue scale (VAS) (standard deviation = 1.5). However, only one study reported this outcome. High vs. lower level of intentional endometrial injury Intentional endometrial injury versus either no intervention or a sham procedure We are uncertain whether endometrial injury improves live birth/ongoing pregnancy as the quality of the evidence has been assessed as very low. The evidence is of low quality.

We included four randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with 3905 participants. The studies compared professional oral care with usual oral care. The studies were at high risk of bias. The studies compared professional oral care with usual oral care. The studies were not able to determine whether professional oral care reduced the risk of pneumonia-associated mortality compared with usual oral care. The evidence is current to January 2016. We found no high-quality evidence to determine which oral care measures are most effective for reducing nursing home-acquired lung disease. Further trials are needed to draw reliable conclusions.

The review of trials found that the use of self-monitoring of blood glucose was effective in reducing the risk of diabetes in patients with diabetes who had a duration of one year or more. However, the effects of SMBG were not significant for patients with diabetes who had a duration of one year or more. The review found that the cost of SMBG was more expensive than the costs of standardized usual care. The review found that the use of self-monitoring of blood glucose was more expensive than the costs of standardized usual care. The review included 11 studies involving 471 participants with AsPD. However, data were available from only five studies involving 276 participants. Only two studies compared the effects of different psychological interventions on relapse and aggression. The studies were not well conducted and the results were not clear. The evidence is current to January 2015. Three interventions (contingency management with standard maintenance; cognitive behaviour therapy with standard maintenance; 'Driving Whilst Intoxicated program' with incarceration) showed improvement in at least one outcome in at least one study. The studies were not well conducted and the results were not clear. The review found that there was insufficient trial evidence to support the use of any psychological intervention for adults with AsPD.

The review included 11 studies. Six studies included only children, or only adults, or only males; a large range of antibiotics were used and outcomes were measured in different ways. There was a moderate risk of bias because of unreported methods details or because an unknown number of participants were likely to have chest or sinus infections. Participants receiving antibiotics for the common cold did not better in terms of lack of cure or persistence of symptoms than those on placebo (random-effects) based on a total of 1047 participants. The risk of adverse effects in the antibiotic group was 1.8, 95% confidence interval (CI) 1.01 to 3.21, (random-effects) but there was no greater risk in children (RR 0.91, 95% CI 0.51 to 1.63). The risk of persisting acute purulent rhinitis with antibiotics compared to placebo was 1.3, 95% CI 0.47 to 1.13) but there was an increase in adverse effects in adults when given for the common cold and in all ages when given for acute purulent rhinitis. There is no evidence of benefit from antibiotics for the common cold or for persisting acute purulent rhinitis in children or adults. There is evidence that antibiotics cause significant adverse effects in adults when given for the common cold and in all ages when given for acute purulent rhinitis.

We found 62 studies with 4241 participants. The studies compared morphine modified release (Mm/r) with other opioids. Six studies compared MIR with other opioids. Two studies compared oral Mm/r with rectal Mm/r. Three studies compared MIR with MIR by a different route of administration. Two studies compared MMI with MIR given at different times. One study was found comparing each of the following: Mm/r tablet with Mm/r suspension; Mm/r with non-opioids; MIR with non-opioids; and oral morphine with epidural morphine. We found that morphine is an effective analgesic for cancer pain. Pain relief did not differ between Mm/r and MIR.

We searched for studies that compared open surgery with steroid injections. We found 14 studies, totalling 1260 participants, with 1361 trigger fingers. The age of participants included in the studies ranged from 16 to 88 years; and the most participants were women (about 70%). The average duration of symptoms ranged from three to 15 months; and the follow-up after the procedure ranged from eight weeks to 23 months. The studies reported nine types of comparisons: open surgery versus steroid injections (two studies), percutaneous surgery versus steroid injection (five studies), open surgery versus steroid injection plus ultrasound-guided hyaluronic acid injection (one study), percutaneous surgery plus steroid injection versus steroid injection (one study), percutaneous surgery versus open surgery (five studies), endoscopic surgery versus open surgery (one study), and three comparisons of types of incision for open surgery (transverse incision of the skin in the distal palmar crease, transverse incision of the skin about 2-3 mm distally from distal palmar crease, and longitudinal incision of the skin) (one study). Most studies had significant methodological flaws and were considered at high or unclear risk of selection bias, performance bias, detection bias and reporting bias. The primary comparison was open surgery versus steroid injections, because open surgery is the oldest and the most widely used treatment method and considered as standard surgery, whereas steroid injection is the least invasive control treatment method as reported in the studies in this review and is often used as first-line treatment in clinical practice. Compared with steroid injection, there was low-quality evidence that open surgery provides benefits with respect to less triggering recurrence, but it has the disadvantage of being more pain-related. Evidence was downgraded due to study design flaws and imprecision.

The review of three randomised controlled trials (RCTs) found that RT plus PCV may improve the survival of people with AO or AOA. However, the results of this trial were not reported in the original review. The review found that the use of biomarkers to identify people with increased sensitivity to RT plus PCV is important for the development of new treatments for AO and AOA.

We found 21 studies with a total of 1525 participants. The studies were of low risk of bias for randomisation and allocation concealment. One small study showed that biofeedback plus exercises was better than exercise alone. One small study showed that adding biofeedback to electrical stimulation was better than electrical stimulation alone. The combined data of two trials showed that the number of people failing to achieve full continence was significantly lower when electrical stimulation was added to biofeedback compared against biofeedback alone. Sacral nerve stimulation was better than conservative management which included biofeedback and PFMT (at 12 months the incontinence episodes were significantly fewer with sacral nerve stimulation). There was not enough evidence as to whether there was a difference in outcome between any method of biofeedback or exercises. There are suggestions that rectal volume discrimination training improves continence more than sham training. Further conclusions are not needed from the available data.

We searched for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that compared the use of a urinary catheter with not using one. We found 39 trials. The trials were generally small and of low or moderate quality reporting data on only few outcomes. The results of five trials were heterogeneous but tended to suggest a higher risk of (re)catheterisation if a catheter was not used postoperatively. The data given only an imprecise estimate of any difference in urinary tract infection. Urethral catheterisation versus suprapubic catheterisation In six trials, a greater number of people needed to be recatheterised if a urethral catheter than a suprapubic one was used following surgery. Shorter postoperative duration of catheter use versus longer duration In 11 trials, the seven trials with data showed fewer urinary tract infections when a catheter was removed earlier (for example 1 versus 3 days). In 11 trials, the seven trials with data showed fewer urinary tract infections when a catheter was removed earlier (for example 1 versus 3 days). Clamp and release policies before catheter removal versus immediate catheter removal In a single small trial, the clamp-and-release group showed a significantly increased incidence of urinary tract infections (RR 4.00, 95% 1.55 to 1029). Despite reviewing 39 eligible trials, few firm conclusions could be found because of the multiple comparisons considered, the small size of individual trials, and their low quality. Whether or not to use a particular policy is usually a trade-off between the risks of morbidity (especially infection) and risks of recatheterization.

We found no evidence of significant difference between urokinase or ethanol lock treatments with concomitant systemic antibiotics and systemic antibiotics alone for the number of participants cured, the number of recurrent CVC-related infections, the number of days until the first negative blood culture, the number of CVCs prematurely removed, ICU admission and sepsis. The studies were small and had methodological limitations. No studies were published on antibiotic lock treatment alone. More well-designed trials are needed to further explore the effect of antibiotic or other lock treatments in the treatment of CVC-related infections in children with cancer.

The review of trials found that there is no evidence that the education of adults with neck pain is effective. There is a need for more research to determine whether the education of adults with neck pain is effective.

The review included 49 studies and pieces of literature that were identified from the broader literature providing evidence on policy implementation and consumer experiences. The thematic synthesis showed that ideally communication may be considered as a long-term multicomponent programme, ensuring that notification and support are coordinated; that communication is tailored and responsive to need; and that activities to support individual risk communication, such as widespread education and monitoring of access to health care for people at risk, are in place. The thematic synthesis also showed that poor communication practices may have negative effects or cause harm, such as discrimination in accessing health care. There is insufficient rigorous evidence to determine the effects of interventions to inform people at risk of CJD or vCJD and to support them subsequently, or to identify the best approach to communication in these situations.

We included one randomised clinical trial (N = 304 randomised; 204 completed; 276 analysed) that evaluated opioids (proremained release oxycodone/naloxone versus placebo). After 12 weeks, RSL symptoms had improved more in the drug group than in the placebo group. More patients in the drug group than in the placebo group were drug responders (using the IRLSSS: RR 1.82; 95% CI 137 to 2.42 and the CGI: RR 2.14; 95% CI 145 to 316). The proportion of remitters was greater in the drug group than in the placebo group. Quality of life scores also improved more in the drug group than in the placebo group. Quality of sleep was improved more in the drug group measured by sleep adequacy, and sleep quantity. There was no difference between groups for daytime somnolence, problems staying awake during the day, or naps during the day. More adverse events were reported in the drug group. The major adverse events were gastrointestinal problems, fatigue, and headache.

The review included 15 trials, involving 1022 adults with dorsally displaced and likely or evidently unstable distal radial fractures. All trials compared external fixation versus plaster cast immobilisation, but there was significant variation in terms of patient characteristics and interventions. The evidence is current to January 2016. External fixation reduced redisplacement, improved anatomical results and most of the over-residence of surgery was minor. Probably, some complications could have been avoided using a different surgical technique for pin insertion. There was insufficient evidence to establish a difference between the two groups in serious complications such as reflex sympathetic dystropy.

Four studies compared crowns with fillings; two of them compared conventional PMCs with open sandwich restorations, and two compared PMCs fitted using the Hall Technique with fillings. In the two studies using crowns fitted using the conventional method, all teeth had undergone pulpotomy prior to the crown was placed. The last study compared two different types of crowns: PMCs versus aesthetic stainless steel crowns with white veneers. We searched for studies that compared PEP to other methods of airway clearance. We found 28 studies (involving 788 children and adults) which compared PEP to other methods of airway clearance. The evidence is current to January 2015. The studies compared PEP to other methods of airway clearance, including a mask, autogenic drainage, oral oscillating PEP devices, high-frequency chest wall oscillation and BiPaP and exercise. The results of the studies were not reported in sufficient detail to provide any high-quality evidence. There was limited evidence reported on adverse events; these were measured in five studies, two of which found no events. In a study where infants performing either PEP or PDPV experienced some gastro-oesophage, there was no evidence of adverse events.

The review of studies found that CZP is effective for induction of clinical remission and clinical response in participants with active CD patients. However, the evidence is not clear whether the risk of serious adverse events differs between CZP and placebo as the 95% CI includes the possibility of a small decrease or doubling of events. Further studies are needed to assess the long-term efficacy and safety of CZP in CD patients.

We searched for studies that compared the effects of treatment with adenosine A1-receptor antagonists, dopamine, nesiritide, or serelaxin on death, hospitalisations, worsening heart failure or kidney function, high blood pressure, low blood pressure or quality of life. We found 31 studies (23,762 participants) with data on CKD patients. The studies were conducted in the general population but provided subgroup data for people with CKD. The evidence is current to January 2015. The results of this review are current to May 2016. In acute heart failure, the effects of adenosine A1-receptor antagonists, dopamine, nesiritide, or serelaxin on death, hospitalisations, worsening heart failure or kidney function, high potassium levels, low blood pressure, or quality of life were uncertain due to sparse data or were not reported. In chronic heart failure, the effects of angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (ACEi or angiotensin receptor blockers), aldosterone antagonists, and vasopressin receptor antagonists on death (any cause) were uncertain. Treatment with ACEi or ARB may reduce the risk of death (any cause) (moderate certainty evidence). Treatment with ACEi or ARB had uncertain effects on hospitalisation for heart failure, as treatment estimates were consistent with the results of the review. The evidence is current to October 2014.

The review included three studies involving a total of 4545 women. The studies were of moderate quality and were conducted in the USA, the USA, the USA, and the USA. No serious complications were reported in the trials and no maternal deaths occurred. The infants were not more likely to have a severe complications (e.g. a pgs agr) or to be admitted to the newborn intensive care unit (e.g. more than 48 hours). The risk of instrumental delivery (including caesarean section, ventouse and forceps extraction) was not different between groups. The number of women who had a high risk of hyperstimulation was not different between groups. There was no difference between the two types of monitoring for any of the maternal or newborn outcomes.

We included two studies involving 54 participants with CVI. The quality of the evidence was very low due to the small number of studies and small number of participants. One study reported no difference between the exercise and control groups whereas the other reported a reduction in symptoms in the exercise group. In one study, increased change in ejection fraction, half venous refilling time and total venous refilling time were observed in the exercise group compared with the control group. One study reported no difference between the exercise and control groups with regard to quality of life or ankle range of motion. However, muscle strength assessed by dynamometry at low speed did not differ between the two groups in this study, variable peak torque at high rate was lower in the control group than in the exercise group. The incidence of venous leg ulcers, incidence of surgical intervention to treat symptoms related to CVI and exercise capacity were not evaluated or reported in either of the included studies.

The review of trials found that hyaluronan/hylan is an effective treatment for OA of the knee with beneficial effects: on pain, function and patient global assessment; and at different post injection periods but especially at the 5 to 13 week post injection period. The quality of the evidence was moderate to high. However, there are few randomised head-to-head comparisons of different viscosupplementations and authors should be cautioned, therefore, in drawing conclusions about the relative quality of different products.

We found 10 randomised clinical trials (RCTs) with a total of 4052 participants. Four RCTs (1881 women) compared misoprostol with placebo given in addition to conventional uterotonics. The results of these trials did not provide any additional benefit for our primary outcomes such as maternal mortality, serious maternal disease, admission to intensive care or hysterectomy. Two RCTs (1787 women) compared 800 mcg sublingual misoprostol versus oxytocin infusion as primary PPH treatment; one trial included women who had received prophylactic uterotonics, and the other did not. The primary outcomes did not differ between the two groups, but women given sublingual misoprostol were more likely to have additional blood loss of at least 1000 mL. Misoprostol was associated with a significant increase in vomiting and shivering. Two trials attempted to test the effectiveness of estrogen and tranexamic acid, respectively, but were too small for any meaningful comparisons of pre-specific outcomes. One study compared lower segment compression but was not well designed to assess effect on primary outcomes. We did not find any trials evaluating surgical techniques or radiological interventions for women with primary PPH unresponsive to uterotonics and/or haemostatics. The evidence is current to January 2016.

The review of trials found that short-term cysteine supplementation of cysteine-free PN in preterm infants improves nitrogen balance. However, there is insufficient evidence to assess the risk of cysteine supplementation, especially regarding metabolic acidosis, which has been reported during the first two weeks of cysteine chloride administration.

We found 15 studies with a total of 1282 participants. The studies included a total of 1282 participants, and the results were based on a total of 1282 participants. The studies included a total of 430 participants who converted to Alzheimer's disease dementia and 130 participants who transferred to other forms of dementia. The results of the studies were based on a total of 51% to 90% of the data. The accuracy of the CSF t-tuu was evaluated in seven studies (291 cases and 418 non-cases). The sensitivity rates ranged from 51% to 90% and the specificity rates ranged from 48% to 88%. At the average specificity of 72%, the estimated sensitivity was 75%, the positive risk ratio was 27%, and the negative risk ratio was 0.32. The accuracy of the CSF p-tuu/ABeta ratio was assessed in five studies (140 cases and 293 non-cases). The sensitivity rates were between 80% and 96% and the specificities were between 33% and 95%. The quality of the studies was low, and the results were not well reported. The quality of the evidence was low for the CSF t-tuu and the CSF p-tuu/ABAeta ratio.

We found four trials that compared the treatment of bacterial keratitis with topical corticosteroids and without topical corticosteroids. The total number of included participants was 611 (612 eyes), ranging from 30 to 500 participants per trial. One of the three small trials was a trial of the largest study: the Steroids for Corneal Ulcers Trial (SCUT). All trials compared the treatment of bacterial keratitis with topical corticosteroids and without topical corticosteroids and had follow-up periods ranging from two months to one year. These trials were conducted in the USA, Canada, India, and South Africa. All trials reported data on visual acuity ranging from three weeks to one year, and no of them found any important difference between the corticosteroid group and the control group. The trial of the SCUT reported that time to re-epithelialization in the steroid group was 53% slower than the placebo group after adjusting for baseline epithelial defect size. However, the SCUT did not find any important difference in time to re-epithelialization. For adverse events, no of the three small trials found any important difference between the two treatment groups. The investigators of the largest trial reported that more patients in the control group developed intraocular pressure (IOP) elevation. One trial reported quality of life and concluded that there was no difference between the two groups (data not available). We did not find any reports regarding economic outcomes. The trials were generally of good methodological design, but all trials had significant losses to follow-up (10% or more) in the final analyses. Further, three of the four trials were underpowered to detect treatment effect differences between groups and inconsistency in outcome measurements precluded meta-analysis for most outcomes relevant to this review. There is insufficient evidence as to the effectiveness and safety of adjunctive topical corticosteroids.

We included four trials with 450 participants. The evidence is current to January 2016. We found no evidence from trials that percutaneous vascular interventions are better than intravenous thrombolytic treatment for ischaemic stroke. The quality of the evidence was low. The evidence is current to February 2015.

We included one trial, involving 120 families and 143 children. The study was conducted in high-income countries. The study did not report on long-term outcomes for children (high-school completion or income). The evidence is current to January 2016. No evidence showed that centre-based day care, than no treatment (care at home), improved or worsened children's cognitive ability or psychosocial development. No other measures of child intellectual or psychosocial development were reported in the included study. Moreover, no evidence showed that centre-based day care, than no treatment (care at home), improved or worsened work of parents, as measured by the number of mothers in full-time or part-time work and maternal hours per week in paid work or household income over 100 a week. This study did not report on long-term outcomes for children (high-school completion or income). The quality of the evidence was very low because of the small number of studies and the small number of participants.

We included three randomised trials that evaluated intramedullary nailing versus plate fixation in 213 participants, with useable data from 173 participants of which 112 were male. The average age of participants in individual studies ranged from 41 to 44 years. There were no trials comparing surgery with non-surgical treatment. The three included trials were at high risk of performance bias, with one trial also being at high risk of selection, detection and attrition bias. Overall, the quality of the available evidence was rated as very low for all outcomes, suggesting that we are very unsure about the estimates for all outcomes. However, the results of three different measures of foot and ankle function showed a small difference in favour of nailing, but the results of individual trials indicated that this was very likely to be a clinically important difference. In addition, the results of one trial (85 participants) showed no clinically important difference in pain between the two groups. There is only a small number of trials that have been conducted to assess the effectiveness of surgery or the best surgical intervention for distal tibial metaphyseal fractures in adults. The evidence is current to January 2015.

We included 11 studies involving a total of 38,742 participants. The evidence is current to January 2016. The evidence is current to February 2015. The results of this review suggest that BPLDs may reduce the risk of recurrent stroke and other vascular events in people with stroke or TIA. The evidence is based on trials that compared ACE inhibitors or diuretic drugs with placebo or no treatment. The evidence is current to October 2017. The evidence is current to September 2018. The evidence is current to May 2014. The results of this review suggest that BPLDs may reduce the risk of recurrent stroke and other vascular events in people with stroke or TIA. The evidence is current to April 2016.
The review found that B-sitosterols improve urinary symptoms and flow measures. However, their long term effectiveness, safety and ability to prevent BPH complications are not known.

We found 26 trials comparing salmeterol to placebo and eight trials comparing with salbutamol. The studies included 62,815 participants with asthma (including 2599 children). In six trials (2,766 patients), no serious adverse events data could be obtained. All-cause mortality was higher with regular salmeterol than placebo but the increase was not significant. Non-fatal serious adverse events were significantly increased when regular salmeterol was compared with placebo. One extra serious adverse event occurred over 28 weeks for every 188 people treated with regular salmeterol (95% CI 95 to 2606). There is insufficient evidence to assess whether the risk in children is higher or lower than in adults. There was no significant increase in death or non-fatal serious adverse events with regular salmeterol compared with regular salbutamol. There was also a clear increase in risk of asthma-related death in patients not taking inhaled corticosteroids in the two large surveillance studies. However, the confidence interval for patients who were taking inhaled corticosteroids is small and we can not conclude that the inhaled corticosteroids reduce the risk of asthma-related death.

The review of trials found no evidence that postnatal thyroid hormone treatment reduces the severity of respiratory distress syndrome, infant death or mortality in preterm infants with respiratory distress syndrome.
