We found four trials involving 317 people. Three trials studied oral magnesium, with doses ranging from 12.5 mmol/day to 20 mmol/day. One trial studied parenteral magnesium (16.24 mEq a day for 24 hours). The trials were of low quality and the results were not well reported. There was no study that measured all the identified primary outcomes and met the outcomes of this review. Only one trial measured clinical symptoms of seizure, delirium tremens or components of the Clinical Institute withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol (CIWA) score. A single outcome (handgrip strength) in three trials (113 people) was amenable to meta-analysis. There was no significant increase in handgrip strength in the magnesium group. No clinically important changes in adverse events were reported. There is insufficient evidence to determine whether magnesium is beneficial or harmful for the treatment or prevention of alcohol withdrawal syndrome.

We included 15 trials involving 3057 participants. The evidence is current to January 2015. The trials were conducted in the USA, the United States, the United States, and the United States. The trials were of high quality. The evidence is current to February 2016. The results of this review suggest that antibiotics can reduce the time it takes to treat rhinosinusitis, but only 5 to 11 more people per 100 will be cured faster if they receive antibiotics than placebo or no treatment. However, 13 more people had side effects with antibiotics compared to placebo or no treatment. Five fewer people per 100 will have clinical failure if they receive antibiotics compared to placebo or no treatment. The quality of the evidence was low.

The review of trials found that D2 lymphadenectomy can improve the survival of patients with resectionable cancer of the stomach, but the increased risk of postoperative mortality reduces its therapeutic benefit.

The review of studies found that oral naltrexone did not perform better than treatment with placebo or no pharmacological agent in the field of opioid dependence. However, the number of people retained in treatment was low (28%). The results of this review suggest that oral naltrexone did not perform better than treatment with placebo or no pharmacological agent with respect to the number of participants re-incarcerated during the study period. However, the number of people retained in treatment in the included studies is however low (28%). The conclusion of this review is that the studies conducted have not allowed an adequate evaluation of oral naltrexone treatment in the field of opioid dependence. Consequently, maintenance therapy with naltrexone can not still be considered a treatment which has been scientifically demonstrated to be superior to other types of treatment.

We included two studies that examined modification to fluids. The first study, a cross-over trial, investigated the immediate effects on aspiration of two viscosities of fluids (nectar thick and honey thick) compared to regular fluids in 351 participants with dementia. Regular fluids with a chin down head posture, as well as normal fluids without any intervention were also compared. The second study, a parallel designed randomised controlled trial, compared the effect of nectar and honey thick fluids with a chin down head posture over a three-month period in a subgroup of 260 participants with dementia. Outcomes were pneumonia and adverse intervention effects. Honey thick fluids, which are more consistent with descriptors for 'spon thick' or 'excessively thick' fluids, showed a more positive effect on immediate elimination of aspiration during videofluoroscopy, but this consistency showed more adverse effects in the second follow-up trial. During the second three-month follow-up trial, there were a greater number of events of pneumonia in participants receiving honey thick fluids than those receiving nectar thick fluids or taking regular fluids with a chin down head posture. There were no deaths classified as 'certainly related' to the type of fluids prescribed. Neither trial assessed quality of life. The overall quality of evidence for outcomes in this review is low. We are uncertain about the immediate and long-term effects of modifying the consistency of fluid for swallowing problems in dementia as too few studies have been completed. There may be differences in outcomes depending on the grade of thickness of fluids and the sequence of interventions trialled in videofluoroscopy for people with dementia. Patient-based clinical trials are needed to assess the effect of modifying the consistency of fluid for swallowing problems in dementia.

The evidence is current to January 2015. We included six randomised controlled trials with a total of 788 women. The largest of these trials included 396 women eligible for this review. There was no evidence of a significant difference between natural cycle and standard IVF in live birth rates (reported in two studies, 425 women), clinical pregnancy (reported in four studies, 351 women), ongoing pregnancy (reported in three studies, 485 women), multiple pregnancy (reported in two studies, 527 women) or cycle cancellations (reported in two studies, 159 women). There was no evidence of a significant difference between natural cycle and standard IVF in rates of OHSS (reported in one study, 60 women), clinical pregnancy (reported in four studies, 351 women), ongoing pregnancy (reported in three studies, 485 women), multiple pregnancy (reported in two studies, 527 women), gestational abnormalities (reported in one study, 18 women) or cycle cancellations (reported in two studies, 159 women). One trial reported that the oocyte retrieval rate was significantly lower in the natural cycle group (MD -4.40, 95% CI -7.87 to -0.93), 60 women. There were insufficient data to draw any conclusions about rates of treatment cancellation. Results on treatment costs were inconsistent and more data are awaited. The evidence was limited by imprecision. Results for pregnancy rate and for cycle cancellation were sensitive to the choice of statistical model: for these outcomes, use of a fixed effect model suggested a benefit for the standard IVF group. Moreover the largest trial has not yet completed follow up, but data have been reported for over 95% of women.

We included 72 randomised controlled trials that included 2470 participants. The evidence is current to January 2016. The studies were small and the interventions varied in terms of both the goals of treatment and the virtual reality devices used. The risk of bias present in many studies was unclear due to poor reporting. This is a review of studies that compared virtual reality with conventional therapy approaches in people with stroke. We found that the use of virtual reality and interactive video gaming was not more beneficial than conventional therapy approaches in improving upper limb function. Virtual reality may be beneficial in improving upper limb function and activities of daily living function when used as an adjunct to usual care (to increase overall therapy time). There was insufficient evidence to reach conclusions about the effect of virtual reality and interactive video gaming on gait speed, balance, participation, or quality of life. This review found that time since onset of stroke, severity of impairment, and the type of device (commercial or customised) were not strong influencers of outcome.
